| 472.S4 | dispatcher/mod.rs (4603) + runtime/mod.rs (4721) split | God-files split | — | ⊘ skip — navigation via grep/IDE jump works; LSP does not suffer. Open if hot churn |
| 472.S5 | proto/anycast.rs:212 `count as u8` | Truncating cast | — | ⊘ skip — theoretical; MAX_ANYCAST_CANDIDATES=16 << 255 |
| 472.S6 | session_cipher.rs forward secrecy on the DH secret | Full FS requires a protocol rework | — | ⊘ skip (defaults reverted) — Variant A reverted: the defaults `REKEY_BYTES_THRESHOLD = 128 GiB` / `REKEY_TIME_THRESHOLD_SECS = 32 days` were kept so that long-lived idle sessions do not trigger a rekey every hour. The config knobs `[session] rekey_bytes_threshold` / `rekey_time_threshold_secs` existed before this too — highly sensitive deployments can lower them to 100 MiB / 1 hour (the `MLKEM_REKEY_*` values). A full Signal-style ratchet is a separate epic (~500 LoC), not a priority. |
| 472.S7 | Eclipse 51%-honest-handshake | The attacker controls > half of the peers we handshook with | — | 📦 backlog — design problem (diversity score, BFT) |
| 472.S8 | identity master key rotation | Grace period "both valid" | — | 📦 backlog — protocol design |
| 472.S9 | mailbox content_id collision dedup | A foreign content_id → de-dups a legitimate one | — | 📦 backlog — needs a sender-sig in content_id, a wire change |
| 472.S10 | mailbox backlog after rotation | the old identity_id is not cleared | — | 📦 backlog — design a rotation event handler |
| 472.S11 | RwArcMap<K,V> generic wrapper | 8+ copies of `Arc<Mutex<HashMap>>` | — | ⏸ deferred — moving on to 472.6/472.7 which use the same pattern; a general refactor afterwards |
| 472.S12 | per-frame `format!` in session/runner.rs hot path | per-frame allocation cost | — | ⏸ deferred — measure a flame graph under real load |
| 472.S13 | `let _ = write_all(...)` unhandled in several places | silent error swallow | — | ⏸ deferred — surgically when a bug is observed |
| 472.S14 | AtomicU64::load(Relaxed) for invariants | possibly too weak an ordering | — | ⏸ deferred — needs a formal analysis of the specific places |
| 472.S15 | Holding a sync lock across .await | potential deadlock | — | ⏸ deferred — surgically via a grep pass |
| 472.S16 | `as` casts on peer-controlled values (~10 places) | silent truncate | — | ⏸ deferred — most are UB-safe (`u32 as usize`); the dangerous few |
| 472.S17 | Unix socket TOCTOU (admin path) | pre-create attack | — | ✅ done — `bind_unix` now does a 4-step check: (1) refuse symlink at bind path, (2) parent dir uid+mode (sticky-bit `0o1777` OK — sticky protects against non-owner-delete; otherwise requires owned + non-world-writable), (3) refuse if path is a non-socket file (operator data), (4) cleanly unlink the stale socket then bind. 6 unit tests. |
| 472.S18 | Admin token zeroize | leak on panic-dump | — | ✅ done — `LocalToken` (the foundation for AdminToken + IpcToken) got `Zeroize + ZeroizeOnDrop`. `write_token_file` zeroizes the hex-String temp before drop; `read_token_file` zeroizes the read Vec. 1 unit test on explicit zeroize() clearing the inner array. |
| 472.S19 | Revocation gossip stale-seq on restart | persist max_seen_seq across restart | — | ✅ done — persistence was already wired (`encode_file` writes max_seen_seq, `decode_file` loads it, `RevocationCache::open` is called from runtime/mod.rs:798). It was a false positive in my backlog. Added an explicit regression test `stale_seq_replay_rejected_after_restart` (open → record → drop → reopen → attempt a replay with a lower seq → StaleSeq, attacker hash NOT injected). |
| 472.S20 | DHT probe-by-id reveals the target node's transport | RouteRequest{target_id=victim} gets `RouteResponse{transports}` for free — an attacker de-anonymizes any known node_id at no cost | HIGH | gate disclosure | ✅ done — two-level protection. **Level 1 (PoW-gate)**: `RouteResponse` with `transports` is deferred behind a `PowChallenge`/`PowResponse` exchange — while `pow_difficulty > 0`, probe-by-id is no longer free (cost ≈ 2^difficulty BLAKE3 hashes). `PowPendingTable` was extended with `request_id` so the deferred RouteResponse echoes the original request_id for correlation. **Level 2 (config knob)**: `[routing] discovery_mode = "public" | "contacts_only" | "introduction_only"` — `ContactsOnly` silently drops probes from unknown requesters (`peer_pubkeys` cache miss), `IntroductionOnly` always returns an empty `transports[]`, leaving only `relay_ids`. 4 new unit tests: `epic472_s20_pow_gates_transport_disclosure`, `epic472_s20_no_pow_path_replies_immediately`, `epic472_s20_contacts_only_drops_unknown_requester`, `epic472_s20_introduction_only_strips_transports`. PowAccept (legacy) is also sent after verify for backward compat. The wire protocol did not change. Level 3 (Tor-style introduction points with rendezvous) is a separate epic ~500 LoC, in backlog. |

## Epic 473 — Removal of the mailbox subsystem

**Motivation:** we are building the network layer (the "bloodstream"). The mailbox mixes three different layers in one component: transport (MailboxPut/Fetch frames), storage (TTL, eviction, ack-then-delete, persisted seq cursors), application semantics (durable async delivery contract). It is a clot in the network iteration: ~4.2k LOC just in `node/mailbox/`, plus references in ~25 files. Audit finding #3 (missing auth on `MailboxFetchReplica`) is a direct consequence of the implicit layering.

The mailbox can be brought back later as a **separate crate `veil-mailbox`** on top of a stabilized network layer. The messenger will be built on top of the network, and async delivery there can be implemented one of several ways: DHT.store with TTL, multinode self-sync, or an external relay.

| ID | What | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 473.1 | Remove `veilcore/src/node/mailbox/` (944+173+366+157+410+866+960 = 3876 LOC, 7 files) | ✅ done |
| 473.2 | Remove `veilcore/src/node/runtime/mailbox_cleanup.rs` (332 LOC) + dispatcher mailbox-helpers (`pending_replica.rs`, `pending_fetch_replica.rs`) | ✅ done |
| 473.3 | Remove from `proto/delivery.rs`: `MailboxPutPayload`, `MailboxFetchPayload`, `MailboxFetchResponse`, `MailboxAckPayload`, `MailboxFetchReplicaPayload`, `MailboxReplicatePayload`. Keep `Forward`, `DeliveryStatus`, `Chunk*`, `Transit`, `RecursiveRelay`, `DeliveryEnvelope` (used by e2e/ipc). | ✅ done |
| 473.4 | Remove from `proto/family.rs::DeliveryMsg`: `MailboxPut`, `MailboxFetch`, `MailboxAck`, `MailboxReplicate`, `MailboxFetchReplica`. From `DiscoveryMsg`: `GetMailboxSet`. Leave the numeric slots empty (no renumber, to make clear it is a former zone). | ✅ done |
| 473.5 | Remove from `proto/discovery.rs`: `GetMailboxSetPayload`, `MailboxSetResponse` + tests. | ✅ done |
| 473.6 | Remove from `cfg/model.rs`: `MailboxConfig`, `MailboxBackendKind`, the `mailbox` field in `Config`. From `cfg/mod.rs` re-exports. | ✅ done |
| 473.7 | Remove from the dispatcher (`delivery.rs`, `discovery.rs`, `mod.rs`): `handle_mailbox_*` methods, the `pub mailbox: Arc<MailboxService>` field, `mailbox_dht_replication`, `mailbox_replica_count`, the GetMailboxSet handler. | ✅ done |
| 473.8 | Remove from `runtime/mod.rs`: the `MailboxService` init, the `mailbox_cleanup` loop. From `runtime/debug.rs` the debug dumps. From `runtime/tests.rs` the mailbox tests. | ✅ done |
| 473.9 | Remove from `proto/budget.rs`: mailbox-specific constants (`MAX_FETCH_ENTRIES`, `MAX_MAILBOX_*`). From `node/abuse/mod.rs`: mailbox quotas. From `node/mesh/{mod,bridge}.rs`: the mesh-mailbox bridge. From `cmd/debug.rs`: the CLI debug. | ✅ done |
| 473.10 | Remove mailbox-touching tests: `node/identity/integration_tests.rs` (mailbox-related scenarios), `node/dispatcher/tests.rs` (mailbox tests). | ✅ done |
| 473.11 | Remove from `node/discovery/mod.rs::handle_get_mailbox_set` + `node/routing/miss_handler.rs` the mailbox-DHT-fallback. | ✅ done |

## Epic 474 — Audit follow-ups (probe-by-id pattern)

From the review after 472.S20: the same probe-by-id leaks were found in the `Diag` and `Discovery` family.

| ID | What | Severity | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 474.1 | DiagPing/TraceProbe oracle: `Ping{target=X}→Pong{responder=X}` confirms the existence of X for any session-peer; `TraceProbe{target=X, ttl=N}` reveals every hop. | HIGH | ✅ done — added a `discovery_mode` filter in `dispatch_diag`: in `ContactsOnly` a Pong is returned only if `p.sender ∈ peer_pubkeys`; in `IntroductionOnly` neither Pong nor TraceHop is ever sent. TraceProbe transit (forward to the next hop) is preserved — we only gate the final reveal. Per-peer rate-limit is already applied at the dispatch level (`abuse.rate_limiter.allow(peer_id)` for all frames before routing into the family-handler). 3 unit tests: `epic474_1_diag_ping_contacts_only_drops_unknown_sender`, `epic474_1_diag_ping_introduction_only_drops_even_known_sender`, `epic474_1_trace_probe_introduction_only_drops_final_hop`. |
| 474.2 | GetAppEndpoint/GetAttachment probe-by-id: `discovery.rs:139-205` — any session-peer can learn which endpoints/attachments the target hosts. | HIGH | ✅ done — added an `IntroductionOnly` gate in `dispatch_discovery`: `GetAttachment` and `GetAppEndpoint` return `not_found` regardless of whether a record exists locally/in DHT. `Public` and `ContactsOnly` keep the existing behavior — a direct query is already authenticated by the session (peer_id ∈ peer_pubkeys by definition of a live session), so ContactsOnly is redundant here. The PoW-gate is **not applied** — endpoint/attachment metadata is less sensitive than `transports[]` from RouteResponse (no direct IP leak), the overhead of a PoW exchange is not justified. 2 unit tests: `epic474_2_get_attachment_introduction_only_returns_not_found`, `epic474_2_get_attachment_public_still_responds`. |
| 474.3 | MailboxFetchReplica has no auth | HIGH | ⊘ obsoleted by Epic 473 (mailbox removed) |
| 474.4 | FIND_NODE leaks transports of routing-table neighbors (`Contact{node_id, transport}`). | MED | ✅ done — Mode-aware filter (Option B). **Wire change:** `CapabilitiesPayload` +1 byte `discovery_mode: u8` (3 bytes total; backward-compat via trailing-tolerance — pre-474.4 peers send 2 bytes, the decoder defaults the missing one → Public). **Semantics:** unknown values (Variant C) → `IntroductionOnly` (most-restrictive forward-compat default). **Plumbing:** `perform_ovl1_handshake` takes `discovery_mode: DiscoveryMode`, `OvlHandshakeResult.remote_capabilities.parse_discovery_mode()` exports the peer's mode. `Contact { node_id, transport, discovery_mode }` with a `Contact::with_mode(...)` constructor. **Filter:** `handle_find_node` + `handle_find_value` select Public-only from the routing table; cap `min(K_req, K_dht, ceil(N_public/2))` — one request does not give out >~50% of Public neighbors, forcing the attacker into ≥2× more queries for enumeration. Internal routing (`find_closest_nodes` for next-hop selection) is NOT filtered — privacy-opt-in nodes remain reachable as relays. 5 unit tests in `node::dht::kademlia::tests::epic474_4_*` + 2 in `proto::session::tests::capabilities_*` (legacy 2-byte decode + unknown-byte → IntroductionOnly). 4 existing Vivaldi/RTT tests adjusted for the cap. Full sweep 2146/2146 green. |
| 474.5 | RecursiveQuery (Epic 294) — a DHT proxy, inherits the #474.4 leak. | MED | ✅ done — the same filter as in 474.4 via the `KademliaService::find_closest_public_node_ids` helper, called from the `handle_recursive_query::FIND_NODE` answer path. |

## Epic 475 — Decoupled transport resolution (Option A)

**Motivation:** threat model — censorship-resistance within a single country. A censor-scanner currently gets 20 transports in 1 RTT with a single `FIND_NODE` → ~10 RTT to enumerate all Public nodes in the /20 keyspace → a full IP map in minutes. The mode-filter (Epic 474.4) protects only opt-in privacy nodes; Public defaults — the bulk — remain enumerable.

**Design:** decouple the routing graph from the address graph.
- `FindNodeResponse` loses the `transport` field — contains only `node_id`s.
- New RPC `ResolveTransport{node_id} → SignedTransportResponse{transport, ttl, signature_by_target_identity}`.
- The resolver applies a PoW-gate + a `discovery_mode` filter + a per-peer rate-limit.
- The client caches `node_id → transport` locally (TTL ~24h, signed responses protected against cache-poisoning).
- Cumulative crawl-cost grows from O(N/K) RTT to O(N) RTT × PoW-CPU — ~200× slower for a mass scan.

**UX cost:** +150ms on a cold lookup (warm — 0 impact); +500ms on startup once. Below the human-perception threshold for a messenger.

**Wire-protocol bump:** new `DiscoveryMsg::FindNodeV2` slot — both message types in parallel during the migration window.

**Estimated scope:** ~500-700 LoC + cache impl + invalidation + signed-response forge resistance + tests + docs. 1-2 weeks.

**Phasing.** To avoid a megacommit, this is split into phases:

- **Phase 1 (foundation, current commit):** wire types, server handlers, in-memory transport cache, basic tests + docs. **Defense is not active yet** — the V2 message type exists but V1 keeps working as before; the walker still uses V1. This is the infrastructure for the later phases.
- **Phase 2 (475.5):** the outbound DHT-walker switches to V2 by default. **Defense activates** — an attacking scanner starts getting empty transports via V1, V2 requires a per-node Resolve for each transport.
- **Phase 3 (475.4b/475.4c):** a PoW-gate on `ResolveTransport` + signed transport responses (the target-identity-key signs its own transport, forge-resistant against cache-poisoning) — both done.
- **Phase 4 (475.3b/475.6):** persistent on-disk announcements + V1 removal.  Both done — 475.6 ahead-of-schedule (no deployed network → no observation window needed).

| ID | What | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 475.1 | `proto/family.rs` — add `DiscoveryMsg::FindNodeV2`, `FindNodeV2Response`, `ResolveTransport`, `ResolveTransportResponse` (slots 10-13) | ✅ done (Phase 1) |
| 475.2 | `proto/discovery.rs` — `FindNodeV2Payload`, `FindNodeV2Response { node_ids: Vec<[u8;32]> }`, `ResolveTransportPayload`, `ResolveTransportResponse { node_id, transport: Option<String>, observed_at_unix }` | ✅ done (Phase 1) |
| 475.3 | `node/dht/transport_cache.rs` — local LRU cache + TTL eviction. **Phase 1: in-memory only.** Phase 4 will add persistence. | ✅ done (Phase 1) |
| 475.4 | `KademliaService::handle_find_node_v2` (V2 without transport) + `handle_resolve_transport` (Public-only filter). Phase 1: no PoW + no signature. | ✅ done (Phase 1) |
| 475.4b | PoW-gate on `ResolveTransport` (~17ms BLAKE3 work) | ✅ done (Phase 3) — `ResolveTransportPayload` wire-bumped to 52 bytes (32 node_id + 4 time_bucket BE + 16 pow_nonce). PoW input = `BLAKE3("epic475.4b/resolve_pow/v1" \|\| requester_node_id \|\| target \|\| time_bucket_be \|\| nonce)`; `requester_node_id` is taken from the OVL1-session context (peer_id), not sent over the wire — this blocks requester spoofing. Defaults: `RESOLVE_POW_DIFFICULTY=16` (~7ms median on fast x86, ~14ms on low-end ARM), `RESOLVE_POW_BUCKET_SECONDS=60`, `RESOLVE_POW_TIME_WINDOW_BUCKETS=1` (≈120s replay window). PoW failure → silent `not_found` (verification = 1 BLAKE3 hash ≈1µs, dht_quota already bounds CPU; escalating to Violation would create clock-drift false-positive evictions). Pre-Phase-3 32-byte sender → decode-fail → Violation in dispatcher. `NetworkPeerQuerier` mines PoW before each ResolveTransport via `mine_resolve_pow_now(local_node_id, target)`. KademliaService gains a `local_node_id()` accessor. 7 new tests: 4 in `kademlia` (rejects invalid PoW / stale bucket / wrong-requester / wrong-target binding) + 3 in `proto` (deterministic compute / domain-binding / mine→verify roundtrip / mine_now bucket consistency / pre-phase3 decode rejection). |
| 475.4c | Signed `ResolveTransportResponse` (the target's identity key signs `(node_id, transport, expiry)`) — anti-cache-poisoning | ✅ done (Phase 3) — `SignedTransportAnnouncement{node_id, identity_pubkey, signature, expiry_unix, transport}` (≤394 bytes) is signed by the target node's Ed25519 key over `BLAKE3("epic475.4c/transport_announce/v1" \|\| node_id \|\| expiry_be \|\| transport_len_be \|\| transport_utf8)`. The pubkey is NOT included in the signing input — the verifier rederives the binding via `BLAKE3(pubkey) == node_id`, which makes the bundle self-authenticating. Distribution: new `DiscoveryMsg::AnnounceTransport = 14` — fire-and-forget gossip FROM the target TO the peer right after handshake-complete (`runtime::send_local_announcement` is invoked from both in/out paths). KademliaInner gains `transport_announcements: HashMap<node_id, SignedTransportAnnouncement>`; the dispatcher arm verifies `announcement.node_id == session.peer_id` (peers only announce their own id) → `store_transport_announcement(...)` verifies sig+pubkey+expiry → insert. `handle_resolve_transport` now returns the stored announcement instead of a bare String; the walker (`NetworkPeerQuerier`) verifies signature + node_id-binding + expiry + requested-id match before populating `TransportCache`. The maintenance tick adds `prune_orphan_announcements` to bound memory under churn. `ANNOUNCEMENT_VALIDITY_SECS = 30 days` default. The local node signs at startup in `runtime::start` if `local_signing_key.is_some() && advertised_transport.is_some()`. New free function `SessionOutbox::send_oneway` for fire-and-forget frames without oneshot-channel allocation. **Threat reduction:** an attacking resolver can no longer redirect traffic to an attacker IP — it can only deny existence (return `not_found`); to forge a transport, you would need to find an Ed25519 keypair whose pubkey hashes to the target's node_id (a preimage attack on BLAKE3 — infeasible). 8 new tests: sign/verify roundtrip, wire-roundtrip, tampered transport / tampered expiry / pubkey-mismatch / cross-key-forgery / expired / cache-handler, 6 in kademlia (returns-known-public, hides-non-public, public-without-announcement, drops-expired, store-rejects-mismatch, prune-orphans), 1 walker (rejects-forged-announcement). All in-scope sweep 150/150 green. |
| 475.5 | Outbound DHT-walker: use V2 by default, call `ResolveTransport` when needed (with a cache check). **Activates the defense.** | ✅ done (Phase 2) — `NetworkPeerQuerier::find_node` switched to the V2 flow: FindNodeV2 → node_ids → cache lookup → ResolveTransport(node_id) on miss → insert → Contact list. `with_cache(...)` constructor for a shared `Arc<Mutex<TransportCache>>`. Sequential per-node_id resolution (parallel — Phase 3). Backward-compat: peers without V2 → Violation → timeout → empty response → the walker drops the peer (graceful, but V1-only networks do not work, which is OK for a new deployment). 3 new tests: V2 round-trip, warm-cache skip, not_found drop. The bootstrap test was rewritten against a V2 mock. Full sweep 2168/2168. **Defense is now active — the V1 server still answers legacy peers, but scanner clients via V2 punch through only the cache + per-id RPCs (~10× slower enumeration).** |
| 475.3b | TransportCache persistence (on-disk, signed entries) | ✅ done (Phase 4) — persistence is done for the **announcements map** (not for the derived `TransportCache`, which is rebuilt from announcements on first resolve). `SignedTransportAnnouncement` gets `serde::Serialize/Deserialize` derives + base64-encoded `[u8;32]` (via the reusable `kademlia::hex_array`) and `[u8;64]` (new local helper `serde_signature_64`). `KademliaService` gains `snapshot_transport_announcements() -> Vec<...>` and `restore_transport_announcements(snapshot, now) -> (inserted, rejected)` — each entry is re-verified through `verify_transport_announcement` on load (sig + pubkey↔node_id binding + expiry); failures silently dropped. Config knobs in `DhtConfig`: `transport_announcements_persist_path: Option<String>` (None=disabled) + `transport_announcements_persist_interval_secs: u64` (default 120s). Runtime: `restore_transport_announcements_snapshot` is invoked in the startup `config.persist_enabled` gate block; `spawn_transport_announcements_persist_task` via the standard `spawn_persist_loop` pattern (registered as `RuntimeService::PersistTransportAnnouncements` in `task_registry.rs::ALL`); `do_stop_flushes` added a final flush on clean shutdown. JSON format instead of binary — entries are small (~250B JSON), greppable, and tamper-resistance comes from the signatures (verified on load), not from the on-disk format: an attacker with fs-access can downgrade availability (drop entries → the walker re-handshakes), but **cannot** inject forged transports (requires an Ed25519 keypair whose pubkey hashes to the target's node_id). 3 new tests: snapshot-restore roundtrip (3 entries), restore-drops-expired, restore-drops-tampered (signature mismatch). All in-scope sweep 176/176 green. |
| 475.6 | V1 removal — drop FindNode/FindNodeResponse outright (no deployed network → no observation window needed) | ✅ done (Phase 4) — `DiscoveryMsg::FindNode` (slot 0) and `FindNodeResponse` (slot 8) removed from the enum + `TryFrom<u16>` (now → `UnknownMsgType` → `Violation`). `KademliaService::handle_find_node` + the V1 dispatcher arm removed. The `FindNodePayload` type removed; the `FindNodeResponse` struct kept (used as a wire-helper for the `FindValueResponse::Nodes` not-found branch). The Vivaldi/RTT-aware ranking that lived in the V1 server was moved into a new private helper `KademliaService::ranked_public_contacts`, used from `handle_find_node_v2` + `handle_find_value` — feature parity preserved for the V2 path. SessionRunner intercept switched: `FindNodeResponse` → `FindNodeV2Response \| ResolveTransportResponse`. 7 V1 tests converted to V2 (closest/RTT/Vivaldi/half-cap/mode-filter/legacy-default), the V1-only proto roundtrip removed. Build clean; in-scope sweep 138/138. |
| 475.7 | Tests Phase 1: proto roundtrip + handler filter + cache TTL eviction | ✅ done (Phase 1) |
| 475.7b | Tests Phase 2-3: cold/warm latency benchmarks, cache invalidation under load, signed-response forge resistance, PoW gate efficacy | 📦 backlog |
| 475.X-remint | Re-mint local announcement at half-validity to prevent long-running peers from going silent ~30 days after startup | ✅ done — `KademliaService` gains `local_announcement_source: Arc<Mutex<Option<(Arc<SigningKey>, String)>>>` + new `configure_local_announcement_source(sk, transport)` setter (used at runtime startup instead of bare `set_local_announcement`) + `maybe_remint_local_announcement(now) -> Option<NewAnn>` checked from the maintenance tick. Re-mint fires when remaining validity ≤ `ANNOUNCEMENT_VALIDITY_SECS / 2` (~15 days); cheap no-op (~1 hashmap-lookup + 1 cmp) until then; ~50µs Ed25519-sign on the actual re-mint. New maintenance helper `tick_remint_local_announcement` re-gossips to every peer in `session_outbox.peer_ids()` after a successful re-mint via `send_local_announcement`. **Threat reduction:** a patient censor could previously just wait out `ANNOUNCEMENT_VALIDITY_SECS` — after startup a long-lived node became `not_found` for all walkers; now the re-mint is automatic. 4 new tests: no-source-returns-none / fresh-still-skipped / half-expired-triggers / idempotent-after-remint. |
| 475.8 | Docs: protocol-spec.md §5.4 V2 + §5.5 ResolveTransport wire layout. Phase 2 will add a threat-model section to ARCHITECTURE_FULL.md. | ✅ done (Phase 1) |

### Priority / implementation plan

**Sprint 1 (Tier 1 — security): ✅ all closed (no-op)**
Careful manual verification after the agent report showed: all 4 items were false positives:
- 472.1: rekey ALREADY uses fresh DH ephemerals → forward secrecy is correct
- 472.2: PEX replay protected via server-side nonce one-shot consumption; the HMAC comparison goes through crypto-crate const-time verify, not a raw `==`
- 472.3: all decoders with a peer-controlled count have a MAX cap-check
- 472.4: decoders are flat / strictly bounded loops, no self-recursion

Security is better than the audit itself originally claimed. Moving on to Tier 2 where there is real work.

**Sprint 2 (Tier 2 — operability):**
- ✅ 472.7: already done in Epic 454.1b (false positive)
- ✅ 472.8: nonce-watermark auto-rekey (defence-in-depth)
- ✅ 472.5: admin audit-log in `<config-dir>/admin-audit.log` — JSONL append+fsync, 10 mutating commands
- ⊘ 472.6: tokio::spawn naming sweep — rejected (low value, tokio panic-handling is already correct)

**Sprint 3 (Tier 3 — refactor debt):**
- ✅ 472.9: dedup the Vivaldi alias via peer_cache.rs
- ⊘ 472.10: PublishIoError/LookupError — boundary abstractions, keep
- ⊘ 472.11: PowPendingTable is actively used in the routing dispatcher, not legacy

**After Sprints 1-3:** all actionable audit items are closed. Only 472.5 (admin audit-log) remains as a separate task and Tier 4 (skip-list) — design problems for future epics.

---

## Roadmap to the goal

**Goal:** a censorship-resistant decentralized veil network for citizens of authoritarian states. Optional anonymity (Tor-like). Latency-optimized routing. Runs on budget hardware (phone / Raspberry Pi). Hard to block at the DPI level. Hard to enumerate via network scanning.

**Threat model:**
- **State-level censor:** actively blocks connections by IP, ports, DPI-fingerprint, BGP filtering; runs Sybil nodes inside the network for enumeration / eclipse / cache-poisoning; correlates traffic via a passive observer.
- **End user:** possibly behind NAT, low-bandwidth (mobile network), low-CPU (budget phone), unstable connection. Wants low-latency UX in the default mode and strong anonymity optionally.

**What is already done (foundation):**
- Epic 460-475 — V2 FIND_NODE + ResolveTransport + PoW gate + signed announcements + persistence + 50%-cap + mode filter + V1 removal + handshake security + identity layer + transport pluggability foundation.
- Threat coverage already: enumeration is ≥ 2× slower, transport redirection is impossible (requires a BLAKE3 preimage), opt-in privacy peers are invisible, cache-poisoning is protected by signatures.

**What remains to reach production-grade for the target user:**

The 11 epics below are split into 4 clusters (cleanup → connectivity → resistance → deployment). Dependencies: 476→477 → (478, 479 in parallel) → (480, 481 in parallel) → 482 → 483 → 484 → 485. 486 (PQ) — in parallel with any of 480+.

---

## Epic 477 — Identity model rebuild (node_id ↔ device_id)

**Goal:** Bring identity to the user-described model:
- `node_id = BLAKE3(master_pubkey)` — a stable address for the "person".
- `device_id = BLAKE3(device_pubkey)` — a stable address for the "device".
- The master signs `Delegation { device_pubkey, valid_until_unix, master_sig }` → the device gets the right to use this `node_id` for the duration of `valid_until`.
- Standalone mode: if there is no delegation → `device_id == node_id` (a single device = both person and device).

| # | Task | Files | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 477.1 | Drop the old `identity_id` derivation domain-tag — move to `node_id = BLAKE3(master_pubkey)`. Migration: rename the field, update the wire-comment, without changing semantics. | proto/identity_document.rs + cfg/model.rs | ✅ done (caf9388) |
| 477.2 | Standalone mode: detect the absence of a master in the config → device_pubkey is used as master_pubkey → `device_id == node_id`. Handshake without a Delegation in this case. | cfg/sovereign_flow.rs + identity/sovereign.rs + runtime/mod.rs | ✅ done (af207dd) — Option α: runtime always builds an IdentityDocument; standalone uses self-signed degenerate doc (master_pk == device_pk). Wire format unchanged; rest of runtime doesn't branch on standalone-ness. CLI counterpart `identity standalone` lands separately (032dd37). |
| 477.3 | Default `valid_until_unix = now + 7 days` (short, limits leaks). The master periodically re-issues the Delegation; refresh is guaranteed via the mesh on handshake-resume. | cfg/model.rs (default) + identity/publish.rs | ✅ done (per-key valid_until + DELEGATION_VALIDITY_SECS=7d) |
| 477.4 | Re-mint the Delegation at half-validity (mirror of the transport-announcement re-mint from the Epic 475 backlog). Maintenance tick. | runtime/maintenance.rs + identity/sovereign.rs | ✅ done (af207dd) — `tick_reissue_local_delegation` + `SovereignIdentity::reissue_self_delegation`. Standalone-mode only (master_sk in memory); multi-device delegations need master device intervention via `identity delegate-device`, the on-change mtime poll picks up the externally-re-signed doc within 60 s. |
| 477.5 | Multi-device via several Delegations from a single master — keep it, optimize it (≤ N=8 devices without scanning `MAX_IDENTITY_KEYS`). | proto/identity_document.rs | ✅ done (deterministic device_id per IdentityKey, MAX_IDENTITY_KEYS=8 unchanged) |
| 477.6 | CLI: `identity create-master`, `identity delegate-device --validity 7d`, `identity standalone`, `identity revoke-device <id>` (on the master side). | cmd/sovereign_identity.rs | ✅ done (032dd37) — `identity standalone` (auto-bootstrap counterpart) + `identity delegate-device --pubkey-file ... --validity 7d` (master signs new device's delegation). `revoke-device` SKIPPED per Epic 476.S1 — no in-band revocation, mitigation is short validity + auto-reissue. `create-master` is the existing `identity create`. |
| 477.7 | Tests + docs: protocol-spec.md §3 Identity fully rewritten for the new model. | sim/scenarios.rs + docs/identity-model.md | ✅ done — sim scenarios (b5bb92e): standalone, multi-device-shared-master, delegation-re-issue-at-half-validity. Doc rewrite went into `docs/identity-model.md` (the actual sovereign-identity spec; protocol-spec.md §3 is the OVL1 session-handshake IdentityPayload, which is unrelated and didn't carry stale terminology). |

**Status:** all Epic 477 sub-tasks landed.  Deterministic `device_id = BLAKE3(pubkey)`, per-key `valid_until_unix` with 7-day default, dropped `bound_instance_id`, dropped legacy `pubkey_hash` alias.  Verifier ladder gained DeviceIdMismatch + KeyExpired steps.  Standalone-mode runtime auto-bootstrap, auto-reissue tick (standalone-mode), CLI surface refactor (`identity standalone` + `identity delegate-device`), sim scenarios (standalone + multi-device + re-issue), and identity-model.md rewrite all landed in three follow-up commits (af207dd + 032dd37 + b5bb92e) plus a docs commit.

**Acceptance (delta against original 477):** a standalone device (a phone without a second device) works "out of the box" without flags — **landed (af207dd)**; multi-device — the "phone + laptop under one name" scenario converges via handshake — **landed (b5bb92e)**; compromise of a single device-key erases access within ≤ 7 days without master intervention — **landed (wire format + verifier + auto-reissue tick)**; the sim-scenario verifies a device-key change with failover at the peers — **landed (b5bb92e: re-issue scenario closes the auto-reissue loop)**.
