| 462.26 | **QR: identity sharing (public)**: `cmd/identity/qr.rs` — render a QR code to the terminal with public identity info for adding to contacts. Payload: `veil:identity?id=<hex identity_id>&master_pk=<hex>&name=<optional>` (URI scheme, compatible with QR-scanning apps — browser can open via a deep link handler). CLI: `veil-cli identity qr [--name <preferred name>]`. Unicode block rendering via `qr2term = "0.3"` crate (pure-Rust, no C deps). Terminal-fallback ASCII mode for non-UTF8 terminals (`--ascii`). Integrated into the `identity create` flow — is shown at the end with an instruction "scan this to share your identity". **Implementation note**: URI layer ships — `proto/identity_contact.rs` defines `IdentityContact { identity_id, master_algo, master_pubkey, name: Option<String> }` with canonical `to_uri()` / `from_uri()` + `ContactUriError` enum. Format: `veil:identity?id=<64-hex>&master_algo=<ed25519\|falcon512>&master_pk=<hex>[&name=<normalized>]`. Scheme + algo-name case-insensitive on parse, hex case-insensitive, field order arbitrary on decode but canonical on encode. Master-pk length enforced per algo (32 B Ed25519 / 897 B Falcon-512). Name field routed through `NameClaim V2` normalizer so homoglyph / Unicode / case vectors are rejected at the URI boundary. 4 KiB size cap. 23 tests — Ed25519 + Falcon-512 round-trips, case-insensitive parse, canonical field order, duplicate / unknown / malformed / missing field rejection, wrong hex length, oversized, non-normalizable name, live-identity integration (URI produced from a real `create_identity` output preserves the `identity_id == BLAKE3(master_pk)` binding after round-trip). **Renderer + CLI shipped**: `qrcode = "0.14"` (pure-Rust, `default-features = false` — strips the `image` / `svg` transitive deps since terminal output doesn't need them). `veil-cli identity qr [--veil-dir PATH] [--name NAME] [--ascii]` loads the on-disk `SovereignIdentity`, builds an `IdentityContact`, prints the canonical URI on its own line, then renders a QR matrix beneath it — Unicode half-block by default (▀ / ▄ / █ / space, each terminal row covers 2 QR rows so the output stays ≈square in fixed-cell terminals) with inverted convention (QR-dark → light glyph) for dark-bg terminals, `--ascii` fallback emits `##` / `  ` per module. 4-cell quiet zone per QR spec. EC level M balances scanner robustness against the ~100–200 B URI payload. 4 CLI tests: happy-path URI + half-block glyph presence, `--ascii` fallback emits `##` and suppresses half-block chars, `--name` round-trips into the URI, unprovisioned-sovereign → `SovereignLoad` error | **P1** | ~200 | `proto/identity_contact.rs`, `cmd/sovereign_identity.rs` (`qr`, `render_qr_halfblock`, `render_qr_ascii`), `cmd/cli.rs` (`IdentityCommand::Qr` + `IdentityQrArgs`), `Cargo.toml` (`qrcode = "0.14"`) | ✅ done |
| 462.27 | **QR: cold master backup (encrypted)**: `cmd/identity/qr_backup.rs` — **password-protected** QR for offline cold backup in case of loss of the paper and the encrypted file. Payload: base64-url(`MasterSeedFile` encrypted bundle from 462.4b). Flow: `veil-cli identity export-qr-backup --password ****` → encrypts master_seed + displays QR (usually photographed and stored offline). Restoration: `veil-cli identity import-qr-backup --from-image backup.png --password ****` — decrypts and restores the identity. Password **never** encoded in the QR — out-of-band (verbal / note in a safe). Multi-QR split if the bundle > 2KB via `--split N`. **Not for mobile login** — for that there is the 462.30 pairing ceremony. Use case: paper phrase lost, encrypted file corrupted — the photo backup remains. **Implementation note**: library layer ships — `cfg/identity_master_file.rs` refactored to expose byte-level helpers `encode_master_seed_encrypted_with(seed, password, m, t, p) -> Vec<u8>` + `decode_master_seed_encrypted(bytes, password) -> Zeroizing<[u8; 32]>` (the file-based `save`/`load` wrappers now delegate to these); same wire layout as the on-disk format so `master.enc` and the QR payload are interchangeable.  `cfg/identity_master_qr.rs` (new) defines `MASTER_BACKUP_URI_SCHEME = "veil:master-backup"` + `MASTER_BACKUP_URI_V1 = 1` + `MAX_MASTER_BACKUP_URI_BYTES = 4096` (single-QR fits at EC level Q).  `encode_master_backup_uri(seed, password)` + `_with(...)` produces canonical `veil:master-backup?v=1&data=<base64url-no-pad>` (~225 B for default Argon2id params); `decode_master_backup_uri(uri, password)` accepts shuffled field order, case-insensitive scheme, rejects oversize / wrong scheme / missing query / missing/duplicate/unknown fields / unsupported version / invalid base64 / wrong-password-or-tampered with one indistinguishable error kind.  `MasterBackupUriError` covers all the failure modes.  15 tests — round-trip recovers seed, wrong password rejected, wrong scheme, missing query, missing/duplicate/unknown fields, unsupported version, invalid base64, shuffled field order, case-insensitive scheme, oversized URI, tampered data field, byte-helper round-trip (regression guard for the underlying encode/decode pair).  **CLI shipped**: `veil-cli identity export-qr-backup (--password-file FILE | --phrase-file FILE) --qr-password-file FILE [--veil-dir P] [--ascii]` — recovers the master_seed via the same password/phrase fixtures the rotate/revoke/pair-listen flows use, encrypts under a SEPARATE QR password (intentional: the QR backup password should never be co-located with the source decryption material), encodes via `encode_master_backup_uri`, renders the QR matrix (half-block by default or `##` via `--ascii`, same renderer as 462.26), and emits a WARNING line reminding the operator to convey the QR password out-of-band.  `veil-cli identity import-qr-backup <URI> --password-file FILE [--veil-dir P] [--label NAME]` decodes the URI via `decode_master_backup_uri`, runs `restore_identity` into the target veil_dir (which itself writes `identity_document.bin` + `device_identity_sk.bin` + `instance_id`), prints the recovered identity_id.  Defensive UX: `--password-file` and `--phrase-file` are mutually exclusive on `clap`; runtime-side neither-set returns a clear error.  6 CLI tests — export emits canonical URI + QR-block characters + WARNING, full export→import round-trip recovers the identity_id and mints a fresh per-device subkey, export via `--phrase-file` works, missing-seed-source is a clear error, import with wrong password rejected, import with malformed URI rejected. Final wire-format choices documented above; `--split N` for multi-QR payloads above the 4 KiB envelope cap is a P3 follow-up (default-Argon2id payloads are ~225 B → single QR fits at EC level Q) | **P2** | ~300 | `cfg/identity_master_file.rs`, `cfg/identity_master_qr.rs`, `cmd/cli.rs` (`IdentityCommand::{ExportQrBackup, ImportQrBackup}`), `cmd/sovereign_identity.rs` (`export_qr_backup`, `import_qr_backup`, `read_master_seed_from_source`, `read_password_file_trimmed`) | ✅ done |
| 462.28 | **Device linking event (push notification)**: when a new instance is added to the InstanceRegistry, dispatcher sends an event `DeviceLinkedEvent { new_instance_id, label, linked_at, linked_by_instance }` via DELIVERY_FORWARD with `InstanceTag::All` (except the just-added one — it already knows it connected itself). The receiving instances show the user an alert ("A new device 'Mobile-iPhone' is linked to your identity"). Protection: event is signed by the adding instance's identity_sk — cannot be forged. A receiving instance can trigger an anomaly workflow if it did not initiate the linking (possibly compromise). **Implementation note**: proto layer ships (signed `DeviceLinkedEvent` with self-link guard); dispatcher fan-out wiring depends on 462.12/462.20 | **P1** | ~200 | `proto/identity_events.rs` (new) | ✅ done (proto layer) |
| 462.29 | **Identity fingerprint (safety numbers)**: helper `identity_fingerprint(identity_id, their_identity_id) -> String` — deterministic 60-digit numeric representation (Signal-style): `BLAKE3("veil.fingerprint.v1" || min(ids) || max(ids))` → split in 6-digit groups. Pair-symmetric (Alice and Bob see the same digits, can compare over the phone). CLI: `veil-cli identity fingerprint --with @alice`. App-level UX: messenger shows the fingerprint in the contact profile; users compare out-of-band for verification. Changes on compromise-induced rotation → user alert "safety number changed". **Implementation note**: library layer ships with 60-digit output in 12 × 5-digit groups; CLI subcommand lands with 462.5/462.9 | **P1** | ~150 | `crypto/identity_fingerprint.rs` (new) | ✅ done |
| 462.30 | **QR pairing ceremony (mobile login) — per-instance key + OOB confirmation**: the main login mechanism on a new device. **Flow**: (1) **source device** (where the identity is active with master_sk): `veil-cli identity pair-invite --ttl 300s` → source generates ephemeral `pair_secret: [u8; 32]`, publishes `PairingInvite { identity_id, pair_secret_hash, source_instance_id, expires_at }` to DHT signed by identity_sk, displays QR `veil:pair?id=<hex>&secret=<base64>&endpoint=<transport>` + **human-readable 6-digit OOB confirmation code** (random, shown on the source screen only — never in the QR). (2) **target device** (mobile): scans QR → connects direct to the source endpoint → authenticates via pair_secret → **target generates its own new identity keypair `(target_sk, target_pk)` (master_seed NEVER transferred — only the target device knows its sk!)**. (3) **Master certification step**: target sends target_pk to source; source unlocks master_sk (password prompt) → `master_sk.sign(certify_msg(target_pk, valid_from))` → source appends `IdentityKey { pubkey: target_pk, bound_instance_id: target_generates_random, master_sig }` to the IdentityDocument → publishes v++. (4) **OOB verification step**: source displays "Target shows code: XXX-XXX?" with its stored 6-digit code; target displays same code (derived deterministically from the session key); user visually compares → confirms on source. If mismatch → abort, remove key from document. (5) target receives IdentityDocument snapshot + generates InstanceEntry with `bound_identity_key_idx` → publishes updated InstanceRegistry → triggers 462.28 DeviceLinkedEvent. **Security critical**: (a) master_seed NEVER transferred — target the device never sees master material; (b) target's identity_sk never leaves the target device; (c) OOB confirmation code protects against QR phishing (attacker-as-fake-target) — user sees the real code on the source, fake target cannot show a matching code; (d) pair_secret ephemeral (5 min TTL, one-time-use), QR only hash; (e) if the target device is lost/compromised, revoke only its identity_key — the other instances keep working. Integration tests: 2-device pairing, OOB mismatch detection, expired pair_secret. **Implementation note**: library layer ships — (a) `proto/pairing_invite.rs` defines signed `PairingInvite { identity_id, pair_secret_hash, source_instance_id, issued_at, expires_at, signing_identity_key_idx, sig }` (magic `PI` v1, `PAIRING_INVITE_SIG_CONTEXT = "veil.pairing_invite.v1"`, 2 KiB cap, `canonical_signing_bytes` + `signing_message` helpers, `is_valid_at`, `dht_key(identity_id, source_instance_id)` — concurrent invites from different source instances don't collide); (b) `hash_pair_secret(&[u8;32])` domain-separated BLAKE3 (`veil.pair_secret.v1`) so the published hash cannot alias other protocol values; (c) `PairingUri { identity_id, pair_secret, endpoint, expires_at_unix }` with `to_uri()` / `from_uri()` emitting canonical `veil:pair?id=<64-hex>&secret=<b64url-no-pad>&endpoint=<raw>&expires=<u64>` + `PairUriError` (oversize, bad scheme, missing/duplicate/unknown/malformed field, bad hex/b64, wrong length, invalid expires, endpoint empty/oversized/reserved-char rejection — `&=?#` forbidden in endpoint); (d) `crypto/pair_oob.rs::derive_pair_oob_code(session_key)` — domain-separated (`veil.pair.oob.v1`) 6-digit code rendered as `XXX-XXX` (3-3 split, Signal/WhatsApp UX) + `PairOobCode::parse`/`to_string_canonical` helpers; (e) `node/identity/publish.rs::sign_pairing_invite` producer (signs with active identity_sk, rejects inverted validity window + out-of-bounds sig_key_idx). 43 tests — wire round-trip, magic/version/truncation/trailing/oversize guards, empty-sig rejection, inverted-window rejection, canonical-signing-bytes suffix, signing-message context prefix, `is_valid_at` window, `dht_key` stability + per-instance distinctness, `pair_secret_hash` domain separation, URI round-trip (canonical + shuffled field order), scheme/algo case-insensitive, duplicate/unknown/malformed/missing-field rejection, invalid-hex/b64 rejection, wrong-length secret, invalid-expires, reserved-char endpoint, empty/oversized endpoint, oversized URI, producer sign↔verify against active subkey, bad sig_key_idx, inverted window, end-to-end plausibility (source publishes invite + QR → target scans URI → `hash_pair_secret(scanned) == invite.pair_secret_hash`), OOB determinism + both-devices-converge via X25519 DH sanity, OOB domain-separation vs raw BLAKE3, OOB canonical shape, OOB parse rejection cases. 2058 lib tests pass; clippy clean. **Source-side CLI shipped**: `veil-cli identity pair-invite --endpoint <uri> [--ttl-secs N] [--veil-dir PATH] [--ascii]` — draws a fresh 32-B `pair_secret` from `OsRng`, domain-separated-hashes it, calls `SovereignIdentity::sign_pair_invite` (new thin convenience over `sign_pairing_invite` that fills `identity_id` + `active_instance_id` + `sig_key_idx` from the loaded handle), emits canonical `veil:pair?…` URI + QR matrix (half-block by default or `##` via `--ascii`, same renderer as 462.26) + the transport endpoint line the target dials back on. `--ttl-secs` hard-capped to 1 h (`PAIR_INVITE_MAX_TTL_SECS = 3600`) with zero-ttl rejection — keeps `pair_secret` exposure tight. `pair_secret` never persisted (lives only for CLI render); only the signed hash survives on disk. 7 CLI tests: happy-path URI+half-block+endpoint line, `--ascii` fallback suppresses half-block chars, URI round-trip → `PairingUri::from_uri` recovers `pair_secret` that still hashes non-zero, TTL oversized rejection, TTL zero rejection, unprovisioned-sovereign rejection, reserved-char endpoint rejection. **Target-side URI inspector**: `veil-cli identity inspect-uri <uri>` — pure-parsing diagnostic that dispatches on scheme (`veil:identity` → `IdentityContact::from_uri`, `veil:pair` → `PairingUri::from_uri`) and pretty-prints fields (identity_id, master_algo, master_pubkey, name / endpoint, expires_at, pair_secret_hash). Expired pair-invites flagged `EXPIRED`. 7 tests (contact with/without name, pair with future expiry, expired pair, unknown scheme, malformed contact, malformed pair). **Session-frame wire layer shipped**: `proto/pair_session.rs` — 3 fixed-layout frames driving the ceremony after target dial-back: `PairingHello` (target → source, 147 B: pair_secret_hash + target_ek_pk + target_id_pk + target_instance_id + BLAKE3-keyed MAC over prior fields), `PairingCert` (source → target, 39 B header + signed `IdentityDocument` up to 8 KiB: source_ek_pk + doc_len + signed_document), `PairingConfirm` (target → source, 37 B: confirmed-byte + BLAKE3-keyed proof over session_key).  Magic (`PH`/`PC`/`PF`) + version + truncation + trailing-data + oversize-doc guards.  `PairingHello::mac_input` / `PairingConfirm::proof_input` helpers expose the exact preimage bytes so both sides can recompute without duplicating layout.  23 wire tests — round-trip, bad magic, bad version, truncation, trailing-data, oversize doc-len, bad confirmed-byte, MAC/proof-input determinism + domain separation. **Runtime state machines shipped**: `node/identity/pair_runtime.rs` — transport-agnostic `PairingSource` + `PairingTarget` state machines.  Source holds `IdentityDocument` + `identity_sk` + `master_sk` + `pair_secret` + `pow_difficulty` + `now_unix`; on Hello it verifies `pair_secret_hash` matches the published invite, verifies the BLAKE3-keyed MAC (with `pair_secret` as the key, so only holders of the raw QR secret can forge), draws its own X25519 ephemeral, derives `session_key = BLAKE3_keyed(pair_secret, PAIR_SESSION_CONTEXT || shared)`, calls `build_certify(identity_id, ALGO_ED25519, target_pk, target_instance_id, now)` + master-signs to append the target's `IdentityKey`, bumps `document_version` + re-mines PoW + re-signs `document_sig` with the active `identity_sk`, returns the Cert bytes + `derive_pair_oob_code(session_key)`.  Target holds the scanned `PairingUri` + its own fresh Ed25519 SK (seed wrapped in `Zeroizing`) + fresh `target_instance_id` + X25519 ephemeral; `build_hello()` emits the Hello; `handle_cert()` decodes the Cert, asserts `doc.identity_id == uri.identity_id` (blocks MITM swapping entire documents), locates the target's subkey in `doc.identity_keys`, derives the same `session_key` + OOB code; `build_confirm(user_approved)` emits the Confirm with a keyed proof binding the confirmation bit to the session key.  Source's `handle_confirm` constant-time-verifies the proof; `UserAborted` surfaces cleanly so the caller can roll back the pending append.  Ceremony is fully transcript-bound: forged Hello MAC / wrong pair_secret / flipped bytes / MITM'd ephemerals / substituted doc.identity_id / forged proof / wrong-state invocations all reject.  8 ceremony tests — end-to-end happy path (two sides converge on same OOB, doc ends up with +1 master-certified IdentityKey, document_sig re-verifies), user-abort, MITM ephemeral-pk swap (caught by MAC), wrong pair_secret, forged confirm proof, cert with mismatched identity_id (caught by target), wrong-state Hello on source, wrong-state double-Hello on target.  2210 lib tests + clippy clean.  **Async transport layer shipped**: `node/identity/pair_transport.rs` — transport-agnostic `AsyncRead + AsyncWrite` drivers that plug the ceremony state machines onto any stream.  Length-prefixed wire format: `u32-be length || body`, body capped at `MAX_PAIR_FRAME_BODY = MAX_PAIR_CERT_DOC_SIZE + 512` (8.5 KiB) — the reader rejects oversized `length` *before* allocating to defeat resource-exhaustion on the dial-back port.  `read_frame` / `write_frame` are the raw codec helpers.  `run_pair_source(&mut PairingSource, &mut stream, oob_confirm: FnOnce(&str) -> bool) -> Result<SourceTransportOutcome>` reads Hello → calls `handle_hello` → writes Cert → invokes `oob_confirm(oob_code)` to prompt the source-side operator → reads Confirm → cross-checks target's `confirmed` bit against the local decision.  `run_pair_target(&mut PairingTarget, ...)` mirrors it: build+write Hello → read Cert → `handle_cert` → `oob_confirm` → write Confirm carrying the user's bit.  Both drivers always ship a Confirm even on abort so the peer's state machine observes a well-formed frame (no TCP read timeouts on rejected pairings).  Error surface: `PairTransportError::{Io, FrameOversized, Ceremony, TargetAborted, SourceAborted}` — source-side abort takes precedence over target-side abort on the source (local decision fires first).  7 tests via `tokio::io::duplex`: happy-path both-approve (OOB matches across sides, target SK → pubkey matches appended `IdentityKey`, +1 key in finalized doc), target-abort surfaces `TargetAborted` on both sides, source-abort surfaces `SourceAborted` on source and `Ok` on target (target is crypto-complete, source is the one refusing to persist), both-abort surfaces per-side decisions, frame round-trip, oversized-declared-length rejection, EOF-on-truncated-header bubbles as `Io`.  2217 lib tests + clippy clean.  **Paired-device persistence + TCP CLI shipped**: `cfg/sovereign_flow.rs` — new `DEVICE_SIG_KEY_IDX_FILE = "device_sig_key_idx.bin"` (2 B BE u16) carries a per-device `sig_key_idx` override so target devices whose `identity_sk` signs as subkey `≠ document.sig_key_idx` can still be loaded via `SovereignIdentity::load_from_dir` (which now consults the override when present, falling back to `from_parts_active` on source-side single-device layouts).  `save_paired_target_state(veil_dir, doc, sk_seed, sig_key_idx, instance_id, label)` atomically writes the 4-file paired target layout (`identity_document.bin` + `device_identity_sk.bin` + `device_sig_key_idx.bin` + `instance_id`) with `SigKeyIdxOutOfBounds` + `Io` error surface. `node/identity/pair_transport.rs` — `run_pair_source_tcp(host_port, source, oob_cb)` / `run_pair_target_tcp(host_port, target, oob_cb)` wrappers that bind/connect + dispatch to the async drivers; used by both the CLI and a `tcp_wrappers_end_to_end_happy_path` integration test (real loopback, both sides converge on OOB). `cmd/sovereign_identity.rs` — `veil-cli identity pair-listen --endpoint tcp://HOST:PORT (--password-file FILE | --phrase-file FILE) [--ttl-secs N] [--veil-dir P] [--ascii] [--yes-i-compared-codes]` binds the listener, prints the canonical `veil:pair?…` URI + QR + endpoint, signs the invite with the active identity_sk, unlocks master_sk via either encrypted `master.enc` (with `--password-file`) or BIP-39 phrase (`--phrase-file`) (same pattern as `identity rotate/revoke`), constructs a `PairingSource` with the loaded keys + #[cfg(test)] pow difficulty, accepts one dial-back, runs `run_pair_source_tcp`, stdin-prompts the operator for OOB compare (unless `--yes-i-compared-codes`), and on success atomic-writes the updated `IdentityDocument` back to `<veil_dir>/identity_document.bin`. `veil-cli identity pair-accept <URI> [--veil-dir P] [--label NAME] [--yes-i-compared-codes]` parses the URI, rejects expired invites (`expires_at_unix ≤ now`), rejects non-`tcp://` endpoints, dials the source, runs `run_pair_target_tcp`, stdin-prompts for OOB compare, and on success writes the paired target state via `save_paired_target_state`. 8 CLI tests cover the error surface (expired invite / non-tcp endpoint / missing-master-unlock) + persistence round-trip (`save_paired_target_state` → `load_from_dir` honours the override → returns target-side `SovereignIdentity`). 2229 lib tests + clippy clean. **Remaining** (out-of-scope for 462.30's P0 deliverable, sit with follow-up epics): running runtime's DHT republish of the updated doc + registry on source side — integrate with the existing 60-second polling loop in `runtime.rs` that already watches `identity_document.bin` mtime; `DeviceLinkedEvent` emit via watcher.rs (462.28 dep); real-TCP `sim/scenarios` integration test exercising the full two-process path (sits with 462.23) | **P0** | ~700 | `proto/pairing_invite.rs`, `proto/pair_session.rs`, `crypto/pair_oob.rs`, `node/identity/publish.rs`, `node/identity/sovereign.rs` (`sign_pair_invite` + override-aware `load_from_dir`), `node/identity/pair_runtime.rs`, `node/identity/pair_transport.rs` (`run_pair_{source,target}_tcp`), `cfg/sovereign_flow.rs` (`save_device_sig_key_idx`, `load_device_sig_key_idx`, `save_paired_target_state`), `cmd/cli.rs` (`IdentityCommand::{PairInvite, InspectUri, PairListen, PairAccept}`), `cmd/sovereign_identity.rs` (`pair_invite`, `inspect_uri`, `pair_listen`, `pair_accept`) | ✅ done (DHT republish + DeviceLinkedEvent live with 462.28 / runtime loop) |

### Size and scope

**Total: ~12 900 LOC + docs** into 50 subtasks (after the second revalidation 2026-04-20):
- Core identity + lifecycle: 462.0-462.9
- Multi-device + instances: 462.10-462.12, 462.30
- Names + resolver: 462.13-462.14
- Wire refactor: 462.15-462.20
- Tier A/B + Tests: 462.21-462.23
- Docs + cleanup: 462.24-462.25
- QR: 462.26-462.27, 462.30 (pairing)
- Messenger gap: 462.28-462.35
- Revocation persistence: 462.36-462.38
- **Security hardening (revision 2)**: 462.39 (per-instance keys), 462.40 (PoW timestamp-bound), 462.42/462.43 (merged), 462.44 (DHT per-id quota), 462.45 (one-time pre-keys FS), 462.46 (multi-replica resolution), 462.47 (extra entropy), 462.48 (anomaly watcher)

**Estimated timeline**:
- Full-focus single developer: 6-7 months
- Parallel team: 4-5 months

### Critical path (revision 2, final)

```
462.0 (design)
  │
  ├─► 462.39 (per-instance identity_sk — architectural revision)  ◄── gates many
  │        │
  │        ▼
  ├─► 462.1 (proto: bound_instance_id + freshness + timestamp-bound PoW)
  │        │
  │        ├─► 462.36 (RevocationCache persistent) ─► 462.3 (verifier)
  │        │                                                 │
  │        │                                                 ├─► 462.16 (handshake w/ IdentityProof)
  │        │                                                 │        │
  │        │                                                 │        └─► 462.17 (session dedup)
  │        │                                                 │
  │        │                                                 └─► 462.37 (revocation gossip)
  │        │
  │        └─► 462.10 (InstanceRegistry, per-instance key-bound) ─► 462.12 (publish/fetch)
  │                                                                          │
  │                                                                          ├─► 462.28 (DeviceLinkedEvent)
  │                                                                          └─► 462.30 (QR pairing + OOB code)
  │

  ├─► 462.4 + 462.4b + 462.47 (master storage + entropy) ─► 462.5 (create) ─► 462.6/7 ─► 462.22 (propagate)

  │                                                                                            └─► 462.38 (auto-freshness)

                                ├──► 462.18 (reputation)
                                ├──► 462.19 (mailbox)
                                ├──► 462.13 (name V2, ASCII whitelist) ─► 462.14 (resolver) ─► 462.46 (quorum)

```

**Critical path stars (block downstream work)**:
- **462.39** — per-instance key model; prereq for 462.10/30/32
- **462.1** — proto fields; prereq for 462.3/10/13
- **462.36** — revocation persistence; prereq for 462.3
- **462.5** — `identity create` flow; prereq for all identity operations
- **462.15** — wire refactor; prereq for all node_id→identity_id migration

**Starting point**: 462.0 design doc → 462.39 architectural decision → 462.1 proto — these three form the foundation. Without them parallel work cannot begin.

**Starting point** — epics 462.0 → 462.1 → 462.2 → 462.3 (design + foundation). Until these are ready — the rest cannot be built. **For messenger readiness** 462.30 (pairing) is additionally critical, 462.32 (E2E cert chain), 462.31 (app-state) — without them it is hard for app developers to use veil as a messenger foundation.

### Decisions (locked 2026-04-20)

| Question | Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Signature default | **Ed25519** | Existing veil, proven, smallest pk/sig. Falcon-512 opt-in via the `master_algo` field. |
| master_seed size | **32 bytes (256 bits)** | BIP39 24-word standard, matches the Ed25519 security level |
| BIP39 wordlist | **Standard BIP39 English** | Interop with Bitcoin wallets, proven, user familiarity |
| Master storage | **Paper (BIP39) by default**, optional encrypted file `--save-encrypted` | Paper safer from malware; encrypted file — fallback UX convenience |
| Encrypted file KDF | **Argon2id** | Modern memory-hard, resistant to GPU brute-force |
| Max identity_keys in the doc | **8** | 1 active + 7 grace period revoked, ≤ 1 KB |
| Max revoked_keys in the doc | **32**, GC entries > 1 year | Balance between audit trail and DHT size |
| IdentityDocument TTL / republish | **6 hours** | Balance freshness vs DHT load |
| instance_id size | **16 bytes** | 128-bit collision space |
| `identity_id` size | **32 bytes** | BLAKE3-256 output, standard |
| PoW: registration | **16** (~1 sec CPU) | Anti-spam new identities without burden'and for legitimate users |
| PoW: document update | **12** (~100 ms) | Anti-spam mutations from a compromised subkey |
| PoW: name claim | **12-30** rarity-dependent | See `required_difficulty(name)` — short names expensive |
| Scheduled rotation recommendation | **1 year** | Documented in the user guide |
| Name max length | **64 UTF-8 bytes** | Enough for `user#1234` style + long anonymous names |
| Max instances per identity | **16** | ≤2.5 KB InstanceRegistry, covers typical user (3-5 devices) with margin |
| Tier B (encrypted transports) | **Opt-in**, disabled by default | Privacy-safe default; user enables if direct multi-device sync is needed |
| Migration strategy | **Hard break** (network not deployed) | Cleaner, less legacy code |

### Messenger gap analysis — what needs to be added to veil (revalidation 2026-04-20)

During the revalidation for the use case "build a messenger on veil'" the following were identified **missing pieces**, covered by epic 462 (additional tasks 462.28-462.35):

| What the messenger needs' | Who provides it | Veil task |
|---|---|---|
| Identity (who am I) | veil | 462.1-462.9 ✅ |
| Address resolution (@alice → delivery target) | veil | 462.13-462.14 ✅ |
| Multi-device instances | veil | 462.10-462.12 ✅ |
| Sync messages between user's devices | veil (mailbox shared) | 462.19-462.20 ✅ |
| Async E2E encryption to an offline recipient | veil (ML-KEM Epic 190) | **462.32** binding to identity cert chain |
| New device login ("scan QR on phone") | veil | **462.30** pairing ceremony |
| Device linking alert ("a new device connected") | veil | **462.28** DeviceLinkedEvent |
| Verify "is this really Alice?" (safety numbers) | veil helper | **462.29** identity fingerprint |
| Contact list / friends list (synced multi-device) | app-layer CRDT on top of veil | veil provides **462.31** storage primitive |
| Read receipts / typing / online status | app-layer CRDT, direct sessions | veil provides transport (Tier A/B ready) |
| Group chat crypto (MLS) | app-layer, independent | veil only transports frames |
| Push notifications to mobile | OS-level (APN/FCM) + veil wake-up trigger | **462.35** wake-up hint for dozing instances |
| Message backup/restore | app-layer, encrypted blob | **462.34** primitive for a signed encrypted DHT blob |
| Block list / mute list | app-layer | no veil primitive needed |
| Profile (display name, avatar) | app-layer storage | 462.31 reusable |

### Additional tasks for messenger readiness

| # | Task | Priority | LOC est. | Files | Status |
|---|--------|-----------|----------|-------|--------|
| 462.31 | **App-state primitive: signed encrypted DHT blob** — generic storage for app-layer state (contacts, prefs, profile, etc.) with auto-sync via DHT. API: `store_app_state(app_id, key, value)` / `load_app_state(app_id, key)`; encryption = ChaCha20-Poly1305 with the key `HKDF(identity_sk, "veil.app_state.v1" || app_id || key)` (all instances of one identity can read). DHT key: `BLAKE3("veil.app_state.v1" || identity_id || app_id || key)`. Cap ≤ 4 KB per blob (MAX_DHT_VALUE_BYTES). Signed by identity_sk. Version-monotonic updates. **Use cases**: contacts list, profile, block list, preferences — everything encrypted, synced across instances automatically. **Implementation note**: proto + crypto layer ships — `AppState` wire format (`AS v1`, ≤ 4 KB cap, length-prefix-collision-safe), AAD-bound `(identity_id, version, app_id, key)`, ChaCha20-Poly1305 with caller-supplied 32-byte `app_state_secret` (derivation policy left to caller; primary device derives from `master_seed`, distributes to instances during 462.30 pairing). Length-prefix DHT keying. Caller still publishes/fetches via 462.15-wired DHT runtime | **P1** | ~350 | `proto/app_state.rs` (new) | ✅ done (proto + crypto layer) |
| 462.32 | **E2E key binding to the identity cert chain (per-instance ML-KEM keys)**: (a) each **instance** has its own ML-KEM keypair (not shared — aligns with the per-instance identity_sk model 462.39); (b) ML-KEM pk certified by master_sk either **by instance's own identity_sk** via the extended MlKemKeyCert. Sender to `@alice` with `InstanceTag::All` **fan-outs E2E ciphertext**: encrypts separately to each instance's ML-KEM pk (Signal-style). With `InstanceTag::Specific(instance_id)` — one ciphertext for the target instance. With `InstanceTag::Any` — veil picks one instance, encrypts to its ML-KEM. Chain: master_pk → identity_key cert → ML-KEM cert → pubkey → ciphertext valid. Tests: tampered mlkem rejected, revoked mlkem rejected, fan-out encryption correctness, each instance decrypts only its own copy. **Implementation note**: proto + crypto layers ship — `MlKemKeyCert` wire format with `cert_version`, `verify_mlkem_cert` (7-step chain check: identity binding → subkey idx → in-doc revocation → instance-binding → algo → validity window → sig), `fanout_encrypt`/`fanout_decrypt_one` producing `FanoutEnvelope` per instance with AAD bound to `(sender_id, recipient_id, instance_id, cert_version)`. Dispatcher fan-out wiring + handshake exchange land with 462.16 | **P0** | ~500 | `proto/mlkem_cert.rs` (new), `node/identity/mlkem_fanout.rs` (new) | ✅ done (proto + crypto layer) |
| 462.33 | **Messenger dev guide**: `docs/messenger-dev.md` — how-to for app developers. Covers: (a) identity discovery (@name resolve), (b) sending a message (DELIVERY_FORWARD with E2E encryption), (c) receiving (subscribe to the AppMessage event stream via IPC), (d) multi-device sync (shared mailbox + CRDT events), (e) group chat overview (MLS recommended, not veil's responsibility), (f) safety number UX, (g) pairing new device, (h) Python / Rust example snippets with `ovl_proto.py` helper. NO new code, everything via existing APIs + 462.28-462.32 | **P1** | — (docs) | `docs/messenger-dev.md` (new) | ✅ done |
| 462.34 | **Message backup primitive (encrypted chat export)**: app-initiated encrypted snapshot for "reinstalled the app, I want to restore my history". Messenger app shapes snapshot (list of messages), veil provides a primitive: (a) split into chunks ≤ 4 KB, (b) encrypt each with `HKDF(identity_sk, "veil.chat_backup.v1" || snapshot_id || chunk_idx)`, (c) store in DHT under `BLAKE3("veil.chat_backup.v1" || identity_id || snapshot_id || chunk_idx)`, (d) manifest record with the list of chunks, signed by identity_sk. Restoration: same-identity instance fetches manifest → downloads chunks → decrypts → hands to app. TTL 30 days default, operator configurable. **Messenger-agnostic** — any app can use it to back up its state. **Implementation note**: proto + crypto layer ships — `ChatBackupChunk` (3.5 KiB plaintext cap), `ChatBackupManifest` (signed index with per-chunk cipher lengths + size-sum self-check), `split_and_encrypt`/`decrypt_chunk` helpers with AAD-bound `(identity_id, snapshot_id, chunk_index, total_chunks, chunk_bytes)` preventing reorder / truncation. DHT publish and TTL tracking land with the runtime wiring | **P2** | ~400 | `proto/chat_backup.rs` (new) | ✅ done (proto + crypto layer) |
| 462.35 | **Mobile wake-up hint**: mobile instance often sleeping (iOS/Android power management). When an incoming message is addressed to an identity with a sleeping mobile instance, veil gives a push-notification hint — short UDP wake packet to the last-known mobile endpoint + optionally via an APN/FCM-proxy (out-of-veil). Hint payload: **only** `{identity_id, instance_id, incoming_sender_hash}` — no content, no sender identity clear-text. Actual message pulled by mobile after wake via the mailbox. Similar to the way, Signal notifications work. NOTE: APN/FCM integration — **app-layer responsibility** (mobile app registers with FCM/APN, veil just provides trigger signal). Veil just knows "kick instance X awake". **Implementation note**: proto layer ships (`WakeHint` exactly 71 bytes — fits any MTU, no payload room by design, BLAKE3-truncated 16-byte sender_hash with domain separator, dedup_key helper). Dispatcher wake-on-deliver wiring depends on 462.12/462.20 | **P2** | ~300 | `proto/wake_hint.rs` (new) | ✅ done (proto layer) |
| 462.36 | **Revocation persistence: peer-side cache + freshness binding**: decides "stale revocation" attack — if identity_sk is leaked, owner revoke' via master, but if the owner is then offline > DHT TTL, attacker can re-publish the pre-revocation version and new peers will accept it. Fix in **3 layers**: **(a)** freshness fields in IdentityDocument — `issued_at/valid_until` + `master_freshness_sig` (master_sk signs a freshness cert for 30 days max), consumer rejects `now > valid_until` → attacker's stale doc expires without requiring the owner's online presence; **(b)** `RevocationCache` in `~/.config/veil/revocations.bin` — persistent local store `(identity_id → max_seen_revocation_seq, HashSet<revoked_pk_hash>)`, survives process restart / DHT eviction / even node migration; verifier checks cache before accept — if the local cache knows `seq=5`, and the fetched doc says `seq=1` → reject as stale; **(c)** `revocation_seq: u64` monotonic counter in each IdentityDocument — bumps on each new revocation, consumer can compare versions independently from DHT state. Tests: attacker republishes v1 post-v2, consumer knows v2 locally → reject; fresh peer fetches v1 from mostly-expired DHT → valid_until saves them only if the owner refreshed freshness within 30d. **Implementation note**: shipped with atomic-rename binary file persistence (custom `RC v1` wire format) instead of sled — simpler, no alpha-dep, same API surface | **P0** | ~400 | `node/identity/revocation_cache.rs` (new), `proto/identity_document.rs` (freshness fields) | ✅ done |
| 462.37 | **Revocation gossip via direct sessions**: each handshake exchanges `RevocationSummary { vec![(identity_id, max_seq)] }` compact list. If peer A knows `@alice.seq ≥ 5`, peer B — only 2, A pushes full `RevocationEntry` records for @alice to B. Bloom filter compression if >100 identities (MAX_GOSSIP_SIZE=8KB). B adds in its local cache (462.36). Epidemic flood: revocation seen → propagates via PEX-style fanout. Result: revocation "lives" in the network independent of the owner's availability — attacker' you need to compromise **all** peers that ever saw the revocation, to suppress it for new consumers. Rate-limit: one gossip exchange per session per hour. Enabled in the OVL1 handshake extension or a separate message type. **Implementation note**: proto layer ships (`RevocationSummary` + `RevocationPush` with master_pubkey for self-validation + caps for DoS resistance + dedup defenses). Handshake wiring + Bloom compression + rate-limiting are follow-ups tied to 462.16 | **P1** | ~450 | `proto/revocation_gossip.rs` (new) | ✅ done (proto layer) |
| 462.38 | **Owner freshness refresh automation**: owner must periodically refresh `master_freshness_sig` (once per ~25 days, before the 30d expiry). CLI `identity refresh-freshness` unlocks master, signs new cert, publishes. Automation options: (a) `veil-cli daemon --unlock-master <schedule>` reads password from secure-storage daily, signs if needed; (b) reminder-based — on `identity show` if freshness expires < 5d, warning; (c) mobile app can do this happens automatically when the user unlocks the app (password already in the secure enclave). **Default UX**: warning notifications 10d before expiry; if user ignores, identity becomes "stale" after 30d and all consumers reject. Recovery: unlock master, refresh. Operator-configurable `FRESHNESS_WINDOW_DAYS` (default 30, range 7-90) for paranoia levels. **Implementation note**: library layer ships (`refresh_freshness` signs a new `master_freshness_sig` given `master_sk` bytes; `severity`/`needs_refresh` policy predicate; `FreshnessConfig` with spec defaults 30/5/10 days and `validate()` against `MAX_FRESHNESS_WINDOW_SECS`; shrink-window refusal). CLI + daemon scheduler lands with 462.5/462.6 | **P1** | ~250 | `node/identity/freshness.rs` (new) | ✅ done (library layer) |
| 462.39 | **Per-instance identity_sk model (core architectural revision)**: **the key decision in Epic 462** — each device has its own identity_sk, master-certified separately. NOT a shared-key model. `IdentityDocument.identity_keys[i]` corresponds to a specific instance via `bound_instance_id: [u8; 16]`. Pairing ceremony (462.30) transfers **never the master_seed** — target generates its own sk, source certifies via master_sk. Revocation granular: revoke only the compromised instance's key, the rest keep working. Security gain: compromise one device ≠ full identity takeover; blast radius = one subkey. Side effects: (a) master_sk unlock' on every pairing (acceptable — user-initiated explicit event); (b) E2E sender fan-outs ciphertext per active identity_key (Signal pattern, see 462.32); (c) any active identity_sk can update shared records (NameClaim, InstanceRegistry) — consistency via version monotonicity. **This epic — revision note**, actual implementation touches 462.1 (proto fields: `bound_instance_id` in IdentityKey), 462.3 (verifier extended: "this key is bound to this instance"), 462.5 (create now creates the first instance-bound key, not generic), 462.10 (InstanceRegistry→identity_keys mapping), 462.30 (pairing), 462.32 (E2E fan-out). Tests: compromise-one-device-recover-others scenario, revocation granularity. **Implementation note**: `bound_instance_id` shipped in 462.1; verifier step 7 verifies binding in 462.3; InstanceRegistry points at `bound_identity_key_idx` in 462.10. 462.5/462.30/462.32 will exercise the full lifecycle | **P0** | amendments ~400 cross-cutting | many files (revisions to 462.1/3/5/10/30/32) | ✅ done (architectural decision) |
| 462.40 | **Timestamp-bound PoW (registration freshness)**: prevent rainbow-table pre-computation of registration PoWs. PoW input: `pow_input = canonical_bytes || current_unix_hour.to_be_bytes() || pow_nonce`. Consumer on receiving an IdentityDocument reject' if `|doc.current_unix_hour - now/3600| > 2` (i.e., > 2 hours skew). Attacker cannot pre-compute — must mine online with a recent timestamp. Same pattern for document updates (462.1) and NameClaim (462.13). Small addition (32 bytes overhead), significant anti-Sybil benefit. **Implementation note**: shipped via `freshness_hour: u32` field in 462.1 IdentityDocument and 462.13 NameClaim; verifier step 3 (`FRESHNESS_HOUR_SKEW = 2`) in 462.3 and resolver in 462.14 enforce the ±2-hour window | **P1** | ~80 | `proto/identity_document.rs`, `proto/name_claim_v2.rs`, `node/identity/verify.rs`, `node/identity/resolver.rs` | ✅ done |
| 462.41 | **QR pairing OOB confirmation code** (merged in 462.30) — see the 462.30 description for the full pairing flow with a 6-digit code. Attacker-as-fake-target detection via visual comparison of source/target codes | **P0** | merged in 462.30 | — | ⊘ merged (see 462.30) |
| 462.42 | **Encrypted mailbox_anchor in Tier B** (privacy upgrade, merged in 462.21) — see the 462.21 description. A separate placeholder task here for visibility | **P2** | merged in 462.21 | — | ⊘ merged (see 462.21) |
| 462.43 | **ASCII-only name whitelist + homoglyph prevention** (amendment 462.13) — explicit task for tracking. See the 462.13 description. Tests: reject `alíce`, `alice` (cyrillic a), `alice ` (trailing space), uppercase normalization | **P1** | merged in 462.13 | — | ⊘ merged (see 462.13) |
| 462.44 | **Per-identity DHT write quota**: DHT nodes enforce "at most N update's per identity_id per hour" (default N=10). Protects from a compromised-identity_sk attacker flooding the DHT with version++ updates. Global per-identity counter on a DHT node (sliding window). Exceeding the quota → `DispatchResult::RateLimited`, not Violation (so as not to trigger an auto-ban on legitimate bursts during compromise recovery). Complements the existing per-peer DhtQuota (Epic 454.1) — per-peer gates publisher side, per-identity gates DHT side. **Implementation note**: `IdentityWriteQuota` ships in `node/abuse/identity_quota.rs` — sliding-window VecDeque per identity, injected `Instant` for tests, `RateLimited` decision (NOT Violation) per spec, `gc()` for idle bucket eviction, default 10/hour. Dispatcher wire-in lands with 462.15 | **P1** | ~250 | `node/abuse/identity_quota.rs` (new) | ✅ done (library layer) |
| 462.45 | **One-time pre-keys for forward secrecy async E2E (X3DH-style)**: prevents retroactive message decryption if the master_seed leaks' in the future. Each instance publishes **pool of ephemeral ML-KEM prekeys** in the DHT (~20 one-time + 1 signed a slowly-rotating fallback). Sender picks an unused prekey, encapsulates shared secret, consumes prekey (DHT record marked "used"). Recipient decapsulates, derives session key, deletes prekey's sk. Forward secrecy: compromise sk later ≠ decrypt past messages (prekey's sk destroyed). Fallback key protects when the pool is exhausted (reduced forward secrecy but available). Pool refill when it drops ≤5 unused. Implementation: `proto/prekey_bundle.rs` + DHT record `PrekeyBundle { identity_id, instance_id, prekeys: Vec<MlKemPrekey>, fallback, master_sig }`, `node/crypto/x3dh.rs` — sender picks, recipient consumes. Integration in the existing delivery path: `E2eContext::encrypt_async` tries prekey first → falls back to fallback key → falls back to long-lived (error warning). **Critical a task for messenger FS**. **Implementation note**: proto + crypto layers ship (`PrekeyBundle` with one-time + fallback at `MAX_ONE_TIME_PREKEYS = 8`, ML-KEM-768 reuse from existing `node/e2e.rs`, `PrekeySecretStore` with consume-as-tombstone forward-secrecy invariant). Delivery-path integration depends on 462.15/462.32 | **P1** | ~700 | `proto/prekey_bundle.rs` (new), `crypto/x3dh.rs` (new) | ✅ done (proto + crypto layer) |
| 462.46 | **Multi-replica identity resolution with quorum**: prevent eclipse attack on @name lookup. Resolver queries **k=3 different DHT replicas** (distinct peer-paths), requires **≥2 matching results** to accept. If divergence → try k+1 more replicas; if consistent divergence → return warning to the user ("possible eclipse or network partition"). Also cross-check with the gossip cache (462.37) — direct peers' revocation knowledge. Config: `identity.resolver_quorum = 2` (default), `resolver_max_replicas = 5`. Negative test: simulated eclipse (malicious peers return stale v1 instead of v2) → resolver queries ≥3, receives a divergent, flags warning. **Implementation note**: shipped via `NameLookup::fetch_name_claim_replicated` trait method + `VerifyConfig { resolver_quorum, resolver_max_replicas }` + `ResolveError::QuorumDivergence`. Tests use a `SplitBackend` fake that models per-replica divergence. Gossip-cache cross-check is a follow-up after 462.37 runtime wiring | **P1** | ~200 | `node/identity/resolver.rs` | ✅ done |
| 462.47 | **Optional user-supplied entropy mixing**: for paranoid users who do not trust OsRng. CLI `identity create --extra-entropy-file <path>` XOR' the file content into the master_seed derivation: `master_seed = OsRng.gen() XOR BLAKE3(file_contents)`. User generates entropy via dice rolls (recommended in docs), coin flips, ambient audio recording. If the file is shorter than 32 bytes → error (prevents weak entropy accidentally). No-op if the flag is not set — OsRng only. Compatibility: file can be any size ≥ 32 B; BLAKE3 always distills to 32 bytes. **Implementation note**: `generate_master_seed_with_extra_entropy` ships in `cfg/identity_master.rs`; uses `blake3::Hasher::new_keyed` with a domain-keyed context and XOR-combines with a fresh `OsRng` draw. CLI flag plumbing lands with 462.5 | **P2** | ~80 | `cfg/identity_master.rs` (extension) | ✅ done (library layer) |
| 462.48 | **Self-document anomaly watcher**: owner's node periodically (~hourly) fetches own IdentityDocument from the DHT, compares to the last-known-good snapshot. Alert the user if: (a) `sig_key_idx` changed to an instance the user does not remember, (b) new `identity_keys` entry that the user did not authorize (unexpected pairing), (c) new `revoked_keys` entry without a corresponding user-initiated revoke action, (d) `document_version` gap > expected (possibly attacker pushed rapid updates). Alerts surface via CLI `identity status` (check on boot) + log WARN event + optional push-notification hook for app integration. If an anomaly is detected: prompt user "rotate immediately?" with an automation shortcut. **Implementation note**: stateless `detect_anomalies(current, last_known_good, user_authorized_state)` ships with 6 anomaly kinds (sig_key_idx→unknown, unauthorized key add, unauthorized revocation, version jump, seq regression, master_pubkey change). Scheduler + snapshot persistence + CLI glue land with 462.5/462.9 | **P2** | ~250 | `node/identity/watcher.rs` (new) | ✅ done (library layer) |

### What is NOT included — separate backlog epics (remains app-layer or future)

1. **Shamir Secret Sharing for master_seed** — advanced backup (split N shares, recover M). Nice-to-have, not MVP.
2. **Hardware key integration** (YubiKey / TPM) for master storage. Follow-up.
3. **Multi-sig master** (threshold signature for rotation requires M-of-N pre-committed keys). Advanced resilience.
4. **Social recovery via guardians** — Signal-style social trust network. Explicitly rejected in this version (no external trust anchors).
5. **DNS-verified handles** (Tier 2) — explicitly rejected: no external dependencies.
6. **Anonymity transport layer** for IdentityDocument / InstanceRegistry publications (Tor routing). Opt-in future work.
7. **Group chat crypto (MLS)** — app-layer responsibility, independent RFC 9420 implementation or via `openmls` crate. Veil just transports.
8. **Identity migration proof** (signed "I am migrating from id_A to id_B" as user-initiated proof) — optional future feature for softer compromise recovery.
9. **App-layer read receipts / typing / presence CRDT** — messenger responsibility using 462.31 primitive + direct sessions.
10. **APN/FCM proxy integration** for real iOS/Android push — separate service, not veil core. Veil provides wake-hint trigger (462.35).
11. **Voice / video real-time streams** — Epic 406 exists; messenger can layer on top, but not MVP.

### Implementation order

**Phase 1 — Foundation + Architectural decisions** (~3 weeks)
- 462.0 design doc
- **462.39 per-instance identity_sk model** (architectural revision — prereq for multiple others)
- 462.1 IdentityDocument proto (with bound_instance_id + freshness fields + timestamp-bound PoW)
- 462.2 crypto helpers
- 462.40 timestamp-bound PoW primitive
- 462.3 verifier (cache + freshness + revocation_seq checks)
- 462.36 RevocationCache (persistent) — starts early for integration into the verifier

**Phase 2 — Master key management + QR** (~3 weeks)
- 462.4 BIP39 paper backup (mandatory display, always)
- 462.4b encrypted file storage (optional addition, password + Argon2id)
- 462.26 QR identity sharing (public identity_id + pubkey for contacts)
- 462.27 QR cold master backup (encrypted, for disaster recovery)
- 462.30 **QR pairing ceremony** (the main mobile-login flow)
- 462.5 CLI create (integrates all: always BIP39, opt-in encrypted, QR display on output)
- 462.8 CLI restore (from BIP39 / encrypted file / QR backup / pairing QR)

**Phase 3 — Document lifecycle** (~2 weeks)
- 462.6 CLI rotate
- 462.7 CLI revoke
- 462.9 CLI show
- 462.22 revocation propagation

**Phase 4 — Multi-device + instances** (~2 weeks)
- 462.10 InstanceRegistry proto
- 462.11 instance_id state
- 462.12 publish/fetch

**Phase 5 — Names** (~1 week)
- 462.13 NameClaim V2
- 462.14 resolver

**Phase 6 — Wire refactor** (~3-4 weeks, biggest)
- 462.15 wire migration
- 462.16 handshake IdentityProof
- 462.17 session dedup
- 462.18 reputation
- 462.19 mailbox
- 462.20 dispatch with InstanceTag

**Phase 7 — Messenger-readiness + security hardening + cleanup** (~5-6 weeks)
- 462.28 device linking event
- 462.29 identity fingerprint helper
- 462.31 app-state primitive (encrypted synced DHT blob)
- 462.32 E2E ML-KEM cert binding **with per-instance fan-out**
- 462.37 **revocation gossip** via sessions (resilience; 462.36 already in Phase 1)
- 462.38 owner freshness refresh automation
- 462.44 per-identity DHT write quota
- 462.45 **one-time pre-keys X3DH FS** (critical — retroactive decryption protection)
- 462.46 multi-replica identity resolution with quorum
- 462.47 optional extra entropy
- 462.48 self-document anomaly watcher
- 462.21 Tier B encrypted transports + encrypted mailbox_anchor
- 462.33 messenger dev guide
- 462.34 message backup primitive
- 462.35 mobile wake-up hint
- 462.24 identity docs + **opsec user guide**
- 462.25 old NameRecord removal

**Phase 8 — Validation** (~2 weeks)
- 462.23 integration test suite
- End-to-end scenarios
- Security review

### Success criteria

**Identity lifecycle**:
☐ User can `identity create` in < 5 sec (PoW + DHT publish)
☐ BIP39 phrase **always** is shown on `create` and the user retypes' 3 random words (confirms written down)
☐ User can `identity rotate` without losing name / reputation / sessions
☐ User can `identity revoke` + propagate to 10+ peers in < 10 sec
☐ Recovery from total device loss via BIP39 (mandatory) OR encrypted file (optional) OR QR cold backup

**Security (core)**:
☐ `master_seed` never hits' disk in plaintext (even with an encrypted file — zeroize-on-drop)
☐ `master_seed` **only on the primary device** — other instance's do not have master; pairing transfers certified identity_sk, not master
☐ Encrypted master file readable only with the correct password (Argon2id + AEAD)
☐ Password for QR transfer / encrypted file is NOT encoded into the QR / file itself (always out-of-band)
☐ All domain-separated sig contexts are verified (not cross-usable)
☐ Pre-image resistance BLAKE3 binding formally documented in the threat model
☐ Compromised identity_sk cannot rotate (no master_sk), only a DoS window

**Security (hardening — revision 2 additions)**:
☐ Per-instance identity_sk: compromise one device → revoke only its subkey, the rest keep working (blast radius containment)
☐ Timestamp-bound PoW: rainbow-table pre-computation is impossible (required hour ±2)
☐ QR pairing OOB confirmation code: attacker-as-fake-target blocked (source shows code, target must echo same code)
☐ Name homoglyph protection: ASCII-only whitelist rejects `alíce`, `alice` (Cyrillic) etc.
☐ Per-identity DHT write quota: compromised key cannot spam the DHT with version++ updates (rate-limited to 10/hour)
☐ One-time pre-keys (X3DH): past messages decryptable NOT later after sk compromise (prekey consumed, deleted)
☐ Multi-replica resolution with quorum: eclipse attack detectable (≥2 matching from ≥3 replicas)
☐ Self-document anomaly watcher: unauthorized update detected → user alerted within ~1 hour

**Revocation persistence (critical — prevents the stale-revocation attack)**:
☐ `IdentityDocument.valid_until` ≤ issued_at + 30 days; consumer rejects stale docs
☐ `master_freshness_sig` updated by the owner' once per ≤30 days; attacker with identity_sk cannot forge (does not own master_sk)
☐ Local `RevocationCache` persists beyond DHT TTL + process restart; verifier consults cache first
☐ Consumer rejects IdentityDocument with `revocation_seq < cached_seq` for the same identity_id
☐ Gossip revocations exchange in each handshake — attacker' you need to compromise ALL peers-that-saw-the-revocation to suppress it for new consumers
☐ Test scenario: owner offline 60 days, attacker republishes pre-revocation doc, new peer connecting 60d later → peer fetches via multiple replicas + asks directly connected peers (gossip) → at least one has revocation → attacker rejected
☐ Worst case: owner's master_freshness_sig expired → attacker's doc also expired → all clients reject; legitimate recovery requires master unlock

**Multi-device / LB**:
☐ 2 nodes under one identity_id work as LB (Any) and as multi-device (All)
☐ Each node has **its own** identity_sk; compromise one → revoke only that instance
☐ Device add/remove in the InstanceRegistry propagate's < 6 hours (TTL)
☐ QR pairing ceremony: source → display QR + OOB code → mobile scan + user compares code → identity live < 90 sec
☐ pair_secret ephemeral (TTL ≤ 5 min, one-time-use)
☐ master_seed **never** transmitted via a pair ceremony (the target generates its own sk)
☐ OOB confirmation code: mismatch aborts pairing with a clear user message
☐ Device linking event reaches existing instances < 30 sec via direct push
☐ Any active identity_sk (of any instance) can update shared records (NameClaim, InstanceRegistry) — version monotonicity ensures consistency

**Names**:
☐ `@alice` resolve to identity_id via DHT + cert chain verify offline
☐ Name squatting deterrence: rare names require ≥15 min PoW, long random ≤1 sec
☐ Name survives identity_sk rotation (stored under identity_id)

**Messenger readiness**:
☐ E2E message to an offline recipient works via the mailbox + ML-KEM (cert chain verified)
☐ **E2E fan-out**: sender encrypts to each active identity_key (per-instance); recipient instance decrypts only its own copy
☐ Multi-device shared mailbox: both instance's receive the message, GC correct after an ACK from all
☐ **One-time pre-keys**: forward secrecy for async messages — compromise master_seed in the future does not decrypt past messages (prekeys consumed and destroyed)
☐ Safety number (fingerprint) is determined deterministically, pair-symmetric
☐ `store_app_state` / `load_app_state` works for an arbitrary app_id, auto-synced across instances
☐ Chat backup → restore: export snapshot → destroy local state → import → recover all data
☐ Mobile wake-up hint reaches a sleeping instance, triggers mailbox pull on resume

**Operational**:
☐ 462.23 integration suite passes 100% in CI
☐ DHT records size ≤ `MAX_DHT_VALUE_BYTES = 4096` in all realistic scenarios
☐ 462.33 messenger dev guide has a working end-to-end example (text chat 2-device messenger in <200 LOC Python)

### Open blockers

- **None inside the epic** — all core dependencies (Ed25519/Falcon crypto, DHT infrastructure, PoW, HKDF, ChaCha20-Poly1305, ML-KEM) already in veil
- External crates for adding to `veilcore/Cargo.toml`:
  - `bip39 = "2"` — 24-word phrase encoding (Phase 2)
  - `argon2 = "0.5"` — memory-hard KDF for encrypted master storage (Phase 2)
  - `qr2term = "0.3"` or `qrcode = "0.14"` — QR rendering (Phase 2)
  - `image = "0.25"` — QR-scanning from image files (Phase 2, optional)
  - `sled = "0.34"` — persistent KV for RevocationCache (462.36)
  - All `no_std`-compatible and audited

### Phase 6.48 — Epic 462 closeout note (2026-05-05)

**Status:** ✅ implementation closed.  All 50 task-rows closed:
47 ✅ done + 3 ⊘ merged (462.41→462.30, 462.42→462.21, 462.43→462.13).
External crates (bip39, argon2, chacha20poly1305, qrcode) already in Cargo.toml.

**Acceptance-checklist updated**: a number of ☐ items are stale after Epic 473/476:

**Stale (mailbox-dependent, Epic 473 dropped mailbox subsystem entirely):**
- ~~"E2E message to an offline recipient via the mailbox + ML-KEM"~~ — async-delivery moved out of the network layer; will be in a separate `veil-mailbox` crate
- ~~"Multi-device shared mailbox: GC after all-ACK"~~ — mailbox dropped
- ~~"Mobile wake-up hint triggers mailbox pull on resume"~~ — mailbox dropped

**Stale (revocation-dependent, Epic 476.S1a/b dropped revocation cache + gossip + freshness-cert):**
- ~~"`master_freshness_sig` updated by the owner' once per ≤30 days"~~ — `master_freshness_sig` field removed from IdentityDocument
- ~~"Local `RevocationCache` persists beyond DHT TTL"~~ — `revocation_cache.rs` removed (714 LoC)
- ~~"Consumer rejects IdentityDocument with `revocation_seq < cached_seq`"~~ — `revocation_seq` field removed
- ~~"Gossip revocations exchange in each handshake"~~ — `revocation_gossip.rs` removed (625 LoC)
- ~~"Self-document anomaly watcher"~~ — `node/identity/watcher.rs` removed (550 LoC)
- ~~"Test scenario: owner offline 60 days, attacker republishes pre-revocation doc"~~ — revocation gone
- ~~"Worst case: owner's master_freshness_sig expired"~~ — freshness gone
- ~~"`IdentityDocument.valid_until` ≤ issued_at + 30 days; consumer rejects stale docs"~~ — strict revocation removed

Replaced with the simplified model: short-lived identity_keys (≤7d validity), auto-reissue from master.

**Still relevant (covered by `crates/veil-identity/src/integration_tests.rs` — 12 scenarios):**
- ✅ Timestamp-bound PoW
- ✅ QR pairing OOB confirmation code
- ✅ Name homoglyph protection (ASCII whitelist)
- ✅ One-time pre-keys (X3DH) — covered by `prekey_bundle.rs`
- ✅ Multi-replica resolution with quorum (Epic 491)
- ✅ 2 nodes under one identity_id (LB + multi-device)
- ✅ Per-instance identity_sk; revoke only the compromised instance
- ✅ QR pair ceremony end-to-end (`scenario_full_pair_ceremony_end_to_end`)
- ✅ pair_secret ephemeral (TTL ≤ 5 min)
- ✅ master_seed never transmitted via pair
- ✅ OOB confirmation mismatch aborts (`scenario_safety_numbers_pair_symmetric`)
- ✅ Any active identity_sk updates shared records (version monotonicity)
- ✅ `@alice` resolve via DHT + cert chain (`scenario_qr_contact_import_then_full_cert_chain_validate`)
- ✅ Name survives identity_sk rotation
- ✅ E2E fan-out — sender encrypts to each active identity_key (`mlkem_fanout.rs`)
- ✅ Safety number deterministic + pair-symmetric

**Outstanding (not yet integration-tested, defer to consumer-driven phase):**
- store_app_state / load_app_state auto-synced across instances — no dedicated integration scenario, but the primitives (NameClaim/InstanceRegistry write APIs) shipped
- Chat backup → restore — out-of-scope for the network layer; nf separate `veil-backup` crate
- 462.33 messenger dev guide — will be sealed once the mailbox crate appears

**Production-wise**: the Epic 462 identity layer is ready.  Open items require a consumer (messenger app) for validation, not additional work in veil.

## Epic 479 — Latency-aware end-to-end routing — closed (absorbed by 137/142/144)

> ✅ **Closure 2026-05-05:** premise of the epic is stale.  Tasks 479.1-479.3
> were in fact implemented in parallel through other epics, even before
> 479 was planned.  Verified in the code:
>
> | # | Status | Where it was actually implemented |
> |---|--------|--------------------------|
> | 479.1 multi-path probing | ✅ done | **Epic 142** Multipath delivery — ECMP + redundant send + flow pinning (`delivery.rs:374` "Epic 136/137: weighted random hop selection") |
> | 479.2 end-to-end RTT samples | ✅ done otherwise | **Epic 137/143** ROUTE_PROBE collects RTT into `RttTable`, which feeds' into `effective_score` (`delivery.rs:367-370`).  Wire-protocol-level timestamps in Forward+ACK — rejected as superfluous: probe-based RTT already works |
> | 479.3 RouteCache by latency_class | ✅ done otherwise | **Epic 144** QoS classes (RT/IN/BK/BG) implemented as per-request score modifiers, not separate caches: `is_realtime` 2× jitter weight, `is_bulk_or_bg` narrow-bw penalty (`delivery.rs:439-447`).  Acceptance-test goal "interactive picks low-latency 2-hop, bulk picks 1-hop" is achieved by the same scoring |
> | 479.4 latency-spike failover | ✅ soft-mitigated | **Epic 137** RTT EWMA automatically penalizes spikes — high-RTT paths fall into the score without an explicit RouteWithdraw.  Soft-mitigation is slower (one EWMA tick vs sub-second withdraw) but simpler, and a hard trigger does not block production |
> | 479.5 5-node sim scenario | ⬜ deferred (verification only) | Useful as and a regression test, does not block functionality.  Existing routing scoring tests cover the logic itself; sim-scenario would be an integration test, not a unit |
>
> Premise "Vivaldi currently ranks only in FIND_NODE" was **incorrect**:
> Vivaldi distance is used in data-path delivery as a fallback when
> RTT is unknown (`delivery.rs:423-424`: `lv.distance_estimate(rv)`).
> This came from Epic 142 ECMP scoring and Epic 218.10 cap-flag filter.

**Goal:** Vivaldi currently ranks only in FIND_NODE (the topology of the DHT walk). It needs to be extended to **data-path forward** — route selection to the recipient by real end-to-end latency, not just by XOR-distance.

| # | Task | Files | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 479.1 | Multi-path probing: when there are ≥ 2 next-hop candidates to the target — a parallel probe (small ping), pick lowest-latency. | node/routing/* + node/dispatcher/control.rs | ✅ done — Epic 142 |
| 479.2 | End-to-end RTT samples via the forward path — each Forward-frame carries `sent_at_unix_ms`, ACK contains `received_at_unix_ms` + `processed_at_unix_ms`. | proto/delivery.rs + node/dispatcher/delivery.rs | ✅ done otherwise — Epic 137/143 ROUTE_PROBE |
| 479.3 | RouteCache now keyed-by-`(target, latency_class)` — bulk-traffic vs interactive different paths. | node/routing/cache.rs | ✅ done otherwise — Epic 144 QoS modifiers |
| 479.4 | Failover triggered by latency-spike (the current route grew > 2× baseline) — RouteWithdraw + repick. | node/dispatcher/control.rs | ✅ soft-mitigated — Epic 137 RTT EWMA |
| 479.5 | Sim-scenario: a 5-node network with different latency between pairs — interactive traffic picks the path with min latency despite a higher hop-count. | sim/scenarios/* | ⬜ deferred — verification only |

**Acceptance:** when there are 2 paths (1-hop 200ms vs 2-hop 50ms+50ms=100ms) interactive traffic picks the 2-hop; bulk traffic picks the 1-hop (less overhead). Failover on a latency-spike < 500ms.
