## Epic 481 — Out-of-band bootstrap — closed (3 ✅ done + 2 sliced ship; 2 sub-pieces deferred)

> ✅ **Closure 2026-05-05:** 5 acceptance items shipped directly or
> absorbed:
>
> - 481.1 QR-invite ✅
> - 481.2 Password-URL ✅
> - 481.3 Friend-to-friend: **CLI signed-invite ✅** (operator publishes signed URL, recipient verifies); in-band wire-frame deferred (see below)
> - 481.4 Multi-source seeds: **HTTPS ✅** + **signed-bundle ✅ via Epic 492**; `.onion` deferred (see below)
> - 481.5 Cache ✅ (per-node disk cache; DHT-record publish/fetch not needed — nodes without bootstrap never reach the DHT)
>
> Acceptance ("a new user joins in < 1 min via QR / HTTPS even if
> the public seed-IP is blocked") **achieved** via 1+2+4+5.
>
> ## Deferred sub-pieces (large-scope, parked)
>
> | # | What | Why deferred | Re-open trigger |
> |---|------|----------------|------------------|
> | 481.3-inband | New wire-frame `IntroduceRequest{introducer, sponsoree, expiry, sig}` for in-band peer vouching via PEX | ~200-300 LoC of new infrastructure (proto + dispatcher + sig verify + rate-limit + composability with PEX trust) with marginal ROI: the bootstrap problem is already solved by 5 other mechanisms; transitive-trust signal adds no new value on top of the post-handshake trust model.  YAGNI until a concrete use-case | A scenario appears, that is not covered by the existing 5 layers (for example, mass-onboarding a flash-mob without an operator URL) |
> | 481.4-onion | `.onion` seed-source via a Tor SOCKS proxy | Requires Tor integration — external dep ~1000+ LoC OR external `torsocks`-style transparent proxy.  Censorship coverage is already strong without `.onion` (HTTPS on CDN + signed bundle on DHT + cache); `.onion` adds defense-in-depth for high-paranoia scenarios but does not close a unique gap | Operator deployment will decide "Tor is needed for a concrete threat" |

**Goal:** Censor blocks the hardcoded seed-list. A new user must join the network via a channel, that the censor does not control: QR from a friend, password-protected URL, a paper note.

| # | Task | Files | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 481.1 | QR-code invite — contains `(node_id, transport_uri, signed_introduction)` + optional PoW-challenge for anti-spam. CLI: `veil invite --qr`. | cmd/invite.rs (new) + identity/sovereign.rs (existing pair-* infra) | ✅ done — new module `node/bootstrap/invite.rs` with the canonical URL `veil:bootstrap?pk=<b64>&t=<endpoint>&a=<algo>&nc=<b64>[&tls_cert=<b64>][&tls_ca_cert=<b64>]`. Field set 1-to-1 matches `cfg::BootstrapPeer` (transport, public_key, nonce, algo, optional TLS), so `decode_uri()` directly appends to `[[bootstrap_peers]]`. **No new wire format / no signature** on the invite itself — out-of-band channel (QR scan / paper note / signed text) is considered uncensored, pubkey from the URL is verified by the handshake afterwards; replay not a threat since `bootstrap_peers` are discarded after the initial FIND_NODE. Canonical encode-order guarantees stable QR caching; decode accepts arbitrary order, case-insensitive scheme prefix. New CLI surface: `veil-cli bootstrap {invite [--qr] \| join --uri <...> \| decode --uri <...>}`. `invite` takes the first `[[listen]]` entry (preferring `advertise` if set) + `[identity]` keypair → URL [+ ASCII halfblock QR via shared qrcode-crate path]. `join` idempotently appends to the config (skip if the pk is already present). `decode` — preflight without mutation. Limits: `MAX_BOOTSTRAP_URI_BYTES = 4 KiB` (Falcon-512 + TLS chain fits). 13 unit tests (round-trip minimal + with_tls / arbitrary field order / case-insensitive scheme / bad scheme / missing pk / dup field / unknown field / oversized / endpoint reserved chars / unknown algo / canonical encoding stable / handler-layer encode-decode) + 1 helper test. Build clean. |
| 481.2 | Password-protected pairing URL — `veil://pair/<base64>?p=<password-hash>`, recipient enters a password, decode invite. | cmd/invite.rs | ✅ done — new module `node/bootstrap/encrypted_invite.rs` wraps the existing 481.1 invite in Argon2id + ChaCha20-Poly1305 envelope. **Censorship-resistance pattern:** the operator posts the URL on a large public channel (forum, paste, social media — easy to censor), shares the password on a small private channel (Telegram, in-person, Signal — hard to censor at scale). Censor sees the URL but cannot decrypt without the password. **Wire format:** `veil:pair?b=<base64url>` where the body = magic("EI") + version + kdf-id + Argon2id-params(m_cost_kib, t_cost, p_cost) + salt(16B) + nonce(12B) + ciphertext+tag. KDF params ship in-band → future tightening (e.g. m_cost = 64MiB) loads older URLs transparently. **Defaults:** m_cost = 32 MiB / 2 passes / 1 par (interactive-friendly even on a budget phone, ≈ 200-400 ms on modern hardware, raises offline brute-force to ≈ 10⁹ attempts/$ for a moderately complex password). **Anti-downgrade:** decoder rejects KDF params below MIN_M_COST_KIB(8MiB)/MIN_T_COST(1)/MIN_P_COST(1) — censor can't re-publish URL with m_cost=1 and brute-force on a laptop. **AAD = "veil.pair.v1"** binds ciphertext to purpose. **Salt + nonce per encryption are random** so two postings of the same invite produce distinct URLs (observer cannot correlate "this is the same invite reissued" without decrypting). **Threat model docs** are explicit which does NOT defend: weak passwords, compromise of password channel itself, replay (inner URL is static — issuer needs one-shot semantics → rotate `[identity]` keypair after redemption). **CLI integration:** existing `veil-cli bootstrap invite/join/decode` extended with `--password <PW>` flag — without a password emit/decode plain `veil:bootstrap?…` (Epic 481.1 path), with a password emit/decode `veil:pair?…`. Dispatcher `decode_either_uri` routes encrypted vs plain based on URL scheme prefix; **rejects `--password` on plain URI loudly** (operator likely pasted wrong URL from clipboard, fail-loud beats silently joining wrong network). 11 unit tests cover roundtrip + TLS-fields preserved + wrong-password (Aead error indistinguishable from tamper, no oracle) + tampered-ciphertext + bad-scheme + malformed-base64 + KDF-downgrade-rejection + truncated-body-no-panic + size-cap + distinct-ciphertexts; 4 CLI dispatcher tests cover encrypted-routes-correctly + missing-password-error + plain-with-password-rejected + plain-without-password-works. Build clean. |
| 481.3 | Friend-to-friend gossip: an already-connected peer can issue a one-time bootstrap-token (signed) for its friend — friend connects via a peer-introducer. | proto/discovery.rs + node/dispatcher/discovery.rs | 🔄 partial — **CLI signed-invite slice done** (the highest-impact zero-rework piece; network-layer in-band token issuance + transitive trust via PEX deferred — both require new wire frames which carry rework risk and the CLI-only slice already covers the operator's primary use case). New `node/bootstrap/signed_invite.rs` complements Epic 481.2 encrypted-invite: 481.2 protects channel (password = shared secret per recipient — doesn't scale), 481.3 attests issuer (signature verifies single-issuer-many-recipients — operator signs ONCE, posts publicly, anyone with issuer pubkey verifies). **Wire format:** `veil:signed-invite?b=<base64url>` body = magic("SI") + version + algo + issuer_pk + issued_at_unix(u64) + expiry_unix(u64) + signature + inner_uri. Inner URI = existing 481.1 `veil:bootstrap?…` so signed-invite is a thin wrapper, not a redesign. **Canonical signed message** = `"veil-signed-invite:v1\n" + inner_uri + "\n" + issued_at + "\n" + expiry` — domain-separated (prevents cross-protocol signature reuse — an identity_proof sig from elsewhere can't be repurposed), pins validity window in the signed payload (attacker who captures envelope cannot extend lifetime). **Anti-tamper:** `tampered_signature_rejected`, `tampered_inner_uri_rejected`, `tampered_validity_window_rejected` tests verify each field is signature-protected. **Validity cap:** MAX_VALIDITY_SECS = 1 year (operator typo `--expiry-secs 10` getting 10 years is impossible). **MAX_SIGNED_INVITE_BYTES = 8 KiB** (Falcon-512 envelope sanity ceiling — Ed25519 typical ≈ 600 B). **Algos:** Ed25519 + Falcon-512, matches existing `SignatureAlgorithm` enum. CLI surface extends 481.1: `bootstrap invite --sign [--expiry-secs N]` signs with local `[identity]` keypair (1h default validity); `bootstrap join --uri T --verify-issuer <PK>` (REQUIRED for signed URIs — without expected issuer, accepting an envelope is meaningless because the envelope's claimed issuer is attacker-controlled); `bootstrap decode --uri T [--verify-issuer <PK>]` (preflight; without `--verify-issuer` shows `UNVERIFIED — claimed issuer: <PK>` block so operator compares against OOB-known pubkey before trusting). **Mutual exclusion:** `--sign + --password`, `signed URI + --password`, `encrypted URI + --verify-issuer`, `plain URI + --verify-issuer` all rejected loudly (not silently accepted) — operator likely pasted wrong URL from clipboard, fail-loud beats joining wrong network. 13 module unit tests cover sign/verify roundtrip + verify-without-expected-issuer (internal-consistency mode) + expired/wrong-issuer/tampered-sig/tampered-inner-uri/tampered-window/inverted-window/validity-too-long/bad-scheme/oversized-URL rejections + canonical-message-domain-separator. 6 dispatcher tests cover all CLI flag-combination paths. **Composition with 481.2:** since both are wrappers around `BootstrapPeer`, an operator that needs BOTH attestation AND channel encryption can `sign(encrypt(invite, pw), sk)` manually as two-pass — defaults are mutually exclusive at the single-flag layer to keep CLI surface obvious. |
| 481.4 | Multi-source seed-list: config accepts `[bootstrap]` a section with a DNS-name (resolves to a pool of IPs), HTTPS URL (returns signed seed-list), `.onion` (via Tor if installed). | cfg/model.rs + node/bootstrap/* | 🔄 → ✅ done (mostly) — **HTTPS slice done** (this entry); **signed seed-list ✅ absorbed by Epic 492** (`crates/veil-bootstrap/src/signed_bundle.rs` — sign/decode/verify implemented, sim-tested cross-node distribution). Only `.onion` is deferred (Tor integration scope). Original entry text: New `node/bootstrap/https.rs`: hand-rolled HTTP/1.1 GET over the existing TLS stack — **no new deps**, reuses `connect_tls_client_stream` (rustls default OR `tls-boring` Chrome fingerprint under feature flag) so the bootstrap fetch carries the same JA3 as veil traffic — DPI sees ordinary HTTPS, not a fingerprintable rust-HTTP-client pattern. **Wire format reused:** response body parsed via existing `decode_bootstrap_bundle` (Epic 455.4 JSON shape) → operator can serve the same file from HTTPS, DHT publish, and local config. **Hard caps:** `MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES = 64 KiB`, `MAX_TOTAL_BYTES = 72 KiB` (defends against adversarial CDN returning multi-MB blob), `DEFAULT_FETCH_TIMEOUT = 10 s` (best-effort — fall through to other layers, never block startup). **HTTP minimal-by-design:** GET only, `Connection: close`, no chunked encoding, no compression, no redirects, no cookies, no auth — operators that need any of those host a redirect-free static endpoint. **`http://` rejected** at parse time (cert chain is the operator's authentication that the response is theirs, not a censor-injected forgery). **`user@host` userinfo rejected** as phishing footgun. New `cfg::GlobalConfig::bootstrap_https_urls: Vec<String>` (default empty; multiple URLs allowed for redundancy across CDNs). Wired into `spawn_bootstrap_task`: fires UNCONDITIONALLY when any URL is configured (operator may have stale `bootstrap_peers` AND fresh HTTPS endpoint), each URL fetched concurrently, results dedup'd by pubkey, registered + dialed via the same outbound-connector path the DNS layer uses (peer_id namespace `0x8800_0000` — between DNS at `0x8000_0000` and synthetic/gateway range `≥ 0xC000_0000`, so the discovered-peer cache from Epic 481.5 properly skips ephemeral bootstrap peers). **Censorship-resistance value:** operator hot-rotates seed-IPs by editing one JSON file on a CDN — no binary rebuild, no DNS TTL to wait out. Censor would need to (a) block the operator's HTTPS hostname (CDN-fronting + multiple hostnames defeats this), (b) block the entire CDN (high collateral damage — Cloudflare/CloudFront/Fastly host millions of legit sites), or (c) compromise the cert authority (out-of-band, expensive). **Diag updated:** `node bootstrap-status` now shows 5 layers (added L3 HTTPS bootstrap URLs, with `curl -v <url>` verification hint), verdict criteria recomputed against `total_layers = 5`. 17 unit tests cover URL parsing (all rejection paths), HTTP response parsing (status/body extraction, non-200, missing-separator, bad proto, truncated stream no-panic), end-to-end via `tokio::io::duplex` fixture (request shape verified, response decoded into BootstrapPeer list, 503 propagates as BadStatus, oversized response triggers TooLarge), bundle-format compat with layer-2/3. Build clean. **Hardening (post-ship dedup fix):** new `filter_already_known(seeds, known_pubkeys)` helper prevents the HTTPS task from registering peers that ALREADY appear in `[[bootstrap_peers]]`, `[[peers]]`, or the discovered-peer cache — fixes a regression where peer X listed in BOTH operator config AND HTTPS bundle (or BOTH cache AND HTTPS bundle) was getting two PeerConfigEntry registrations and double-dialed. Real-world impact: an operator who hosts the same seed list at two CDN endpoints, or pins a friend in `bootstrap_peers` while also fetching them from HTTPS, was burning double the battery + creating twice the DPI-visible handshake traffic per startup. Snapshot of known pubkeys is taken SYNCHRONOUSLY before spawning the HTTPS task (no race vs concurrent reloads / cache upserts). When dedup drops > 0 entries, an `bootstrap.https.dedup` info log surfaces the count for operator visibility. 5 new unit tests cover: drops pubkey already in bootstrap_peers, drops pubkey already in cache, keeps all when known set is empty, handles empty input, drops all when every pubkey known (pathological case). |
| 481.5 | Seed-list as a DHT record — after the first successful join the node caches an extended seed-list, and uses it on subsequent joins. Resilience against takedown of the original seeds. | node/dht/* | ✅ done — scoped to **discovered-peer cache** as the highest-impact, lowest-rework slice (DHT-record publish/fetch deferred — nodes without bootstrap'never reach the DHT at all, so a per-node disk cache has marginal ROI vs DHT-side wiring). New `node/bootstrap/cache.rs` with `DiscoveredPeerCache` (HashMap, keyed by pubkey, LRU eviction at `MAX_DISCOVERED_PEERS = 32`, atomic-write JSON envelope `{version: u32, peers: [...]}`). Fields persisted = wire-compatible `BootstrapPeer` + `last_seen_unix`. Wired across runtime: (a) new `cfg::GlobalConfig::discovered_peers_cache_path: Option<String>` knob; (b) `NodeRuntime` and `NodeServices` hold `Arc<Mutex<DiscoveredPeerCache>>`, init at startup from configured path (or in-memory variant if the path == None); (c) `outbound_connector.rs` upserts cache after every successful handshake-complete (skipping synthetic peer_ids `≥ 0xC000_0000` so gateway-bridge ephemeral peers do not pollute the cache); (d) `service_tasks.rs::spawn_bootstrap_task` splices cached snapshot as the 4th rung of the fallback chain — after `config.bootstrap_peers` / `builtin_seeds()` / DNS, before "give up"; (e) maintenance loop calls periodic `tick_save_discovered_peers_cache()`; (f) `do_stop_flushes` calls final flush so even a crash between ticks does not lose fresh entries. 9 unit tests (empty load / upsert+snapshot / same-pubkey-refresh / freshest-first ordering / LRU at cap / save-load round-trip / corrupt-file → empty cache / in-memory save no-op / disk-format envelope shape) — all pass. Build clean. **Censorship resistance:** authoritarian-state censor can take down operator seed-IPs (1), poison shipped binary's `builtin_seeds()` (2), blacklist DNS domain (3) and node still bootstraps from layer (4) — peers it has personally proved reachable. Censor would need per-user state (every IP this user has ever talked to) plus simultaneous block of all of them — dramatically more expensive than blocking a published seed list. |

**Acceptance:** a new user, who has a pair/group of friends already in the network, joins in < 1 minutes by scanning a QR; a new user without friends-in-the-network joins via a bootstrap URL (HTTPS) even if the public seed-IP is blocked.

## Epic 482 — Optional anonymity (Tor-like circuits) — closed (functionally complete + integration-tested)

> ✅ **Closure 2026-05-05:** anonymity stack functionally closed
> end-to-end + 2 integration sim tests + drop_subscriber wired +
> AS-diversity picker shipped (commit `8ae42a7`).  7 sub-tasks earlier
> shipped as primitives/slices; integration tests confirm full
> pipeline works on the real wire.
>
> Real deferred items (parked, not stale):
> - **Anti-loop TTL field** — wire-format break for a marginal win;
>   existing PER_HOP_OVERHEAD=92 gives natural payload shrinkage per
>   loop iteration → max ~5-6 self-loops before unusable.
> - **Stateful CircuitId** — persistent circuits ~1500 LoC scope;
>   defer until perf-driven need.
> - **Path-product latency optimization** — pairwise RTT discovery; own slice.
> - **Bandwidth-quota tracking + claim verification + reputation** —
>   abuse-prevention follow-ups.

**Goal:** Optional mode: send_anonymous() builds a 2-3 hop circuit, sender / receiver IP are mutually unknown, observer cannot correlate by timing/size.

| # | Task | Files | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 482.1 | Circuit abstraction: `CircuitId`, per-hop layer encryption (X25519 ECDH with the relay-pubkey, AEAD layer). | node/anonymity/circuit.rs (new) | 🔄 partial — **two primitives shipped (single-hop AEAD + multi-hop circuit envelope); CircuitId for stateful circuits, return-path, relay-directory lookup deferred**. **(a) `node/anonymity/onion.rs`** — single-hop AEAD primitive (described below). **(b) `node/anonymity/circuit.rs`** — multi-hop circuit envelope on top of `onion`. Each layer's plaintext is `[32B next_hop_id][inner]` where `next_hop_id` is the destination of the next forward (or `FINAL_HOP_SENTINEL = [0u8; 32]` meaning "I am the destination, what follows is the payload"). All-zeros sentinel is safe because real `node_id`s are BLAKE3(pubkey) → all-zeros pre-image is cryptographically unreachable. **API:** `build_circuit(payload, hops: &[Hop]) -> Vec<u8>` (sender side; wraps in REVERSE hop order — innermost hop first), `peel_circuit(envelope, my_sk) -> Result<PeelResult>` (relay side; `PeelResult::Forward { next_hop, inner }` for intermediate hops or `PeelResult::Final { payload }` for destination). **`PER_HOP_OVERHEAD` = 92 B** (32 next-hop-id + 60 onion). For 510 B cell + N hops, max user payload = `510 - 92*N` (sentinel is internal overhead, not user-visible). N=1 → 418 B, N=2 → 326 B, N=3 → 234 B, N=5 → 50 B, N=6 rejected at build time. **(c) `node/anonymity/packet.rs`** — user-facing API combining cell + circuit + onion. `build_anonymous_cell(payload, hops) -> Result<[u8; CELL_SIZE]>` and `peel_anonymous_cell(cell, my_sk) -> Result<CellPeelResult>` where `CellPeelResult::Forward { next_hop, outbound_cell }` already carries a 512-byte cell — relay forwards as-is, no further packing. Re-packing through `cell::pack` after each peel is the LOAD-BEARING anonymity property: without it, observer sees cell sizes shrink monotonically along the circuit (512 → 420 → 328 → 236 ...) and can immediately infer hop position, deanonymizing. 11 packet tests including `epic482_1_observer_sees_uniform_cell_sizes_through_all_hops` (collects byte lengths of every transmission in a 3-hop circuit, asserts all == 512) and `epic482_1_observer_cannot_correlate_two_sends_of_same_payload` (same payload + same hops sent twice → distinct cells, fresh ephemeral keys). **Anonymity property** verified end-to-end by `epic482_1_intermediate_hop_cannot_see_payload_or_final_destination`: hop2 in a 4-hop circuit sees neither the payload bytes nor hop4's node_id in its plaintext (only hop3's node_id + hop3's still-encrypted onion). **Tight scope (deferred to follow-ups):** NO `CircuitId` for stateful circuits (this module ships stateless single-message circuits — one onion = one message; persistent circuits need a separate session-keyed mode), NO return-path / reply-path encoding (rendezvous-style return-onion is Epic 482.5 or a one-shot `replyable: true` flag), NO padding to fixed size at circuit layer (cell layer provides that), NO relay-directory lookup (caller supplies hop tuples; DHT-based relay discovery is Epic 482.4). 8 circuit unit tests cover: build rejects zero hops, single-hop yields Final at the only hop (degenerate base case useful as building block), 3-hop walks through all hops correctly, intermediate hop cannot see payload or final destination (THE anonymity property), per-hop overhead constant matches components (92 B), envelope size grows by exactly PER_HOP_OVERHEAD per added hop, wrong hop sk at intermediate fails loudly with AEAD error (no silent forwarding-decision leak), empty payload through 1 hop works (circuit liveness ping). Combined with **(a) `node/anonymity/onion.rs`** primitive: single-hop X25519 ECDH + ChaCha20-Poly1305 + BLAKE3-derived AEAD key, wire format `[32B ephemeral_pk][12B nonce][ciphertext + 16B tag]` = 60 B overhead, ephemeral_pk + nonce bound into AEAD AAD with `"veil-onion-v1\0"` domain prefix (anti-tamper + anti-cross-protocol-replay), fresh ephemeral keypair per call (forward secrecy), 12 onion unit tests covering roundtrip / all four tamper modes / multi-hop composition / hop-cannot-skip-layers negative test. New `node/anonymity/onion.rs`: single-hop X25519 ECDH + ChaCha20-Poly1305 + BLAKE3-derived AEAD key. **Wire format per layer:** `[32B ephemeral_pk][12B nonce][ciphertext + 16B tag]` = 60 B overhead. **Anti-tamper:** ephemeral_pk + nonce bound into AEAD AAD with `"veil-onion-v1\0"` domain prefix → tampering ANY field (ephemeral_pk, nonce, ciphertext) fails AEAD verification (defense in depth on top of AEAD's natural integrity). Domain prefix prevents cross-protocol signature reuse. **Forward secrecy:** fresh ephemeral keypair per call, secret half consumed on diffie_hellman, never stored. **API:** `wrap_for_hop(payload, hop_pk_bytes) -> Vec<u8>` + `unwrap_at_hop(envelope, hop_sk) -> Result<Vec<u8>>`. **Multi-hop composition** is `wrap → wrap → wrap` in REVERSE hop order; demonstrated by `epic482_1_layered_three_hop_composition` test. Each hop unwraps ONE layer, sees only the next layer's bytes, cannot decrypt past — verified by `epic482_1_layered_hop_cannot_skip_to_inner_layer` negative test. **Composes with cell (Epic 482.2):** for N-hop circuit fitting in 510 B cell, max innermost payload = `510 - 60 * N` (N=3 → 330 B). **Tight scope (deferred):** (a) NO multi-hop wire format — circuit module owns hop ordering, next-hop addressing, return-path. (b) NO padding — output is `len(payload) + 60`; cell layer provides fixed-size envelope. (c) NO replay protection — circuit owner does sequence numbers + freshness. **CircuitId, hop directory, circuit construction, send_anonymous() API** all still ⬜ todo — but the cryptographic floor is now stable + tested. 12 unit tests cover roundtrip (small / empty), envelope-size invariant, wrong-hop-sk / tampered-ciphertext / tampered-ephemeral_pk / tampered-nonce all fail AEAD, too-small envelope pre-AEAD rejection, two-wraps-of-same-payload yield distinct envelopes (anti-correlation), 3-hop layered composition correctness, layered hop cannot skip to inner layer (the load-bearing onion property). |
| 482.2 | Fixed cell size (512 bytes) with padding — sender sends a cell-of-cells, observer sees only uniform 512-byte chunks. | node/anonymity/cell.rs (new) | 🔄 partial — **primitive shipped, wire integration deferred to circuit work (Epic 482.1)**. New `node::anonymity::cell` module (first occupant of new `node/anonymity/` directory). **Wire format:** exactly 512 bytes per cell — `[u16 BE payload_len][payload bytes][zero padding to 512]`. **Censorship-resistance value:** observer sees a stream of identical-size chunks regardless of actual message size; cannot infer message length, cannot tell two cells belong to the same logical message (when paired with circuit layering from 482.1). Combined with layered AEAD (also 482.1), observer also can't tell sender / final destination. **API:** `pack(payload) -> Result<[u8; 512]>` + `unpack(cell) -> Result<Vec<u8>>`, `MAX_PAYLOAD_PER_CELL = 510`. **Anti-tamper:** `unpack` validates EVERY padding byte is 0x00 (full walk, not short-circuit — a short-circuit would technically leak a one-byte position oracle though at this layer cells are AEAD-authenticated by the caller so practical impact is nil; the full walk costs 512 byte compares per cell which is negligible vs the AEAD verify). Catches bit-rot (caller forgot AEAD-wrap), malleability attempts, and pack-implementation bugs in the network. **Tight scope (deferred to follow-ups, not this commit):** (a) NO multi-cell messages — caller must fit ≤ 510 B; multi-cell sequencing belongs in a higher layer that owns sequence numbers + reassembly. (b) NO encryption at this layer — caller wraps cells in AEAD before the wire (separation keeps the primitive testable without crypto fixtures and lets the circuit owner pick its own AEAD shape: single-key vs onion). (c) NO timing controls — cell-rate shaping (constant-rate emit vs natural traffic) belongs in dispatcher integration. **Why ship the primitive standalone:** zero-rework foundational piece that every Epic 482.x sub-piece will depend on; getting the wire format settled now means later integration work is just plumbing, not redesign. 10 unit tests cover: roundtrip (small / empty / max-size), pack rejects oversize, unpack rejects wrong-size input + declared-len overflow + nonzero padding (including first-padding-byte edge case for off-by-one), distinct-payloads-distinct-cells sanity, payload-size-does-not-leak (1-byte vs 510-byte both produce 512-byte cells). |
| 482.3 | Relay role: opt-in, a node declares «I am willing to be a relay» in `CapabilitiesPayload.flags`. Bandwidth-quota separate from ordinary traffic. | proto/session.rs + node/abuse/* | 🔄 partial — **declaration shipped, separate bandwidth-quota deferred to follow-up**. New bit `cap_flags::ANONYMITY_RELAY = 1 << 2` in `CapabilitiesPayload.flags` — distinct from existing `CAN_RELAY` (= forwarding arbitrary frames for indirect-delivery), because the anonymity-relay role carries other costs (constant-rate cells, anti-correlation timing requirements, extra bandwidth budget) and requires explicit opt-in. New config section `[anonymity]` with `relay_capable: bool` (default false — anonymity relay should not be enabled by default). The field is cached on `NodeRuntime`, `NodeServices`, and `SessionRuntimeContext` (snapshot at startup, propagated through 3 scaffolding types), is read during the handshake `complete_handshake_from_capabilities` and sets the bit next to the existing `SUPPORTS_SOVEREIGN_IDENTITY`. **Wire-format change** (without backward-compat per user permission): the `perform_ovl1_handshake` function gained a 13th argument `anonymity_relay_capable: bool`. The relay directory layer (Epic 482.4) will filter circuit candidates by this bit — operator that did not opt in' will never be picked as a hop. **Tight scope (deferred to follow-ups, a separate slice):** separate bandwidth-quota for anonymity traffic vs ordinary traffic (needs tracking infra at the per-session level — adds rework risk in the current iteration); cell-rate shaping (constant-rate emit) — that is already dispatcher integration (Epic 482.x main); per-session counter "how many circuits I forward'" — abuse-prevention follow-up. **Tests:** 3 end-to-end handshake tests via `tokio::io::duplex` exercise wire format in both directions: bit OFF by default (most common case — operator did not opt in'), bit ON when opted in by both peers (Epic 482.4 will be matching), asymmetric case (A opted in, B did not — each side sees the TRUTH of the peer', no information leak across handshake). |
| 482.4 | Relay directory — DHT-stored signed entry `(relay_node_id, advertised_bandwidth, last_seen)`; sender queries to build the circuit. | node/anonymity/directory.rs (new) | 🔄 partial — **primitive shipped, periodic-publish + sender-query the plumbing as a separate slice'**. New `node/anonymity/directory.rs`. **Wire format (binary):** `[magic="RD"][version][sig_algo][node_id 32B][x25519_pk 32B][advertised_bps u32 BE][last_published_unix u64 BE][issuer_pk_len u16][issuer_pk][sig_len u16][signature]`. **DHT key:** `relay_directory_dht_key(node_id) = BLAKE3("veil:v1:anonymity-relay\0" || node_id)` — domain-separated from other DHT-key derivations (verified test `relay_directory_dht_key_domain_separated_from_bootstrap_bundle` — gen-purpose anti-cross-protocol-injection). **Architectural decision: per-node DHT-keys, not a single directory blob** — Tor-style "directory authorities" — that is a single point of takedown, which a censor in an authoritarian state would attack first. Instead each relay self-publishes'. Senders' discover candidates via their DHT routing table / PEX, then `dht_get_local(relay_directory_dht_key(candidate))` for the signed entry. Filtering at the sender side; no central authority that can revoke or censor a relay. **Canonical signed message:** `"veil-relay-directory:v1\0" || node_id || x25519_pk || bandwidth_be || last_published_be` — domain prefix prevents cross-protocol signature reuse (signed_invite from Epic 481.3 cannot be replayed'). Including all metadata in the signed message: attacker cannot substitute a different `x25519_pk` (would let an attacker decrypt onion traffic for a legitimate relay), inflate `advertised_bps` (skew sender's load-balancing), or backdate `last_published_unix` (extend entry past natural freshness window) — ALL FOUR tamper modes have negative tests. **Algos:** Ed25519 + Falcon-512 (existing `SignatureAlgorithm` enum). **Freshness:** `is_fresh(entry, now, window)` — separate from verify, configurable per-deployment (default 24h). Sender can tighten to 1h to bias toward freshly-republished, or relax to 7d for weakly-connected nodes. **SENDER-SIDE DISCOVERY SHIPPED:** new helper `directory::discover_relay_hops(candidate_node_ids, fetch, now_unix, freshness_window) -> Vec<DiscoveredRelay>` — designed around injected `fetch` closure (NOT a hard dependency on `KademliaService`) so the primitive is testable against an in-memory `HashMap` stub + integrable at runtime layer with the real `dht.get_local(relay_directory_dht_key(...))` + future-proof for iterative-find DHT lookup (network-wide discovery instead of local-only). Filter chain per candidate: `fetch → decode_entry → verify_entry → is_fresh → node_id-matches-DHT-key`. The last check — anti-impersonation: without it an attacker could publish A's signed entry under B's DHT-key — sender which iterates its routing table and sees B would pull out the attacker's `x25519_pk` thinking this is B's relay key. `discover_relay_hops` enforces that the entry's claimed `node_id` matches the candidate under which it was fetched. **9 discovery tests:** empty candidates → empty result; valid+fresh candidates → returned (preserves input order — verified by separate test); missing DHT entry → silently skipped; stale entry → silently skipped; tampered entry → silently skipped (signature verify fails); node_id mismatch → silently filtered (anti-impersonation negative test + sanity-check what would happen with corrected DHT key); mixed-realistic-candidates → only valid+fresh survives (composes ALL filters); end-to-end round-trip with sign_entry → decoded back identically (publish + discovery wiring stays consistent). Output type `DiscoveredRelay { hop, advertised_bps, last_published_unix }` carries metadata alongside circuit-layer Hop so a downstream load-balancer / freshness-preferer can sort candidates by something other than arrival order. **Anonymity stack is now FUNCTIONALLY COMPLETE on the publish + discovery sides** — the next natural slice is integration into the dispatcher (Epic 482.7 send_anonymous() API + new frame family for onion cells), but before that all the necessary infrastructure is ready. **Bandwidth-claim verification —** the field is operator-self-reported, relays can lie; reputation-based downweighting — a separate follow-up. Revocation — relays which want to stop they simply stop publishing; entries naturally expire from DHT via the value-store TTL. **PUBLISH the plumbing SHIPPED:** new field `cfg::AnonymityConfig::advertised_bps: u32` (default 0 = "I don't know / don't shape" — senders treat as "lowest-priority candidate"); `NodeRuntime` now holds `anonymity_x25519_sk: Arc<x25519_dalek::StaticSecret>` (generated fresh at startup — rotates on restart, fine because directory entries 24h freshness-bounded; in-memory only, no on-disk persistence — compromise of disk = loss of past anonymity, exactly what forward-secret rotation prevents); new maintenance helper `tick_publish_relay_directory_entry(...)` builds + signs + `dht.store_local(relay_directory_dht_key(node_id), entry_bytes)` each maintenance tick (typ. 60s). When `relay_capable = false` — strict no-op (returns false, no DHT mutation). When `true` — each tick advances `last_published_unix` so DHT consumers see the node as "freshly alive" inside the 24h freshness window. Without this slice'the capability flag (Epic 482.3) and the signed-entry primitive were sitting code — now relay-capable nodes ACTUALLY publish and DHT consumers ACTUALLY can discover them via `dht.get_local(relay_directory_dht_key(...))`. **3 helper tests** (via the real `KademliaService::new` + `NodeLogger::new_noop()`, not mocked): no-op-when-disabled (nothing in DHT), publishes-and-fetchable-when-enabled (entry in DHT, decode + verify pass, x25519_pk + bps + node_id matches), advances-last-published-on-repeated-calls (two tick's → strictly increasing timestamps after 1s sleep). **18 unit tests primitive +** sign/decode/verify roundtrip, DHT key deterministic + distinct per node_id + domain-separated from the bootstrap-bundle, ALL FOUR tamper modes (x25519_pk / advertised_bps / last_published / node_id) fail verify, tampered signature fails, wrong issuer_pk fails, freshness within window accepts, freshness past window rejects, freshness window configurable, unsupported version rejected, bad magic rejected, truncated bytes rejected, oversized blob rejected pre-decode, typical entry well under 4 KiB cap. |
| 482.5 | Rendezvous points (hidden-service-style) — receiver advertise rendezvous_point in DHT, sender builds a circuit to the rendezvous, without knowing the receiver-IP. | node/anonymity/rendezvous.rs (new) | 🔄 partial — **slice 1: signed advertisement primitive SHIPPED**; rendezvous-relay state machine + sender API + receiver API + DHT republish loop — separate slices (mirror of the Epic 484.3 progression where slice 1 was the manifest primitive). **Why rendezvous matters for censorship resistance:** without it, receiver needs **direct inbound connectivity** (or via gateway) to be reachable.  Under aggressive inbound-blocking (CGN-NAT, censor blocking inbound TCP, mobile carrier firewall) receiver simply cannot accept inbound connections — game over.  Rendezvous flips the connectivity requirement: BOTH sender and the receiver only need OUTBOUND connectivity (which censors can't realistically block — it's HTTPS-shaped traffic that would cripple all browsing if blocked).  Architectural inspiration: Tor onion services.  **Slice 1 (signed advertisement primitive):** new `node/anonymity/rendezvous.rs` with a `RendezvousAd` struct.  **Wire format:** binary BE with a magic="RA" + version + sig_algo + receiver_node_id (32B) + rendezvous_node_id (32B, third-party meeting point) + auth_cookie (16B, shared secret receiver gives senders out-of-band — rendezvous accepts only Introduce frames presenting known cookie) + receiver_x25519_pk (32B, sender encrypts Introduce frame to this key — even rendezvous CANT see what sender sent, only that they sent it; forward secrecy via per-ad rotation) + valid_from_unix (8B) + valid_until_unix (8B) + issuer_pk (varlen base64) + signature (varlen).  **Anti-tamper:** signature covers ALL fields including auth_cookie + receiver_x25519_pk + validity window — censor that captures an old ad + tries to redirect senders to their own rendezvous OR substitute their X25519 key (so they could decrypt Introduce frames) → signature breaks.  **Validity window:** sender MUST check `valid_from <= now < valid_until` (boundary semantics: AT valid_from = valid; AT valid_until = expired — eliminates timing-window ambiguity for a censor racing to use the ad at the last microsecond).  Hard cap `MAX_VALIDITY_WINDOW_SECS = 30 days` — anti-DoS: censor capturing one ad shouldn't be able to keep using it indefinitely; matches "rotate keys monthly" hygiene.  **Domain separation:** canonical signed message domain-prefixed `"veil-rendezvous-ad:v1\0"` — signatures over rendezvous-ad CANNOT replay across other signed primitives (relay-directory, identity-document, signed-invite, update-manifest — each has its own domain).  **DHT key derivation:** `rendezvous_ad_dht_key(receiver_node_id) = BLAKE3("veil:v1:rendezvous-ad\0" \|\| node_id)` — domain-separated from `relay_directory_dht_key` so a rendezvous query CANNOT accidentally hit a relay-directory slot.  **Field caps:** issuer_pk ≤ 2048 chars (accommodates Falcon-512 + future PQ algos), signature ≤ 2048 bytes, total blob ≤ 8 KiB (fits in a single DHT-store frame with slack).  **Algos:** Ed25519 + Falcon-512 (existing `SignatureAlgorithm` enum).  **29 unit tests:** sign+decode+verify roundtrip (Ed25519 + Falcon-512); typical Ed25519 ad fits in < 1 KiB; SEVEN tamper modes — receiver_node_id, rendezvous_node_id, auth_cookie, receiver_x25519_pk (CRITICAL — censor swapping X25519 key would let them decrypt Introduce frames; signature MUST catch), valid_from, valid_until (CRITICAL — censor extending validity past intended rotation), signature itself, wrong issuer pubkey; bad magic / unsupported version / unknown sig_algo / truncated blob / oversized blob / trailing garbage all rejected at decode; inverted validity window rejected at sign; validity window > 30 days rejected at sign; oversized issuer_pk rejected at sign; validity-window boundaries (within passes; before valid_from = NotYetValid; AT or after valid_until = Expired; AT valid_from = valid — boundary edge); canonical message starts with a domain separator; DHT key deterministic + distinct from relay-directory key + changes with node_id; cross-protocol replay protection (ad signature does NOT verify against relay-directory canonical — different domain prefixes guarantee). **SLICES 2-4 SHIPPED (Phase 6.38, 2026-05-02): rendezvous-relay state machine + sender + receiver wiring**. **Wire types:** `IntroducePayload { receiver_node_id, auth_cookie, ciphertext (sealed to receiver_x25519_pk via X25519+AEAD) }`; `RegisterRendezvousPayload { receiver_x25519_pk, auth_cookie }`; `UnregisterRendezvousPayload { auth_cookie }`; `ForwardIntroducePayload { ciphertext }`. New `RelayChainMsg` variants: `RegisterRendezvous=1`, `UnregisterRendezvous=2`, `ForwardIntroduce=3` — distinct from `Hop=0` (cell-encoded onion); these are plain control frames over established OVL1 sessions. **Final-hop tag-byte routing** (wire-break vs Phase 6.37): every Final-hop onion payload now starts with a 1-byte kind tag — `0x01 APP_DELIVER` (direct, slice 4 path) or `0x02 INTRODUCE` (rendezvous-forward, this slice). Dispatcher branches on the tag and routes correspondingly. **Sealed-box crypto:** `encrypt_introduce(plaintext, recipient_x25519_pk)` / `decrypt_introduce(ciphertext, recipient_x25519_sk)` — fresh ephemeral keypair per call (forward secrecy), AAD-bound `INTRODUCE_DOMAIN = "veil-introduce-v1\0"`, ChaCha20-Poly1305 over BLAKE3-derived shared key. Distinct domain prefix from `wrap_for_hop` so cross-protocol replay is rejected. Wire shape `[32B eph_pk][12B nonce][AEAD ct + 16B tag]` = 60 B overhead. **`RendezvousRegistry`:** in-memory thread-safe `cookie → RendezvousSubscriber` map; bounded `DEFAULT_MAX_RENDEZVOUS_REGISTRATIONS = 10_000` (~800 KiB at default). Anti-DoS: cookie collision rejected (different peer can't claim someone else's cookie); imposter unregister silently ignored (only owning peer can drop). `drop_subscriber` removes ALL cookies for a peer — called by dispatcher when OVL1 session closes (TODO: wire from session-runner). **Dispatcher:** 4 new handlers — `handle_register_rendezvous` (parses + inserts into registry), `handle_unregister_rendezvous` (parses + removes), `handle_forward_introduce` (decrypts ciphertext with anonymity_x25519_sk → AppDeliverPayload → app_registry — receiver-side terminal path), `handle_final_introduce` (rendezvous-side: lookup cookie → `RendezvousSubscriber.peer_node_id` → forward `ForwardIntroducePayload` over their OVL1 session). All non-decode failures silent-drop (anti-leak). New `FrameDispatcher.rendezvous_registry: Option<Arc<RendezvousRegistry>>` field — auto-attached when `anonymity_x25519_sk` is set (operator opt-in via `[anonymity].relay_capable = true` bundles both relay + rendezvous capabilities for v1). **Runtime APIs:** `NodeRuntime::register_with_rendezvous(rendezvous_node_id, auth_cookie)` — receiver-side; sends `RelayChainMsg::RegisterRendezvous` over established OVL1 session, derives x25519_pk from local `anonymity_x25519_sk`. `NodeRuntime::send_via_rendezvous(ad: &RendezvousAd, target_app_id, target_endpoint_id, src_app_id, data, hop_count)` — sender-side; encrypts inner AppDeliverPayload to `ad.receiver_x25519_pk`, wraps in IntroducePayload + INTRODUCE tag-byte, builds onion circuit with rendezvous_node_id as the Final-hop, dispatches to first relay. Resolves rendezvous's anonymity x25519 pk via Epic 482.4 directory (`discover_relay_hops`). **Test coverage:** 20 new unit tests in `crates/veil-anonymity/src/rendezvous.rs` (Introduce/Register/Unregister/Forward roundtrip + oversize/truncated/wrong-size; encrypt+decrypt roundtrip + anti-correlation + wrong-key + tampered-ciphertext + truncated; registry register+lookup+unknown+collision+refresh+unregister-only-by-owner + drop_subscriber + capacity). 4 new dispatcher tests in `node/dispatcher/anonymity.rs` (`epic482_5_dispatch_register_inserts_into_registry`, `epic482_5_dispatch_register_no_registry_silent_drop`, `epic482_5_dispatch_forward_no_sk_silent_drop`, `epic482_5_dispatch_introduce_routes_via_cookie` — e2e sender encrypts → dispatcher decodes → cookie lookup succeeds). 49/49 rendezvous tests pass; 12/12 anonymity dispatcher tests pass; cluster validated 8/8 × 7/7 mesh with 0 decrypt failures after redeploy. **SLICE 5 SHIPPED (Phase 6.39, 2026-05-02): DHT republish loop maintenance task**. New `RendezvousPublisherEntry { rendezvous_node_id, auth_cookie, validity_window_secs }` value type + `DEFAULT_RENDEZVOUS_VALIDITY_SECS = 24h` + `rendezvous_ad_needs_refresh(valid_until, now, window)` half-life predicate. New field `NodeRuntime.rendezvous_publisher_entries: Arc<Mutex<Vec<RendezvousPublisherEntry>>>` — empty by default, only receivers using rendezvous-routed inbound delivery touch it. Two new public APIs: `register_rendezvous_publisher(rendezvous_node_id, auth_cookie, validity_window_secs)` (idempotent — replaces same-key entry rather than duplicating) and `unregister_rendezvous_publisher(rendezvous_node_id, auth_cookie)`. New maintenance helper `tick_publish_rendezvous_ads(entries, sk, identity, dht, logger)` — picks entry with the longest validity_window, signs `RendezvousAd`, `dht.store_local(rendezvous_ad_dht_key(receiver_node_id), bytes)`. Skips republish when existing ad still has > half-window remaining (no DHT churn for already-fresh entries). Wired into the periodic-cleanup tick alongside `tick_publish_relay_directory_entry` so receivers with registered entries automatically maintain freshness. **Tests:** 5 new in rendezvous.rs (refresh-threshold boundaries: within first half = no-trigger, AT or past half = trigger, AT or past expiry = trigger; default validity = 24h; entry value-type smoke); 3 new in maintenance.rs (empty-entries no-op, single-entry signs+stores with verify, still-fresh skip with byte-equal DHT contents). 54/54 rendezvous tests + 3/3 slice5 maintenance tests all green; cluster validated 8/8 × 7/7 mesh with 0 decrypt failures after redeploy. **Tight scope (deferred):** End-to-end sim integration test (4-node: sender + 2 relays + rendezvous + receiver). Drop_subscriber wire-up to session-close hook (currently registry retains stale entries until manual unregister or process restart). Multi-cookie republish (currently picks single entry with the longest window — multiple distinct cookies require separate sub-keys per the wire format, future API). |
| 482.6 | Latency-aware circuit construction — Vivaldi from Epic 479 ranks relay candidates; circuit-builder picks the min total latency among anonymity-valid triples. **Novelty vs Tor.** | node/anonymity/builder.rs (new) | 🔄 partial — **picker shipped, path-product latency optimisation deferred (needs pairwise RTT discovery)**. New `node/anonymity/circuit_builder.rs`. **Two strategies:** (a) `pick_circuit_hops_random(candidates, n, prng_byte)` — uniform-random Fisher-Yates partial shuffle, baseline. No info-leak about the sender's network position; is used when latency data is unavailable / untrusted. (b) `pick_circuit_hops_latency_aware(candidates, n, rtt_estimator)` — sort candidates by RTT ascending, pick top-N. **This is precisely the novelty vs Tor:** Tor selects circuit hops by relay bandwidth + uniform random; we select by user-experienced latency (composes with `VivaldiCoord` from Epic 479) — interactive-traffic UX significantly better than Tor's typical 200-800 ms per RT. **Anti-blind-pick safeguard:** candidate without an RTT estimate (Vivaldi did not converge / never probed) sorts to the END, is not let ahead of a known-low-latency candidate. Without this a fresh-discovered relay with no probe data would be picked before a known-good fast one — the user themselves would be horrified. **Anonymity-validity constraints:** both strategies enforce **distinct hops** by `node_id` (same hop twice reduces anonymity to N-1 hops while paying N-hop latency). Geographic / AS-diversity (e.g., "not three hops'from a single `/24` netblock" or one operator) — a separate slice that requires IP / AS metadata. **Tight scope (deferred to follow-ups):** path-product latency optimisation — with only single-sided RTT (sender → each candidate) we cannot optimize for inter-hop legs; pairwise RTT discovery — a separate slice. Bandwidth-weighted selection — candidates carry `advertised_bps`, this picker ignores'; future weighted strategy could bias toward higher-bandwidth for bulk transfers and lower-latency for interactive. Sticky / persistent circuits — this picker returns hop selections, not CircuitId-tagged sessions; stateful circuits in dispatcher integration (Epic 482.7). **12 unit tests** via injected closures (PRNG byte producer + RTT estimator). Random: returns None when pool < n; returns N distinct hops; dedups input pool by node_id; n == pool size returns all distinct; n == 0 edge case returns empty vec (satisfiable trivially). Latency-aware: returns None when pool < n; picks lowest RTT first; unknown RTT sorts LAST (anti-blind-pick); dedups input pool; stable order on RTT ties (reproducibility); all-unknown RTT returns in pool order (Vivaldi-not-converged-yet fallback). **End-to-end composition test:** `circuit_builder::pick_circuit_hops_latency_aware` → `packet::build_anonymous_cell` → 2-hop peel through real X25519 keys → recovered payload bit-equal. **Anonymity stack now FUNCTIONALLY COMPLETE on discovery → ranking → packet build path.** The next natural slice — Epic 482.7 dispatcher integration (`send_anonymous()` API + new frame family for onion cells in flight). |
| 482.7 | API: `send_anonymous(target, payload, anonymity_level)` on top of the existing dispatcher; default mode — direct (Epic 478 path); anonymous — opt-in per-message. | node/api/* | 🔄 partial — **slice 1: relay handler shipped (RECEIVE side); send_anonymous() API + per-app inbox for the final-hop payload as separate slice's**. **Reuse existing reserved frame family:** `FrameFamily::RelayChain = 10` was already reserved (Epic 246) but dispatch was disabled with a comment «pending ECDH rewrite — Epic 461.1». Epic 482's `cell` + `onion` + `circuit` + `packet` ARE that rewrite. Re-enabled handler in the new file `node/dispatcher/anonymity.rs`. **Per-frame flow:** (1) decode body, must be exactly `CELL_SIZE = 512 B` — otherwise Violation. (2) Validate `RelayChainMsg::try_from(msg_type)` — unknown msg_type → Violation. (3) **Format-checks first, crypto-key check last** — ordering matters for security: returning Violation on bad-format leaks "operator misconfig" (acceptable), but on missing-SK leaks "this node not a relay" to the sender' who probed' relay-capability — silent NoResponse in this case. (4) Peel the cell with the local `anonymity_x25519_sk` via `peel_anonymous_cell`. AEAD failure → silent NoResponse (cell wasn't intended for this relay's — leak' this can be probed by the sender). (5) On `Forward { next_hop, outbound_cell }` — send outbound_cell as fresh `RelayChain::Hop` frame via `session_tx_registry.send_to(next_hop, ...)`. **«Drop on next-hop-down» instead of «error back via the inbound path»** — error message would leak' the position of the failing hop to the sender's previous-hop observer', exact correlation attack which the cell+onion+packet machinery designed prevent. Sender retries' a fresh circuit when it notices a lack of response at the app-layer'. (6) On `Final { payload }` — v1 logs receipt + records metric; per-app inbox delivery — a separate slice. **Field plumbing:** new field `FrameDispatcher::anonymity_x25519_sk: Option<Arc<x25519_dalek::StaticSecret>>` (None when the operator did not opt in to relay). Runtime construction shares THE SAME Arc between the dispatcher and `NodeRuntime::anonymity_x25519_sk` (which the relay-directory publish task uses) — not two separate keys. Reload preserves the SK so already-published directory entries (which advertise this pubkey) keep working through reload. **6 unit tests:** dispatch handler via `make_test_dispatcher`: rejects non-cell-size body (Violation), rejects unknown msg_type (Violation), drops silently when SK missing (NoResponse — anti-leak), drops silently on AEAD failure (NoResponse — anti-leak), accepts final-hop payload (NoResponse, payload size logged but not bytes — sensitivity), test-fixture sanity check (1-hop cell roundtrip). **Tight scope (deferred to follow-ups):** `send_anonymous(target, payload, hop_count)` API in NodeRuntime — this is slice 2: composes `discover_relay_hops` (Epic 482.4) + `pick_circuit_hops_latency_aware` (Epic 482.6) + `build_anonymous_cell` (Epic 482.1) + `dispatcher.send_to(first_hop)`. Per-app inbox delivery for Final-hop payloads — slice 3: needs design decisions (which app gets unaddressed message? push to async channel? callback?). Stateful CircuitId — slice 4: enables persistent circuits (build once, send N messages over). Anti-loop / anti-amplification (TTL field in the circuit envelope) — slice 5. **Anonymity stack is now functionally CLOSED on the RECEIVE side** — relay-capable nodes ACTUALLY peel inbound onion cells and forwards correctly. **SEND-side helper SHIPPED slice 2:** new `node/anonymity/sender.rs` — pure-data composition layer `build_outbound_anonymous_cell(payload, candidates, rtt_estimator, target_node_id, target_x25519_pk, hop_count) -> (first_hop_node_id, cell_bytes)`. Caller builds runtime-glue (DHT lookup for candidates, Vivaldi snapshot for RTT, session-registry for the actual transmit); helper stays pure + unit-testable closure's. **`hop_count` semantics:** total hops INCLUDING target. 1 = direct, 2 = 1 relay + target (target hides sender's IP), 3 = 2 relays + target (Tor-standard, sender hidden from the target AND from everyone past the first hop). Higher counts trade payload budget for stronger unlinkability (per `max_payload_for_hops`). **Target pass'ed directly (not discovered)** for two reasons: (1) target may NOT be relay-capable — non-relay must be able to RECEIVE anonymous messages without publishing a directory entry; (2) splitting "lookup target" from "build cell" lets caller resolve target differently per deployment (DHT, mailbox, hardcoded peer, sovereign-identity exchange). **Anti-shortcut:** target automatically filtered out of the relay pool before the picker run's — cannot accidentally assemble circuit A → target → target (which leaks target identity to relay pool members). 9 sender unit tests: zero hops rejected, 1-hop direct (no relays needed, candidate pool can be empty), 2-hop (1 relay + target), 3-hop (Tor-standard), insufficient relays rejected with explicit need/have count, target excluded from relay pool, oversized payload rejected pre-build, hop_count > 5 rejected pre-picker, end-to-end through 3 hops'with RTT-based ordering verification. **Anonymity stack is now functionally CLOSED end-to-end** — receive side via `dispatch_relay_chain`, send side via `build_outbound_anonymous_cell`. **Runtime-API wrapper SHIPPED slice 3:** `NodeRuntime::send_anonymous(target_node_id, target_x25519_pk, payload, hop_count) -> Result<(), SenderError>`. **Pipeline:** (1) Snapshot candidate node_ids from the local routing table — canonical "peers we already know about", consistent with an anti-poison story (the anonymity layer must not consult sources that an attacker can poison faster than DHT). (2) Fetch each candidate's relay-directory entry from local DHT cache (`dht_get_local(relay_directory_dht_key(...))`). (3) `discover_relay_hops` filters by signature + freshness + anti-impersonation. (4) Build cell with an RTT estimator pulled from Vivaldi (`local_vivaldi.distance_estimate(peer_coord) * 1000` for ms; falls back to None if Vivaldi did not converge — picker handles by sorting unknown-RTT to last). (5) Cell hits the wire as `RelayChain::Hop` frame via `session_tx_registry.send_to(first_hop)`. **If the first hop is not reachable — silent drop**, app-layer learns from timeout, NOT from sync error (sync error leaked  first-hop reachability to a sender-side observer' **Anonymity stack is now fully end-to-end usable on the real wire format with real crypto** — sender calls `send_anonymous(...)`, message goes through the circuit, target's `dispatch_relay_chain` peel' and logs receipt. **SLICE 4 SHIPPED (Phase 6.37, 2026-05-02): per-app inbox for Final-hop payloads**. Design choice: Final-hop payload IS an `AppDeliverPayload` (sender wraps before onion-encrypting). Receiver Final-hop dispatcher decodes `AppDeliverPayload::decode(payload)` and routes via `app_registry.route_ipc_deliver(src_node_id, src_app_id, app_id, endpoint_id, data)` — the same `AppEndpointRegistry` as direct delivery. **Apps don't need to know that the message arrived via onion** — it surfaces into their existing `IncomingMessage` channel. **`NodeRuntime::send_anonymous` signature extended:** `(target_node_id, target_x25519_pk, target_app_id, target_endpoint_id, src_app_id, data, hop_count)`. `src_node_id` always wired as `[0u8; 32]` — anonymity layer guarantees the receiver does NOT learn the sender's node identity (replies require Epic 482.5 rendezvous). `src_app_id` carries sender's app handle (caller passes `[0u8; 32]` for "anonymous app"). **Three new dispatcher tests** beyond the existing 6: `epic482_7_dispatch_delivers_final_hop_payload_to_app` (e2e via a test fixture: build cell with AppDeliverPayload → dispatcher peels → app's mpsc receiver gets `AppMessage::Deliver` with anonymity-zeroed src_node_id), `epic482_7_dispatch_drops_silently_on_malformed_app_deliver` (garbage Final payload → silent drop, not Violation), `epic482_7_dispatch_drops_silently_on_unbound_endpoint` (valid AppDeliverPayload but no app bound → silent drop matching direct-delivery semantics). 8/8 anonymity-dispatcher tests pass. End-to-end sim integration test (Epic 482.7 slice 5) — a separate follow-up; the wrapper itself composes well-tested components (9 sender tests + 8 dispatcher tests + all primitives tested in isolation'), but e2e via sim::SimNetwork — explicit test that the full pipeline operates correctly under a realistic 4-node topology (sender + 2 relays + target). |

**Acceptance:** an anonymous message from a sender (Russia) to a receiver (Kazakhstan) passes through 3 relays (3 different countries), receiver cannot determine the sender-IP; observer at any single hop cannot determine the full path; latency overhead < 4× direct.

---

## Epic 483 — Mobile / battery / NAT — closed (6/7 sub-tasks shipped + 2 deferred slices opt-in 2026-05-06)

**Goal:** A node runs on a phone under realistic conditions: battery, OS sleep, mobile network, CGN-NAT, IPv6 transitions.

**Closure summary (2026-05-06):** 6 of 7 sub-tasks shipped; 483.2 (push-notification wake) deferred to the Epic 489 Flutter scope (requires platform-glue + FCM/APNs backend, out of veilcore); 483.5 deferred slices (outbound batching + maintenance-tick throttling) shipped as **opt-in via `[mobile]` config** — default OFF on all profiles incl. mobile, operator opts in after on-device measurement confirms 5%/h+ drain remains.

| # | Task | Files | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 483.1 | Background-mode session preservation — the app in the background → keepalive once per 30 minutes (instead of 30s) + session-resume on foreground. | node/session/runner.rs | ✅ done — **GUI wrapper / mobile app calls \`AdminCommand::SetMobileBackgroundMode { enabled: true }\` from onPause hook → all per-session keepalive intervals immediately stretch by configured multiplier (mobile profile default = 60×, so 30s → 30 min); onResume → \`enabled: false\` → next recomputation tick (within 60s) restores foreground cadence**. Session survives OS suspension instead of timing out → user returns to the app in a working state, not "reconnecting...". **Wire format:** new `mobile.background_keepalive_multiplier: u32` config field (default 1 = feature off; mobile profile = 60), new `MobileConfig::background_keepalive_factor(background_mode: bool) -> u32` helper (returns 1 when off OR multiplier when on, clamped to `MAX_BACKGROUND_KEEPALIVE_MULTIPLIER = 120`). New runtime `AtomicBool mobile_background_mode` (default false) + admin command `SetMobileBackgroundMode { enabled }` returning `Ack { message }`. **Process-global signals in `node/session/runner.rs`** (`MOBILE_BACKGROUND_MODE: AtomicBool` + `MOBILE_BACKGROUND_KEEPALIVE_MULTIPLIER: AtomicU32` + `current_mobile_background_keepalive_factor()` accessor) instead of threading new fields via 22+ SessionRunner construction sites — single-runtime-per-process invariant makes globals safe; Drop-guard pattern in tests resets globals. **Composes multiplicatively with battery scaling (Epic 220):** keepalive recalculation in the session runner runs every 60s and recomputes `keepalive_interval = base × battery_scale × bg_factor` — backgrounded + low-battery sees both factors, can give 30s → 30s × 4 (battery) × 60 (background) = 2 hours, but clamped at MAX_BACKGROUND_KEEPALIVE_MULTIPLIER × MAX_LOW_BATTERY_MULTIPLIER prevents stretching past `idle_timeout` (default 24 h). Background factor change ALSO triggers immediate reschedule (sentinel value 0 ≠ any valid factor ≥ 1 forces first-tick recalc). **Hard cap = 120** (60s base × 120 = 2 hours) — safely under any reasonable `session.idle_timeout_secs` so misconfig OR malicious admin command can't push keepalive past idle_timeout (which would close the session anyway, defeating the purpose). **Runtime startup + reload prime the global multiplier** from `config.mobile.background_keepalive_multiplier` so even nodes that never get a SetMobileBackgroundMode call have the right scale ready when GUI eventually toggles. **9 new unit tests** (5 in session/runner.rs guarding global signals: factor=1 when mode off; factor=multiplier when on; clamping at MAX; floor=1 for the multiplier=0; toggle off→on returns to scaling — Drop-guard restores defaults so tests don't poison each other; 4 in cfg/model.rs guarding the helper: same shape for config-side resolution). The mobile profile regression test was extended to lock in `background_keepalive_multiplier = 60` value. **Slice 2 (CLI surface):** new `veil-cli mobile background-mode <on|off>` CLI command + `cmd/mobile_cmd.rs` handler + `Mobile(MobileArgs)` variant in the Command enum. Sends `AdminCommand::SetMobileBackgroundMode` via admin socket (requires running node — toggles per-process runtime state). Operator-friendly output echoes daemon's Ack + adds "what just changed" implication line ("keepalive cadence stretched, sessions will survive OS suspension" on enable; "foreground keepalive cadence restored at the next recomputation tick (≤ 60s)" on disable). **`OnOff` enum** (clap value_enum) instead of `bool` field — strict canonical forms (`on` / `off`, case-insensitive) so a typo doesn't silently parse as off-by-default; clap rejects `yes` / `1` / `enable` with "[possible values: on, off]" hint. Useful for: mobile app integrators testing integration without writing IPC code; cron-based cellular saving (flip background mode at night via crontab); headless deployments where daemon acts as mobile gateway (admin manually toggles via SSH). 4 unit tests cover `OnOff::as_bool` round-trip + clap value_enum parser sanity + case-insensitivity + strict-rejection of non-canonical forms (`yes` / `1` / `enable`). **Tight scope (deferred):** session-resume on the foreground transition (Epic 215 SESSION_TICKET already handles this protocol-level; current slice doesn't auto-trigger it on background→foreground edge — operator workflow is "background → server saves session via long keepalive → foreground → existing session still alive, no resume needed"). |
| 483.2 | Push-notification-driven wake — FCM/APNs delivers «you have a message» → the app wakes → fetch. Optional integration (requires a backend). | platform-glue (out of veilcore) | 📦 backlog — out-of-veilcore scope, moves into Epic 489 Flutter app territory when the backend service for push' appears. |
| 483.3 | NAT traversal hardening — STUN-style hole-punching via an online peer as coordinator. | node/nat/* (existing) + veil-proto/control + dispatcher/control + runtime + sim | ✅ done — **all four slices shipped: signaling (1) + auto-coordinator helper (1.5) + candidate-promotion fallback (2) + outbound-dial-failure auto-trigger (3)**.  Wire format `NatProbeRequestPayload.target_node_id` + `NatProbeReplyPayload.final_target_node_id` for relay forwarding; runtime API `NodeRuntime::attempt_nat_traversal_via` + `try_nat_traversal` (auto-pick coordinator) + `try_nat_traversal_promote_uris` (URI promotion via responder candidates) + `nat_fallback_dial` (auto-trigger on outbound failure with active session).  Sim tests cover each slice end-to-end.  **Tight scope (NOT shipped):** actual UDP hole-punching (`veil-nat::NatPuncher::punch`) — sessions are TCP-based, dial-target discovery via signaling + re-dial TCP is the practical mechanism.  **Known sim-infra issue:** four sim tests fail on master due to an unrelated `identity_document.bin` sharing regression (Epic 477.2 + sim's flat `/tmp/` veil-dir layout); production code paths verified via git history + manual review. |
| 483.4 | IPv4 → IPv6 transition — handle the case where a node switches between networks (WiFi → mobile data); session-resume via identity, not via the transport-tuple. | node/session/runner.rs + node/outbound_connector.rs + node/mobile_sink.rs + node/runtime/mod.rs | ✅ done — **WiFi ↔ cellular flip recovery latency drops from ~30-90 s to ~100 ms (new TCP RT + SESSION_TICKET fast-resume RT)**.  `force_reconnect_notify: Arc<Notify>` shared by `NodeRuntime` + `NodeServices`: `MobileEventForwarder::network_changed` walks session_tx_registry, unregisters every active peer, then `notify_waiters()`.  Outbound-connector loops `select!` on the notify alongside pre-check sleep so they short-circuit and re-handshakes immediately.  Sim test `epic483_4_network_change_triggers_fast_reconnect` (~100 ms vs would-be 30 s without notify wakeup). |
| 483.5 | Battery-aware scheduling — on a mobile device: reduce probe frequency, batch outbound frames, skip background republish. | node/runtime/maintenance.rs + node/session/runner.rs + cfg/model.rs | ✅ done **(3 slices: route-probe + maintenance-tick throttle + outbound batch — last two opt-in, default OFF)** — **route-probe throttling slice (Phase 6.45):** new `cfg::MobileConfig` (top-level `[mobile]` section, default off — desktop / server / Pi-on-AC deployments see zero behaviour change unless operator opts in): `low_battery_threshold_pct: Option<u8>` + `low_battery_multiplier: u32` (default 4, capped at `MAX_LOW_BATTERY_MULTIPLIER = 16` so a misconfig "1 probe per hour" footgun is impossible; floor at 1 for `multiplier = 0` typo).  `MobileConfig::battery_multiplier(battery_pct)` returns 1 when threshold unset OR battery > threshold OR battery == 0 (sentinel for "AC / unknown" — never throttle on this signal).  Wired into `spawn_route_probe_task_with` (Epic 143 ROUTE_PROBE adaptive scheduler): once per outer 1-s tick, sample `local_battery_level()` (single file read on Linux `/sys/class/power_supply/BAT*/capacity`, no-op elsewhere), compute multiplier, then stretch each per-peer `adaptive_probe_interval` result via `base.saturating_mul(battery_mult)` BEFORE the existing ±10% jitter.  Throttle reacts to charger plug-in within ~1 s.  6 unit tests cover all multiplier paths.  **Maintenance-tick throttling slice (2026-05-06, opt-in):** new `low_battery_throttle_maintenance: bool` field in `MobileConfig` (default false, mobile profile also false — operator must explicitly opt in after on-device measurement).  When `true` AND battery is at-or-below threshold AND the multiplier > 1, the maintenance tick skips eviction + diagnostic phases (`tick_evict_expired_primary_stores`, `tick_evict_secondary_caches`, `tick_evict_transport_cache`, `tick_refresh_runtime_summary`, `tick_refresh_adaptive_params`, `dht.prune_orphan_announcements`) on `(multiplier-1)/multiplier` of ticks via `MobileConfig::skip_throttleable_maintenance(battery, tick_index)` decision helper.  Deadline-driven phases (Epic 475 announcement re-mint, Epic 477.4 sovereign re-issue, Epic 482.4 relay-directory publish, Epic 482.5 rendezvous re-sign) AND memory-budget pressure eviction (`tick_account_memory_and_evict_over_budget`) AND congestion accounting AND heartbeat ALWAYS run — throttling them would break correctness OR trigger false-positive watchdog kicks.  Mobile config now flows into `spawn_maintenance_tick(cleanup_interval, e2e_key_ttl, mobile_cfg: MobileConfig)` (was 2-arg).  **Outbound-frame batching slice (2026-05-06, opt-in):** new `outbound_batch_window_ms: Option<u32>` field in `MobileConfig` (default None, mobile profile also None).  When BOTH `outbound_batch_window_ms` AND `low_battery_threshold_pct` configured AND battery is low, the session runner defers the priority-queue drain pass while the queue head priority is BULK or BACKGROUND, up to `window_ms` since the previous flush.  REALTIME / INTERACTIVE frames bypass the gate — peek returns them and `coalesce_eligible` evaluates false → drain proceeds immediately.  Coalesce deadline merged into the existing `sleep_until` budget so the runner emerges from `await_next_input` exactly when the window expires (bounded-latency contract preserved).  Process-global atomics `MOBILE_LOW_BATTERY_THRESHOLD_PCT: AtomicU8` (sentinel 255 = disabled) + `MOBILE_OUTBOUND_BATCH_WINDOW_MS: AtomicU32` (0 = disabled), primed at startup + reload from `config.mobile`, follow established pattern (`MOBILE_BACKGROUND_KEEPALIVE_MULTIPLIER`).  `current_outbound_batch_window(battery_pct: u8) -> Option<Duration>` returns `Some(window)` only when ALL gates pass (window != 0, threshold != DISABLED, battery non-zero AND ≤ threshold), otherwise `None` ⇒ fast path in the session runner.  New `PriorityQueue::peek_priority() -> Option<u8>` inspect-without-advance helper.  Hard cap `MAX_OUTBOUND_BATCH_WINDOW_MS = 1000` defends against "10 s coalesce" misconfig that would stall ROUTE_PROBE liveness.  Total: 22 cfg::model tests + 4 priority_queue tests + 8 session::runner global-signal tests = 34 new unit tests.  **Why opt-in for both deferred slices:** real-device on-Android battery measurement is the gate — landing the primitives now means operator can flip flags incrementally in response to concrete drain numbers, instead of shipping behaviour change without validation.  Re-open trigger originally tracked in the TASKS.md Deferred large-scope backlog. |
| 483.6 | Bandwidth shaping for budget connections — config `max_kbps` cap, sender is throttled without exceeding it. | node/abuse/per_peer_limiter.rs (existing) + cmd/handlers.rs | ✅ done — **mobile profile now automatically sets cellular-friendly bandwidth caps + 24h update-check cadence**.  Existing infrastructure already wired (`BandwidthGate` + `NodeCapacityConfig.max_inbound/outbound_bandwidth_kbps`); mobile profile defaults: inbound 2 Mbit/s + outbound 500 kbps + update interval 24h.  Two new tests lock in actual values + extended cross-section guard. |
| 483.6b | Per-peer bandwidth throttling — `with_byte_rate(bytes_per_sec, burst_bytes)` from `node/abuse/per_peer_limiter.rs`, mobile profile activated. | cfg/model.rs + cfg/validate/structural.rs + cmd/handlers.rs + node/runtime/mod.rs | ✅ done — **mobile profile now sets a 64 KB/s per-peer cap; closes single-peer-flood defence layer**.  New `abuse.per_peer_bytes_per_sec: Option<u64>` (None = enforcement off; mobile profile = 65536) + `abuse.per_peer_byte_burst: Option<u64>` (default 4× rate).  Validation rule: must be >= 1024 bytes/sec when set.  Drop counters exposed on the admin Bandwidth wire format: `per_peer_byte_cap_bytes_per_sec` + `per_peer_bytes_allowed_total` + `per_peer_bytes_dropped_total` (saturating add, no wrap on long uptime).  CLI renderer adds `per-peer: cap=X passed=Y dropped=Z (P%)` row when enabled.  12 new unit tests (8 + 4). |

**Acceptance:** a phone in the background for 8 hours on 4G keeps connectivity, battery drain < 5%/hour; reconnection after a WiFi change → 4G network < 3s to a restored session.  483.4 hits this directly (~100ms reconnect); 5%/h target gates the deferred-slice opt-ins via Epic 489 Flutter measurement.

## Epic 484 — Operational deployment

**Goal:** Distribute an easy-to-install binary, update it without losing identity, the operator sees «why am I not connected» in the UI.

| # | Task | Files | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 484.1 | Reproducible binary builds — single-file a binary for linux-x86_64, linux-arm64, macos-arm64, windows-x86_64, android-aarch64. SHA256 + signature. | .github/workflows/* (new) + scripts/build-release.sh | ✅ done — Phase 6.40 (2026-05-02). Three pieces: (1) CLI `veil-cli update sign-manifest` (Ed25519/Falcon-512 sign of SHA-256-bound `UpdateManifest`); (2) `scripts/build-release.sh` deterministic per-target build (SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH + RUSTFLAGS path-prefix-strip + CARGO_INCREMENTAL=0 + cargo --locked); (3) `.github/workflows/release.yml` workflow_dispatch matrix-build for 4 platforms (linux-x86_64/aarch64, macos-arm64, windows-x86_64) with per-target signing on the Linux runner and artifact upload to a GitHub Release. 3 unit tests (verifiable-blob roundtrip, deterministic-with-fixed-inputs, missing-binary error path). Cluster validated 8/8 × 7/7 mesh after redeploy. **Tight scope (deferred):** Android NDK target (489.2 handles veil-ffi `.so`); cosign / sigstore transparency log; macOS `.app` bundle / Apple notarization; Linux `.deb` / `.rpm` packages. |
| 484.3 | Update mechanism — `veil update` verifies the signed manifest on multiple endpoints (anti-takedown), downloads the new version, restart in-place. Identity is not lost. | cmd/update.rs (new) | ✅ done — slices 1-13 SHIPPED; mechanism end-to-end functional on ALL platforms (POSIX + Windows). Slices 1-12 (manifest primitive + closure-failover + HTTPS bridge + check_for_update orchestrator + InstalledVersionStore + UpdateConfig + UpdateChecker + periodic check task + runtime startup wiring + CLI check + apply primitive + CLI apply); Slice 13 Windows .old-shuffle wrapper (NTFS rename-of-open-file before staging→install rename, `cleanup_stale_update_artifacts` startup-time cleanup of leftover .update-old / .update-tmp). 89 Epic 484.3 tests passing total. **No deferred sub-slices.** |
| 484.5 | Diagnostics IPC — structured events for GUI wrappers (mobile app, desktop tray) | node/admin.rs (existing IPC) | ✅ done — three slices shipped: `UpdateStatus` snapshot (Slice 1), `MobileStatus` snapshot (Slice 2), push event stream (Slice 3 — Phase 6.34, 2026-05-02). Slice 3: `LocalAppMsg::Event = 36` wire frame + `EventBus` (broadcast capacity 256) + `IpcServer::with_event_bus` + `VeilClient::events()`. Three event kinds (`SESSIONS_CHANGED=0`, `MOBILE_TIER_CHANGED=1`, `IDENTITY_ROTATED=2` — last one publisher deferred to Epic 462 master-rotation flow). Phase 6.35 SESSIONS_CHANGED runtime publisher + FFI surface (`veil_set_event_handler` + `VEIL_EVENT_*` kinds). 7 proto roundtrip tests + 4 SDK integration tests + unit/FFI tests. Cluster validated 8/8 × 7/7 mesh. |
| 484.6 | Centralized telemetry endpoint (opt-in, anonymized) — for understanding aggregated network health without revealing per-user data. | node/telemetry/* (new) | ❌ dropped (architecturally incompatible with an anti-censorship goal) — a centralized endpoint = single point a censor can target; URL in the binary becomes a chokepoint for blocking + privacy concern (anonymized data reveals a population estimate, which is itself useful intel for an attacker). A better-shaped alternative is tracked separately as a future "decentralized network observability" mini-epic (DHT-based aggregate signed records — every node publishes "alive at version X" record, operator manually crawls + aggregates; gives 80% of value — rollout monitoring + churn baseline — without single point of failure). Operator decision 2026-05-05. |

**Acceptance:** end-user downloaded, launched, after 30 seconds sees «connected, 5 peers»; an update to a new version does not break identity; `veil diag` is understandable to a non-technical user. ✅ Met across 484.1, 484.3, 484.5; 484.6 dropped.
