## Epic 485 — Adversary validation

**Goal:** Simulate concrete attacks, measure cost-to-attack vs cost-to-defend. Confirm threat-model assumptions with numbers, not intuition.

| # | Task | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 485.1 | Sybil sim: measure the fraction of eclipse'd honest-targets | 🔄 partial closure — **3 sub-scenarios shipped** mapping the sybil-resistance curve at three operating points: (a) FLOOR (`epic485_1_sybil_flood_does_not_eclipse_routing_table`, H=5 > S=3, observed ~37.5 %); (b) SPEC (`epic485_1b_eclipse_rate_under_low_sybil_fraction_meets_spec_bound`, H=8 >> S=2, max-across-targets < 30 % per Epic 485 acceptance bar, observed ~22 %); (c) STRESS (`epic485_1c_equal_strength_sybil_attack_degrades_gracefully`, H == S == 5, < 60 % bound). 11 assertion points across three topologies, runtime ~29 s, no `slow-sim-tests` gating. **Deferred extensions parked in the Deferred large-scope backlog (2026-05-06):** ID-grinding sybils (need synthetic node_id generation primitive), bucket-pollution sybils (need crafted FIND_NODE-reply injection API), 24h-equivalent churn-aware variant (sim infra is real-time only). All three target distinct bucket-eviction-policy regressions; current 3 scenarios cover the most actionable failure modes (mass-arrival eclipse, recent-dialer bias, single-target dominance). |
| 485.2 | Enumeration cost amplification (PoW gate) | ✅ done — empirical PoW solve cost via `veil_crypto::search_nonce` bench: 12-bit 262 ms / 14-bit 1.56 s / 16-bit 4.96 s, scaling 4× per +2 bits as theory predicts.  Production 24-bit `DEFAULT_POW_DIFFICULTY` extrapolates to ~21 min single-thread → amplification = 21 × 60 × 1000 ms / 1 ms ≈ **1.3 × 10⁶×** vs no-PoW probe baseline.  Even with a 16-core attacker, **80 000× still exceeds the ≥100× spec by 800×**.  Bench-not-sim choice rationale: question is pure CPU-cost ratio, criterion gives tighter intervals than 10-node sim with I/O noise. |
| 485.3 | Traffic correlation on the anonymity layer (entry+exit observer) | ✅ done — **3 unit tests in `crates/veil-anonymity/src/packet.rs::tests::epic485_3_*`**.  (1) Size-distribution collapse: every entry-side cell = 512 B regardless of payload (50 varied 1..234 B plaintexts); H(sizes) = 0 → size-based observer correlation impossible.  (2) Entry/exit byte-histogram |Pearson correlation| < 0.10 across 50 biased-ASCII plaintexts; self-correlation negative control returns 1.0 proving the function works.  (3) Brute-force prefix-matching attack: 50×50 = 2500 attempts, success rate ≪ 10 % (AEAD makes ciphertext uniformly random).  All three negative controls catch regressions where encryption/padding might silently fail. |
| 485.4 | Benchmark suite (PoW + sig-verify + cache hit) | ✅ done — `veilcore/benches/adversary_validation.rs` (criterion) c four groups.  **PoW** (3 difficulties): 12/14/16-bit 262 ms/1.56 s/4.96 s.  **Sig** (Ed25519 + Falcon-512 sign + verify): Ed25519 sign 125 µs / verify 64 µs; Falcon sign 11.7 ms / verify 132 µs.  **Relay-directory verify**: decode 403 ns / full verify 92 µs.  **DHT cache**: `lookup_cache_get_hit` 1.82 µs (~1000× speedup vs full iterative-find), `lookup_cache_get_miss` 406 ns, `lookup_cache_insert` 25.8 µs (cap=1024 LRU eviction scan; informs whether to migrate to the `lru` crate later).  Operator capacity-planning numbers all in one place. |
| 485.5 | DPI-fingerprint test — n-gram analysis | ✅ done — **3 unit tests in `veilcore/src/node/session/handshake.rs::tests::epic485_5_*`** + `ReadTee` infra capturing real OVL1 handshake bytes from both initiator and responder sides.  (1) Marker-absence: captured bytes do NOT match Tor cell signature, OpenVPN opcode 0x21..0x29 with non-OVL1 follow-up, WireGuard noise prologue (0x01 + 3 zero bytes).  (2) Shannon entropy ≥ 7.5 bits/byte on post-handshake AEAD-encrypted segment (cutoff = 800 B to skip plaintext HELLO/IDENTITY/CAPS framing); negative-control all-zeroes blob fails the threshold.  (3) Chi-square uniformity test (df=255, α=0.001 critical ~330): chi² < 350; biased-modulo control fails.  Validates the OVL1 PROTOCOL layer is itself DPI-noise-like; the orthogonal TLS ClientHello fingerprint (Epic 488 / Chrome JA3 mimicry) lives at the transport layer in `veil-transport/tls_boring` and remains validated separately.  Note: original spec mentioned distance-metrics to Tor / OpenVPN / WireGuard reference fingerprints which require curated pcap fixtures we don't have; the marker-absence test approach is the practical equivalent — veil's protocol DOES NOT emit any of those signatures. |
| 485.6 | Field testnet — 10-30 nodes on a real adversarial network | ⏸ skip per operator decision (2026-05-06) — surfaced via real reports after launch.  Not code-side work: requires legal review + operator recruiting in hostile-DPI countries + anonymized log-collection infra + privacy review.  Re-open trigger: post-launch field reports identify systematic blocking patterns not covered by simulated tests. |

**Acceptance:** all sim-scenarios green → ready for a pre-prod release with a known cost-to-attack for each vector.  **✅ Met for all adversarial classes within software-side validation**: Sybil (3 sub-scenarios + 11 assertion points), enumeration cost (10⁶× amplification empirically), traffic correlation (< 10 % bound met), DPI fingerprint (entropy ≥ 7.5 + chi² < 350 + zero competitor markers), capacity-planning bench (4 groups across PoW + sig + relay-dir + DHT cache).

---

## Epic 486 — Post-quantum readiness — closed (GA: hybrid + standalone Falcon-512 + MigrationCert chain-walk, all stand-verified)

**Goal.** Long-term durability against a quantum adversary: shipped hybrid Ed25519+Falcon-512 master keys, ML-KEM-768 hybrid session kex, MigrationCert path for in-place algorithm rotation without losing `node_id` / @name / contact-graph state.

**Closure summary (Phase 6.49 → 6.51, ~3 weeks of slices, 13 commits `fc60f9d…6c78d9b`):** four sub-tasks all shipped with full operator UX, runtime path in the production resolver, sim integration tests, AND cross-host end-to-end verification on the 8-host VPS testnet.

| # | Task | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 486.1 | Re-enable + finish ML-KEM (Kyber) PQ-encapsulation. Hybrid mode: X25519 + ML-KEM 768. | ✅ done — **Slice 3** (commit `28d3050`).  `cap_flags::SUPPORTS_HYBRID_KEX = 1<<3` + new `SessionMsg::HybridKexCt = 19` frame + `HybridKexCtPayload { mlkem_ct }` (length-prefixed, 4 KiB cap).  Raw KEM helpers `mlkem_encapsulate_raw` / `mlkem_decapsulate_raw` in `veil-crypto::x3dh` (return bare `(ct, ss)` without X3DH HKDF binding so the session-layer kex can mix the secret with X25519 directly).  `SovereignHandshakeCtx { local_mlkem_dk_seed, peer_mlkem_ek }` plumbing in `veilcore/src/node/session/handshake.rs`.  Negotiation logic: when both peers advertised `SUPPORTS_HYBRID_KEX` AND peer's IDENTITY frame carried `mlkem_pubkey`, smaller-node_id side encapsulates → writes frame; larger-node_id side reads → decapsulates with the local DK seed; both replace classical `session_keys` via `derive_hybrid_session_keys` before SESSION_CONFIRM.  No silent fallback (defends against active downgrade-MITM).  Bench `veilcore/benches/hybrid_kex.rs` (criterion): classical 1.3 µs, hybrid sender ~185 µs, hybrid receiver ~198 µs.  **Phase 6.51 perf follow-up** (commit `6c78d9b`): isolated parse cost from crypto cost — DK seed expansion = ~80 µs of the 171 µs raw decap, EK validation = ~8.6 µs of 82 µs encap.  Shipped `PreparedEncapsulator::from_bytes` / `PreparedDecapsulator::from_seed` API in `x3dh.rs` for callers that re-key against the same peer (cached decap is 1.8× faster: 96 µs vs 171 µs).  Not wired into handshake.rs (current rekey is X25519-only); ships ready for a future re-key-on-every-N-frames Epic.  5 wire tests + 3 prepared-types tests (bit-exact match with raw on a 3-round encap-decap cycle, length validation for both types). |
| 486.2 | Falcon-512 for master-keys (long-term identity) — hybrid + standalone. | ✅ done — **Slice 1 + extension** (commits `46fdc06`, `28d3050`, `5fb22bd`, `853a18c`).  `SignatureAlgorithm::Ed25519Falcon512Hybrid` (wire byte 3) + `Falcon512` standalone (wire byte 2) — both path full lifecycle: `create_identity`, `restore_identity`, sign + verify, persist + reload.  Hybrid: Ed25519 master half derived from BIP-39 `master_seed` (paper-backup recoverable), Falcon-512 half generated from `OsRng` (NOT recoverable — persisted to a `master_falcon.bin` framed `OFAM` bundle, mode 0o600).  Standalone Falcon-512: rejected at create_identity until **Phase 6.51** unblock (commit `853a18c`); now allowed under explicit `--accept-no-recovery` gate with a loud 7-line warning block, BIP-39 phrase suppression (it's not a recovery medium for Falcon), 3-of-3 backup recommendation in `docs/identity-hybrid-backup.md` Appendix A.  CLI surface: `veil-cli identity create --algo {ed25519|hybrid|falcon512}`, `veil-cli identity restore --algo ... --master-falcon-file <bundle>`, `veil-cli identity show` recognises master_algo byte 3 → `ed25519+falcon512`.  master_falcon.bin format: framed `OFAM` magic + version + sk_len + sk + pk_len + pk (SK+PK bundled because pqcrypto-falcon's SecretKey doesn't expose `.public_key()`).  10 lib tests (hybrid + standalone create/restore roundtrip, missing-bundle rejection, security-non-downgrade matrix) + 9 CLI tests (algo parsing, --accept-no-recovery gate, BIP-39 suppression, restore with/without a bundle, hybrid-PK-mismatch safety net).  Operator runbook `docs/identity-hybrid-backup.md` (~9 KB) with recovery semantics for both paths. |
| 486.3 | Migration path for existing identities (classical → hybrid → PQ) without losing `node_id` / multi-device-state. | ✅ done — **Slice 2 + production runtime + multi-hop verification** (commits `4f5f4f4`, `28d3050`, `5fb22bd`, `6c78d9b`).  `crates/veil-identity/src/migration.rs` ships `MigrationCert { old_master_algo, old_node_id, new_node_id, new_master_algo, new_master_pubkey, issued_at, valid_until, signature }` + binary wire format + `sign_migration_cert` + `verify_migration_cert` + `migration_cert_dht_key`.  Security invariants enforced at sign time AND verify time: 30-day max validity window, security non-downgrade (`security_tier` ranks Ed25519=1 < Falcon512=2 < Hybrid=3), domain-separated signed message `veil.migration_cert.v1`, structural binding `new_node_id == BLAKE3(new_master_pubkey)`.  Resolver-level chain-walk in `crates/veil-identity/src/resolver.rs::NameResolver`: `IdentityLookup::fetch_migration_cert` + `fetch_migration_cert_replicated` (default `Ok(None)` for backward compat); `resolve` walks chains to `MAX_MIGRATION_CHAIN_DEPTH = 4` hops with cycle detection; replica-divergence handled by picking highest-`security_tier` valid cert (ties broken by `issued_at` desc); forged / expired / decode-failure replicas silently dropped.  `publish::publish_migration_cert` write-side helper.  **Production runtime** (commit `5fb22bd`): `Runtime::resolve_identity_verified` extracted into per-hop `resolve_one_identity_doc` + chain-walk loop; `fetch_best_migration_cert_for` quorum-fetches to `RESOLVE_MAX_REPLICAS` cert candidates via `dht_get_replicated`, decode-and-verify each against the local-DHT-cached current master pubkey, picks the best.  7 sign/verify tests + 5 resolver chain tests (one-hop follow, expired ignored, cycle detection, forged-cert dropped, missing-target → IdentityNotFound) + 1 sim integration test (`epic486_3_runtime_chain_walk_follows_and_detects_cycle`) covering baseline / chain-follow / cycle on the real runtime via `dht_put_local` injection.  **Cross-host stand verification** (commit `8c3a108` depth=1 + `6c78d9b` depth=3): on 8-host VPS testnet, mint 4 identities A→B→C→D with published docs + 3 published certs; from a different host call `node resolve-identity A --timeout-ms 8000` → resolver fetches each doc + cert through Kademlia quorum, verifies algo-appropriate signature per hop (Ed25519 for A's master sig, hybrid composite for B/C/D), enforces non-downgrade ladder (ed25519 → hybrid → hybrid → hybrid), and returns D.  Tested all four query depths (0/1/2/3 hops) — all converge to D the same way. |
| 486.4 | Bandwidth/CPU benchmarks — Falcon sigs ~666B (~10× Ed25519), need to understand the impact on budget hardware. AND rotation CLI tooling. | ✅ done — **Bench piece** earlier closed via Epic 485.4 (`veilcore/benches/adversary_validation.rs` `sig` group: Ed25519 sign 125 µs / verify 64 µs; Falcon sign 11.7 ms / verify 132 µs; hybrid verify ~196 µs ≈ ×3 Ed25519-only).  **Rotation CLI piece shipped Phase 6.51** (commits `67ee81c`, `21bfc37`, `8c3a108`).  `veil-cli identity migrate --from <old-veil-dir> --to <new-veil-dir> [--from-phrase-file ...] [--from-master-falcon-file ...] [--cert-out ...] [--valid-for-secs N] [--publish-immediately] [--admin-socket ...]` — algo-aware, mirrors the identical pk_b64 / sk_b64 framing used by create_identity / restore_identity per master_algo, reuses `migration::sign_migration_cert` (which enforces non-downgrade + window cap + `issued < valid_until`).  Pubkey-mismatch safety net: composes the OLD master_pubkey from supplied phrase/bundle, compares against `<--from>/identity_document.bin`'s master_pubkey — fails fast if operator pointed `--from` at the wrong identity dir.  `--publish-immediately` invokes `AdminCommand::DhtPublishReplicated` (Epic 492 path — local store + fan-out to K closest live peers); cert ALWAYS lands to disk first, so failure modes (admin socket missing, daemon connection reset, peer publish timeout) leave the cert recoverable for manual `node dht put` retry.  Generic `veil-cli node dht publish-replicated <KEY> {--value <hex> | --value-file <path>}` CLI verb (commit `8c3a108`) exposes the replicated-DHT-put admin verb beyond the specialised `bootstrap publish` / `identity migrate` callers.  4 migrate CLI tests (Ed25519 → hybrid happy path with verify-against-OLD-master, security-downgrade rejected, same-node_id rejected, unprovisioned-target rejected) + 1 admin-socket test (graceful fallback when daemon missing — cert STILL persisted to disk).  **Stand-verified end-to-end** (commits `21bfc37` + `8c3a108` + `6c78d9b`): on 8-host testnet, full rotation flow from node3 — mint OLD + NEW + migrate --publish-immediately → cert reaches all 7 peers within seconds → cross-host resolve from node5 follows chain and returns NEW; depth-3 chain (commit `6c78d9b`) verified all 4 query depths converge to terminal identity. |

**Acceptance:** node can choose PQ-only mode via config; default — hybrid; classical-only deprecated with a long migration window.  **✅ Met:** all three algo paths (`ed25519`, `hybrid`, `falcon512`) shipped end-to-end (local create/restore/show + DHT publish + cross-host resolve), MigrationCert path lets operators rotate identities in-place without losing @name / contacts, and operator-facing `docs/identity-hybrid-backup.md` documents recovery semantics for each path.  **Coverage matrix:**

```
test layer        | depth 1 chain | depth 2+ chain | falcon-only | hybrid
------------------|:-------------:|:--------------:|:-----------:|:------:
unit (resolver.rs)|       ✓       |  ✓ (cycle/cap) |     —       |   ✓
sim integration   |       ✓       |       —        |     —       |   ✓
cross-host stand  |       ✓       | ✓ (depth=3)    |     ✓       |   ✓

algo path         | local create | local restore | DHT publish | cross-host resolve
ed25519           |      ✓       |      ✓        |      ✓      |         ✓
hybrid (algo=3)   |      ✓       |      ✓        |      ✓      |         ✓
falcon512 (algo=2)|      ✓       |      ✓        |      ✓      |         ✓
chain-walk        |     n/a      |     n/a       |    ✓ cert   |   ✓ OLD→...→NEW
```

**Production-runtime safety properties confirmed by stand verification:**
- Quorum-fetch (`RESOLVE_QUORUM_THRESHOLD` matches required) defends against single-replica forgery.
- Cache-poison overwrite (Epic 491) preserved across the chain-walk extraction — local DHT shard is overwritten with the quorum-winning bytes after every per-hop fetch.
- Algo-appropriate signature verification per hop (Ed25519 for legacy ed25519 OLD masters; canonical hybrid composite (Ed25519 ∧ Falcon-512) for hybrid OLD masters; raw Falcon-512 for standalone-Falcon OLD masters).
- Non-downgrade enforced both at sign time (CLI / library refuse to mint a downgrade cert) AND at verify time (the resolver drops a cert that lowers `security_tier`).
- Cycle detection and `MAX_MIGRATION_CHAIN_DEPTH=4` cap guard against malicious chain construction.

**Carry-over:** none.  Closed cleanly with full feature parity to the original epic spec PLUS additional operator-facing CLI affordances not in the original scope (`--publish-immediately`, `node dht publish-replicated`, `--accept-no-recovery` for standalone-Falcon, `PreparedDecapsulator` performance API).

---

## Epic 489.10 / T1.4 — Mobile push-wake pipeline (mailbox + peer-sync + FCM/APNs) — closed (foundations + operator config + testnet-validated, 2026-05-07)

**Goal.** Give mobile clients in Doze-mode the ability to receive messages without a constant OVL1 connection.  Sender deposits an encrypted blob at K mailbox-relay's → relay fires an FCM/APNs wake → recipient wakes up, fetch's from any replica, acks.  Peer-sync on top of — eventual delivery if all puts missed.

**Closure summary (Phase 6.49, 12 commits `ac019e5…dfa3c75`, ~7300 LoC):** designed as **app-layer service** (mailbox is a built-in app, not a RelayChain opcode) to reuse existing DHT routing / sessions / retransmits / padding.  Smaller protocol surface, zero risk to hot frame-routing path.

| Phase | Commit | What ship'ed | LoC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P0** | `ac019e5` | Persistent X25519 sk + relay pubkey publication.  Fixes restart bug (envelope sealed under a randomly-regenerated pk = unrecoverable). `device_anonymity_x25519_sk.bin` (0o600), `NodeIdentityPayload.relay_x25519_pubkey: Option<[u8;32]>` backward-compat trailer, FFI `veil_get_relay_x25519_pubkey`. | 394 |
| **P1** | `63f70a5` | redb-backed `Mailbox` storage with a per-receiver quota (100 MiB) + global cap (10 GiB) + 7-day TTL + per-receiver rate-limit (60/min token bucket).  Atomic put-with-quota-update in a single redb transaction; FIFO eviction-on-global-hit; receiver-side quota returns hit without eviction (sender → peer-sync fallback). | 1167 |
| **P2** | `d02e404` | Wire opcodes 39-44 (`MailboxPut/PutOk/Fetch/FetchResp/Ack/AckOk`).  IPC `MailboxBackend` trait, `MailboxIpcBridge` in veilcore, SDK helpers, FFI two-call fetch API.  Cookie auth for fetch/ack (constant-time XOR-fold compare), no-auth for put. | 1553 |
| **P3a** | `6a576a6` | New `veil-push` crate: `PushDispatcher` async trait + `LogOnlyDispatcher` default + `PushToken { provider, token }` plaintext format.  Trigger flow: `MailboxIpcBridge::put` → mpsc → `push_dispatch_task` (unseal envelope with the relay X25519 sk → decode token → dispatcher).  Also fixed the P2 cookie-auth bug (was matching the wrong table). | 668 |
| **P3b** | `846a265` | `FcmDispatcher` (RS256 JWT → OAuth2 → POST to fcm.googleapis.com/v1) + `ApnsDispatcher` (.p8 PEM → ES256 JWT → HTTP/2 POST to api.push.apple.com/3/device/{hex}).  reqwest c rustls-tls only (no openssl).  HTTP status mapping: 401/403/404 → InvalidToken, 429 → RateLimited, 5xx → Transport.  17 wiremock-based unit tests. | 1355 |
| **P4** | `06a01b3` | Peer-sync foundations: new `veil-bloom` crate (BLAKE3 double-hashing Bloom + 9 tests, no-FN invariant verified, FP < 3% at target 1%); `Outbox` module in veil-mailbox (per-receiver pending list, redb-backed, 30-day TTL); IPC opcodes 45-50 (`OutboxPut/PutOk/FindMissing/FindMissingResp/Ack/AckOk`); SDK helpers + reader handlers. | 1738 |
| **P5a** | `2349af8` | `BuiltinAppHost` framework — closure-based service spawning via `AppEndpointRegistry`.  5 unit tests (spawn/shutdown, endpoint binding verified, multiple services, hung-service force-abort, Default).  Mailbox is first consumer; future candidates: echo/ping diagnostic, time-sync, peer-sync coordinator. | 378 |
| **P5b** | `d814cfd` | `MailboxAppService` — fire-and-forget `MailboxPut` over veil app-message channel (cross-node fanout path).  `MAILBOX_APP_ID = BLAKE3("veil.mailbox.v1")` (hardcoded + verifying test against drift), `MAILBOX_PUT_ENDPOINT_ID = 1`.  No reply at app-message layer — sender trusts; eventual delivery via multi-replica + peer-sync.  Push trigger hook unified — IPC bridge AND app service feed same mpsc. | 540 |
| **P5c** | `c2c2b9b` | `LookupRendezvousReplicas` IPC opcode + `RendezvousReplicaResolver` async trait + local-DHT `RendezvousResolverImpl` (decode → verify_signature → freshness check).  Returns `Vec<ReplicaWire>` for forward-compat — currently at most 1 entry (single-key publication; K=3 multi-key = future receiver-side change).  Recursive query intentionally not used (would require Arc<NodeRuntime> circular ref). | 594 |
| **P6** | `185ef68` | Operator config: `[mailbox.push]` section with `fcm_credentials_path` + `apns_p8_path/key_id/team_id/bundle_id/environment`.  `ProviderRouter` multiplexes by `PushToken.provider`.  `build_push_dispatcher` falls back gracefully to LogOnly if neither provider configured / both fail to load. | 340 |
| **P7a** | `dfa3c75` | 7 IPC integration tests against a real `IpcServer` + mock backends: mailbox put/fetch/ack round-trip, cookie verify, no-backend fallback, duplicate detection, outbox put/find/ack, lookup_rendezvous_replicas. | 339 |
| **P7b** | (no commit, validation only) | Testnet deploy: 12.5 MiB binary (vs 10.7 MiB pre-T1.4 — +17% from reqwest+jwt+redb deps).  All 8 hosts pulled identical hash (`b40e8e80b15c`), full mesh re-converged within 2 minutes (sess=7 across all), `hs_fail=0` and `dec_fail=0` post-restart on every host.  No regressions. | — |

**Acceptance — overall:** ✅ end-to-end push pipeline shipped with full operator deployment config AND testnet validation.  Apps can:

1. `client.lookup_rendezvous_replicas(receiver_id, K).await → Vec<RendezvousReplicaInfo>`
2. For each replica: `client.send(replica.relay_node_id, MAILBOX_APP_ID, MAILBOX_PUT_ENDPOINT_ID, MailboxPutPayload { ..., push_envelope: replica.envelope }.encode()).await` (fan-out)
3. Mailbox-relay's `MailboxAppService`: store + fire push trigger → unseal → FCM/APNs HTTP call (when operator configured)
4. Receiver wakes via push, fetches via existing `client.mailbox_fetch(cookie)`, acks via `client.mailbox_ack(cookie)`
5. Peer-sync fallback: receiver builds Bloom of received content_ids, sends to sender app-layer; sender app calls `outbox_find_missing(bloom)` → retransmits gaps

**Tests shipped (cumulative across T1.4):** 9 bloom + 26 mailbox + 17 push (pre-router) + 5 router + 10 builtin + 21 IPC integration + 8 outbox-proto + new mailbox/outbox/lookup proto = ~120 new tests, **0 regressions** across the workspace's pre-existing 600+ tests.

**Known limitations (documented in commit messages):**

- **Single-key DHT publication** (lookup returns at most 1 replica today).  Multi-key K=3 = receiver-side `tick_publish_rendezvous_ads` change (separate epic; lookup wire format already accommodates).
- **Local-DHT-only resolver** — recursive query needs Arc<NodeRuntime> or `dht_get_replicated` plumbing duplication; first-meet gap papered over by direct-delivery probe populating cache.
- **No graceful host shutdown integration** in `NodeRuntime::stop` — `BuiltinAppHost::Drop` aborts tasks (acceptable for daemon stop, storage on disk preserves state).
- **No hot-reload of FCM/APNs creds** — daemon restart required.
- **Mailbox-relay role not yet enabled on production testnet** — `mailbox.enabled` defaults off; activating requires ansible playbook update + redeploy (separate operator decision).

**Carry-over:** mailbox-as-app-endpoint pattern unlocks future built-in services with zero protocol-surface cost.  Receiver-side multi-key publication is a single Epic 489.10-followup for true K=3 fanout.

---

## Phase 6.32 / 6.33 incident notes (2026-05-01 + 2026-05-03)

Two transient AEAD-decrypt-failure incidents that drove the rekey-FSM
hardening work eventually shipped as Epic 117 hardening + Phase 6.33 grace
buffer + Phase 6.47-H19 grace ring widening + mutual-rekey-collision
tie-breaker (commit `d916e3b`).  Notes kept for the audit trail; root
cause closed.

**2026-05-01 ~23:25 UTC — Phase 6.32 incident.** Cluster of 8 testnet
nodes (uptime ~6h, sustained 18 MiB/s chat-node traffic ≈ 2.7 TB
cumulative per peer × 7 peers) developed transient AEAD decrypt failures
across most peers (b1=4, n4=4, b3=3, n3=3, n2=3, n1=1 in a 5-min window).
Sessions self-tore via `session.violation` and reconnected; cluster mesh
degraded 6→3-6 sessions per node before stabilising.  No peer hit
ban-action threshold (cumulative ban_actions stayed at 0).  Restart of
all 8 daemons cleared state immediately — 7/7 mesh, 0 decrypt failures
within ~30s.  Initial hypothesis: race in rekey-after-bytes path (Epic
117) at very-long-session edge — each session would have rekeyed ~21
times by 6h.  Confirmed and addressed via subsequent rekey FSM hardening
+ visibility (`session.rekey.{init,ack,complete}` events).

**2026-05-03 ~05:25 UTC — Phase 6.33 regression.** b2's session count
dropped 7→4.  n3 received AEAD-failed frame from peer_id=256bc205 (b2),
session.violation tore down the session.  Same pattern occurred ~1× on
5 other peers over ~8h cluster uptime — total 10 decrypt failures
cluster-wide (b2=3, n1=2, n3=2, b1=1, n4=1, n5=1).  REKEY_RX_GRACE_FRAMES
= 256 exhausted faster under chat-node load (~80-100 frames/sec/session
× 60-KiB frames @ ~5-7 MiB/s per peer): 256 frames covers ~3s in-flight,
but cross-VPS RTT can exceed that, so RekeyAck arrives AFTER buffer
fully consumed by post-rekey OLD-cipher frames.  Fix shipped via
Phase 6.47-H19 widening grace to a 3-deep VecDeque (covers back-to-back
rekeys) + Phase 6.33.b time-based grace (30s).  Mutual-rekey-collision
edge case (both peers' threshold fires within RTT) eventually identified
and fixed via `d916e3b` deterministic tie-breaker.

---

## Phase 6.49 audit zombie cleanup (2026-05-08)

External audit identified 10 stale `pub(crate)` accessors / placeholder
constants / commented-out scaffolding.  Closed batch-style into a single
"zombie cleanup" PR.

| Symbol | Location | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| `_path_type_guard` | `crates/veil-identity/src/sovereign_flow.rs:2052` | Deleted — `Path` import anchored by real signatures |
| `_REFERENCE_MAX_EK`, `_REFERENCE_ML_KEM_EK` | `crates/veil-proto/src/mlkem_cert.rs:241` | Deleted — actual cap is `MAX_PEER_MLKEM_EK_LEN` |
| Unused `BoundedDecoder` API | `crates/veil-proto/src/cursor.rs:153` | 7 unused methods deleted (`b90c36e`, 2026-05-09); 7 retained methods all consumed by `mlkem_cert::decode` |
| `consumed_tickets_len` | `veilcore/src/node/session/ticket.rs:255` | Gated `#[cfg(test)]` + commented "drop cfg + wire observability in the same commit when needed" |
| `AuditOutcome::ok_msg`, `AdminAuditLog::path` | `veilcore/src/node/admin_audit.rs:93` | `ok_msg` deleted; `path` made `#[cfg(test)]`-only |
| `clear_alt_uri`, `swap_attempts_in_window` | `veilcore/src/node/runtime/hot_standby.rs:102` | Both gated `#[cfg(test)]` + "drop cfg + wire-up in the same commit" pattern |
| Stale `allow(dead_code)` on `session_registry` | `veilcore/src/node/dispatcher/mod.rs:578` | Attribute removed; comment updated to point at real production callers in `delivery.rs` |
| `looks_like_tor_cells` no-op | `veilcore/src/node/session/handshake.rs:2092` | Deleted (function + the test assertion); true Tor-cell signature would need per-segment-size DPI, out of scope |
| Placeholder tuple in test | `crates/veil-anonymity/src/circuit.rs:297` | Deleted (along with a dead `_envelope` and `(_, hop1)`); test only walks live path now |
| `endpoint_handles` field with `allow(dead_code)` | `veilcore/src/node/builtin/host.rs:68` | Documented as RAII Drop guard (each `EndpointHandle::drop` deregisters); kept attribute with an explicit anti-refactor warning |

**Enforcement (Phase 6.50.b, 2026-05-10):** `scripts/check-allow-dead-code-anchors.sh`
greps every `#[allow(dead_code)]` attribute and validates each has either a
`///` doc comment OR a `#[cfg(...)]` attribute within 3 lines above.  12/12
surviving sites have anchors; script exits clean.  CI hook pending workflow
refresh; meanwhile available as a git pre-commit.

## Phase 6.50.b security & quality audit closeout (2026-05-11)

Two independent audits (internal + external) cross-referenced; real
findings deduplicated and addressed batch-style.  Items closed during
single sprint.

### Internal + first external pass

| Finding | Severity | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| FFI `Mutex.lock().expect()` panic crossing C ABI = UB | critical | `crates/veilclient-ffi/src/lib.rs:1037,1106` switched to `match … Err(p) => p.into_inner()` poison-recovery; line 1039 simplified to `blobs.len()` |
| Mailbox runtime config knobs locked off | high | `cfg/model.rs` MailboxConfig gained `require_capability_token: bool` + `quota_per_sender_bytes: u64`; wired through node.toml |
| Metrics `0.0.0.0:N` without an auth_token in the deployment defaults | medium | `docker/entrypoint.sh` + `ansible/templates/entrypoint.sh.j2` auto-generate random base64 `auth_token` for non-loopback binds |
| `allow-empty-seeds` was the docker-multiarch default | medium | `docker/build-multiarch.sh` flipped to `production-seeds,quic-session` default |
| SDK RPC bare `rx.await` can hang under daemon-stuck | medium | `veilclient/src/client.rs` gained `await_rpc_reply(rx, what)` helper with a 5s timeout; `node_identity` / `peers` / `mobile_status` / `join_bootstrap_uri` migrated |
| Env-var-mutating tests race under parallel cargo test | medium (test only) | `veilcore/src/cfg/locate.rs` + `crates/veil-identity/src/sovereign_flow.rs` tests serialised via process-wide `OnceLock<Mutex>` |
| Orphan TODO comments without a TASKS anchor | low | 2 TODO comments rewritten as descriptive design notes |
| Unused test-only accessors with a pub(crate) signature | low | 6 accessors gated `#[cfg(test)]` + explanatory comments |
| Clippy `await_holding_lock` warnings in test code | low | 2 tests documented with `#[allow(clippy::await_holding_lock)]` + rationale |
| FFI null-check on `veil_stream_read.buf` | low | false positive — already checked at lib.rs:719 |
| Master_qr.rs:370 "unnecessary unsafe" in test | low | false positive — `String::as_bytes_mut()` is `unsafe fn` |

### External-audit follow-up wave (2026-05-11)

Second-pass external audit; addressed batch-style.  Phases A* = supply-chain
/ process-gate; B* = SDK/FFI surface correctness; C* = code-hygiene polish.

| Finding | Severity | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| RUSTSEC-2026-0119 hickory-proto O(n²) CPU DoS | high | `872e39b` audit-A1: hickory-resolver 0.24 → 0.26.1; API migration in `crates/veil-bootstrap/src/dns.rs` |
| RUSTSEC-2026-0098/0099/0104 rustls-webpki | medium | audit-A1: `cargo update -p rustls-webpki` to 0.103.13; transitive bump |
| No CI gate for new RustSec advisories | medium | `5641d9f` audit-A2: `cargo audit --deny warnings` wired into `hygiene` job |
| IPC TCP/NamedPipe token TOCTOU (write-then-chmod) | high | `91bbe79` audit-A3: token write routed via `veil-local-transport::write_owner_only` (atomic `OpenOptions::mode(0o600)`); zeroizes hex copy on drop |
| Mailbox `capability_token` dropped at SDK boundary | medium | `b243f73` audit-B1: `RendezvousReplicaInfo.capability_token` field; new `veil_mailbox_put_with_capability` FFI entry-point |
| FFI status constants 6/7/8 missing | medium | `a9b5f3e` audit-B2: `VEIL_MAILBOX_PUT_{CAPABILITY_REQUIRED,CAPABILITY_INVALID,QUOTA_PER_SENDER}` constants added |
| Daemon spawn inherits hostile env (LD_PRELOAD etc.) | medium | `2b15508` audit-B3: `veil_util::scrub_command_env` does `env_clear()` + narrow allow-list; wired into `spawn_restart_child` + `spawn_background_node_process` |
| cbindgen `usize → uintptr_t` + duplicate `#include` lines | low | `26d2f80` audit-B4: `no_includes = true` + curated `sys_includes`; 4 `usize` retyped to `libc::size_t` |
| `chaos_sim::DualOutcome` dead-code anchor regression | low | `50c17bc` audit-C1: trailing `//` rationale moved to a `///` doc-block above the attribute |
| `tls-webpki-roots` build-flag/runtime-flag silent footgun | low | `d63431d` audit-C2: collapsed cfg-gate in `context.rs:508`; `use_system_roots = true` respected unconditionally |
| RUSTSEC-2025-0134 `rustls-pemfile` unmaintained | low | `9438294`: migrated `tls_material.rs` to `rustls-pki-types::pem::PemObject` trait; dropped zombie `rustls-pemfile` dep |

## Iterative-DHT route-discovery fallback (Phase 6.50.b, 2026-05-12)

20-node linear-chain empirical test exposed three layered limits on
multi-hop relay routing: `RouteRequest TTL=7`, `MAX_RELAY_HOPS=16`, and
a retry budget that drops messages if route_cache is not populated in
~3.5s.  Real-world reach in a linear chain was ~9 hops; N1↔N20 (19 hops)
impossible.

**Fix:** route-miss handler now triggers a relay-aware
`RecursiveQuery(FIND_NODE)` fallback after the legacy `RouteRequest` flood
exhausts retries.

| Slice | Commit | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `c4602a9` | Trait `IterativeDhtFallback` + optional slot in `MissHandlerCtx`; `None` default preserves legacy behaviour exactly.  `RoutingLogger` gained default `info()` method for fallback observability |
| 2 | `a23f906` | (Superseded) First impl using `find_node_iterative` + direct dial via `outbound_connector::spawn_outbound_peers`.  Architecturally wrong — direct-dial bypassed the relay-chain invariant |
| 3 | (validation) | 20-node linear-chain run revealed `NetworkPeerQuerier::find_node` requires direct sessions — walker stuck at 1 hop |
| 3.5 | `a7f16b7` + `0bf5c36` | Redesigned `DhtRouteFallback` to use the relay-aware `RecursiveQuery(FIND_NODE)` envelope.  Dispatcher's `handle_recursive_response` already auto-populates `route_cache` from FIND_NODE replies; fallback just fires the query.  No `outbound_connector` involvement — relay chain preserved |

**Empirical result on 20-node linear chain:** route cache on N1 covers
chain-distances {1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11} (up from 1-2 pre-fix —
5× reach extension via relay).  Multiple
`route.dht_fallback.resolved cache_hit=true` events visible across all
chain positions.

**Limit beyond which this still doesn't help:** full N1↔N20 (19 hops)
remains hard because `RecursiveQuery` itself has TTL=40 (plenty) but
the **return path latency** + dispatcher CPU pressure under 20-daemon load
means responses sometimes don't arrive within 10s fallback timeout.
Wider mesh (random graph, diameter ~log N) gets full reach.

---

## Phase 5 — Per-listener visibility + ephemeral rotation — closed (2026-05-20)

Multi-session work that shipped per-listener visibility levels
(Public / Trusted / Hidden / Ephemeral), invite-bundle bearer-credential
format with QR display, and a full rotation lifecycle for anti-port-
clustering.  Production deployment all-8-testnet-nodes verified
end-to-end (every active peer logged the migration-notify on first
rotation cycle).

### Sub-phases

| Phase | Commit | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 5a | (predecessor) | `EphemeralPortBinder::bind_random_port` — random-port helper with EADDRINUSE retry in veil-transport |
| 5b | `bdbf256` | `TransportMigrationNotify` wire frame (SessionMsg=21) + sign/verify helpers with replay-window enforcement |
| 5c | `e118057` | `veil-invite` crate — InviteBundleV1 in CBOR with an Ed25519 sig, base32 encoding, QR rendering (16 unit tests) |
| 5d | `2391c5a` | veil-cli invite create / accept / decode subcommands with an end-to-end config-mutation flow |
| 5d follow-up | `136a4e3` | invite create→accept E2E integration tests (4 scenarios incl. idempotency + visibility-gate rejection) |
| 5e | `c375b3a` | TransportMigrationNotify dispatcher arm in the session-runner — verify + cache-update (6 wire-level tests) |
| 5f.1 | `dc94aa6` | rotation primitive in veil-transport: parse_duration_spec, RotationSpec, run_rotation_loop with BindFn/BroadcastFn traits (15 tests) |
| 5f.2 | `536207a` | `spawn_ephemeral_rotator` production helper with a real session-tx broadcaster (2 wire-level tests) |
| 5f.3 | `0bff816` | accept-loop swap-channel + spawn_listeners integration; ListenConfigEntry gained `ephemeral` field |
| 5f.3 hotfix | `07a8d6e` | stash rotator shutdown senders on NodeRuntime; bug was `drop(handles.shutdown)` killing rotator on startup |
| 5f.3c | `71cdb23` + `08b3f57` | operator docs (RU config-reference [[listen]] + [listen.ephemeral]); canary + revert playbooks; grep-veil-log fix |

### Production deployment artefacts (testnet, 2026-05-20)

* Binary built with `--features veil-bootstrap/allow-empty-seeds`,
  deployed to all 8 hosts (b1/b2/b3 + node1..5) via
  `ansible/deploy-binary-only.yml`.
* Initial canary on node1 (10-min rotation, 5-min grace, range
  51000-51999) validated full lifecycle: bind → broadcast → grace →
  swap.  Port progression: 5556 → 51237 → 51248.
* Hotfix discovered live from the canary (rotator was exiting on startup
  due to a shutdown-sender drop); fixed within one session.
* Final fleet config: `rotation=6h grace=1h range=50000..=60000`
  on all 8 hosts.  `active_sessions=7` stable on every host during
  the rolling restart, no decrypt_failures or session_violations.
* Phase 5e dispatcher arm verified live: node1's first rotation
  broadcast was logged on all 4 active-session peers (node2/3/4/5)
  as `session.migration.notify.applied peer_id=93526afb
  new_transport=obfs4-tcp://0.0.0.0:51248 new_expiry_unix=...`.
  Bootstraps (b1/b2/b3) don't maintain persistent sessions with
  node1 so did not receive the notify — expected.

### Carry-overs (open, lower priority)

* **EN `docs/en/config-reference.md` is a stub** — RU has the
  full `[[listen]]` + `[listen.ephemeral]` schema.  Translate when
  EN docs are picked up as a general initiative.
* **Soak observation** — first production rotation cycle on each
  the node fires ~6h after its restart (~21:00 UTC 2026-05-20).
  Operator can verify the second cycle visually if desired; no
  automation gates this.

### Acceptance bar

* `cargo check --workspace --all-features` clean throughout.
* All slice unit/integration tests green (~50 new tests across
  veil-invite + veil-transport + veilcore).
* Testnet E2E: rotation lifecycle complete without session disruption;
  Phase 5e dispatcher arm picks up the broadcast and updates DHT
  transport-cache on all peers that share an active session.

### Successor

Next epic: **PoW-Gated Rendezvous** (design-doc
[`docs/en/PLAN_POW_GATED_RENDEZVOUS.md`](docs/en/PLAN_POW_GATED_RENDEZVOUS.md))
— extends the visibility ladder with a **Stealth** level where
listeners are bound on-demand only after a PoW-gated request, and
auto-shut after one accepted session or short TTL.

## ECH (Encrypted Client Hello) opt-in — archived design landing-pad (2026-05-21)

**Status:** ⏸ **archived — wait for the activation trigger**.

Anti-censorship strategy ECH would close DPI method #14 (FakeSNI
heuristic) without CDN-fronting.  Design landing-pad shipped 2026-05-20
as [`docs/en/PLAN_ECH_OPT_IN.md`](docs/en/PLAN_ECH_OPT_IN.md);
archived 2026-05-21 since activation requires a fundamental swap
of the rustls crypto provider (`ring` → `aws_lc_rs`, ~5 MB binary
size increase + new C build dependency) and Caddy 2.10+ as a server-
side fronter (rustls 0.23 doesn't expose server-side ECH via the
high-level API).

**Why archived now**: ECH-marked ClientHello is **itself a DPI red
flag** in 2026.  Adoption is ~30% globally; in RU/CN/IR jurisdictions
ECH-marked traffic is actively classified separately from ordinary
HTTPS, with TSPU shipping rules that block ECH ClientHellos outright.
Enabling ECH preemptively could **worsen** censorship resistance
for current deployments.

**Operator-side alternative — CDN fronting** (Cloudflare Workers /
Fastly Compute@Edge) is the recommended #14 closure today, documented
in [`docs/en/DEPLOYMENT_HARDENING.md`](docs/en/DEPLOYMENT_HARDENING.md).
Closes #14 without code-side changes + without the aws_lc_rs migration risk.
Cost: $5-50/mo per CDN.

**Re-open triggers** (any of):
1. CDN-fronting unavailable in target jurisdiction (Cloudflare / Fastly
   blocked, multi-CDN failover infeasible).
2. ECH adoption rate ≥ 50% (track Cloudflare's published stats; ECH
   ClientHello becomes statistically ordinary rather than minority signal).
3. Specific deployment request from a high-sensitivity threat model
   with a dedicated security review explicitly requesting ECH.

When activation triggered:
* Prerequisite slices documented in `PLAN_ECH_OPT_IN.md` (crypto-provider
  swap + Caddy server-side + DNS HTTPS RR record + rustls wire-up).
* Estimated scope: ~650 LoC, 3 sessions.
* Plus test-matrix re-validation for the crypto-provider change.

---


## Config signing (killed 2026-05-21)

**What was**: proposed item to add Ed25519 signature to `config.toml`
with verify-on-load — operator would sign their config with a master key,
daemon would refuse to load tampered configs.

**Why archived (not needed)**: config file lives on the same machine
as the daemon under the same uid; threat model where an attacker can rewrite
config.toml but cannot dump the verify key from disk (or from the
running daemon's memory) is implausible.  File-system-level
protections (POSIX 0600 owner-only + fs-immutable flag for
paranoid setups) cover the realistic threat without adding a new key-
management surface to the operator workflow.

**Operator-side alternative** (recommended): chmod 0600 config.toml +
optionally `chattr +i` (Linux) / `chflags schg` (macOS / BSD) for a
single immutable file.  Documented in [`docs/en/DEPLOYMENT_HARDENING.md`].

**No re-open triggers** — feature deemed unnecessary; revisit only
if a specific threat-model emerges that can't be covered by FS
permissions (e.g., shared-tenant deployments where the daemon-uid != operator-uid).

---

## HMAC/session keys mlock + zeroize-on-drop (killed 2026-05-21)

**What was**: proposed cross-platform work (Linux mlock, Windows
VirtualLock, macOS mlock) k pin AEAD / signing / KDF key material in
RAM so the OS does not swap'out pages to disk, plus systematic
`ZeroizeOnDrop` derive applied to all key-bearing structs.

**Why archived (not needed)**: the threat that mlock specifically
addresses — secrets persisted in the swap file → attacker reads swap post-
mortem — doesn't apply to current and projected veil deployments:

* **VPS / server boxes** under anti-censorship: typically `swapoff -a`
  or simply no swap configured (better latency, deterministic perf)
* **Android** (Epic 489 target): zram-only since Android 5+ (compressed
  in-memory swap), no disk swap by default
* **iOS**: doesn't swap at all
* **Embedded routers** / relay boxes: typically no swap (flash wear)

`mlock` also doesn't protect against the threats that actually matter:

* **Core dumps** (`SIGSEGV` / panic): kernel writes ALL process memory
  including mlocked pages.  Protection requires `prctl(PR_SET_DUMPABLE, 0)`
  + `RLIMIT_CORE = 0`, not mlock.
* **Hibernation**: dumps entire RAM regardless of mlock.  Desktop/laptop
  scenario only — server tier not relevant.
* **Cold-boot / forensic RAM acquisition**: requires physical access +
  powered machine; mlock doesn't help.

`Zeroize` on drop is also marginal in safe Rust: borrow checker prevents
use-after-free; Rust std types initialize on alloc (`Vec::new()` doesn't
read uninit heap, `String::new()` either); residual risk only in
`unsafe` / FFI the boundary.

**Operator-side alternatives** (documented in `DEPLOYMENT_HARDENING.md`):

1. `prctl(PR_SET_DUMPABLE, 0)` — daemon refuses core dumps
   (Linux).  One syscall in `main()`.  Could ship code-side if
   a sensible default; currently leave to the operator via `sysctl
   kernel.core_pattern = |/bin/false`.
2. `setrlimit(RLIMIT_CORE, 0)` — same effect, cross-platform.
3. **Disable swap** on the host (`swapoff -a`; remove from `/etc/fstab`).
4. **Single-uid threat model**: don't run daemon in shared-tenant
   environment (different uid attacker can ptrace and read memory
   regardless of mlock).

**No re-open triggers** — feature deemed unnecessary under realistic
threat model.  Revisit only if:
* Specific deployment requires running under shared-tenant with uid
  separation
* Operator explicitly requires defense-in-depth for cold-storage
  attack scenarios despite swap-off

---

## ML-KEM cert_publish_counter (parked 2026-05-25)

**What was**: proposed item from cross-audit Sprint 2.3 backlog — add
a monotonic `publish_counter` field to `MlkemCert` (the DHT-published
ML-KEM-768 cert) so peers that have seen a newer counter reject any
older cert under the same `(node_id, instance_id)`. Belt-and-suspenders
defence-in-depth against replay of a previously-valid cert during the
overlap window between rotation and the natural `valid_until_unix` expiry.

**Why parked (not killed)**: current defences already bound the attack
window naturally:
* DHT key `(node_id, instance_id)` + signature by identity key means
  attacker cannot forge or shadow a cert at a different slot
* `valid_until_unix` bounds how long an old (potentially-compromised)
  cert is replayable — typically hours, not days
* Counter would only buy faster "instant invalidate" semantics for
  incident response (suspected key compromise) instead of waiting for
  natural expiry

**Cost**: wire-bump `MlkemCert` v1→v2, signed-envelope schema change,
migration window (nodes must read both versions during rollout), per-
peer state to remember last-seen counter per `(node_id, instance_id)`.

**Cheaper alternative (recommended path)**: tighten the default cert
lifetime via `valid_until_unix` (e.g., 4-6 hours instead of 24h). Same
benefit (small attack window) without wire-changes. Operator-driven
rotation `instance_id` remains the "instant invalidate" escape hatch
if truly needed.

**Re-open triggers**: revisit only if a deployment scenario emerges
that requires "instant cert revocation as a first-class primitive"
without the cost of rotating `instance_id` (which terminates active
sessions). Current threat model doesn't justify the wire-bump cost.

---

## Anycast `ttl_secs` on wire (parked 2026-05-25)

**What was**: proposed item from cross-audit Sprint 2.3 backlog — Part
B of anycast TTL work (Part A landed in Phase N: in-memory per-record
TTL filtering on resolve). Part B would extend the signed
`AnycastRecord` envelope to carry `ttl_secs` as a publisher-signed
field, so when DHT-replicates the record across the network, all
receiving nodes respect the publisher's chosen TTL instead of falling back
to their local default.

**Why parked (not killed)**: there's a genuine censorship-resistance
use case here — operator running an obfs4-bridge with fast IP rotation
wants `ttl_secs = 30s` so stale endpoints expire across the network
quickly. Without Part B, operator's TTL choice is honored only on the
local node; replicated copies on remote nodes use defaults.

But the same benefit is achievable cheaper by tightening **default**
anycast TTL globally without wire-changes — a good-enough fast-failover
for most operators.

**Cost**: wire-bump `AnycastRecord` v2→v3, signed-envelope schema
change including `ttl_secs` in the signed message, migration window
(nodes read both v2+v3), verify-side range validation
(`ttl_secs ∈ [MIN, MAX]` so a malicious publisher cannot set absurd
values). ~3-5 days of work + cross-host coexistence period.

**Cheaper alternative (recommended path)**: lower the global default
TTL to favour fast-failover (e.g., 5 min → 60-90 s). Catches the
common "bridge IP rotated" case without wire-changes. Operators needing
long-lived stable-service entries (low republish noise) lose tuning
control — accept tradeoff.

**Re-open triggers**: revisit if (a) operators explicitly request
"per-service TTL control as a first-class primitive" in bridge-
rotation playbooks, or (b) default-TTL tuning proves insufficient
for high-churn services (autoscaling pools, mobile-NAT bridges).

---
